name: 麺屋 極鶏 一乗寺本店 (Menya Gokkei Ichijoji Honten)

noodles.notes: Firm, low-hydration medium-gauge straight noodles chosen to stand up to the spoon-standing, potage-thick broth; served with a deliberate chew so they don't drown in the soup.

ramen.signature_bowl: 鶏だく (Tori-daku) — the original ultra-concentrated chicken paitan, a broth so thick a spoon stands upright in it.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork chashu, sliced thin and laid over the gravy-like broth — a soft, simmered slice rather than aburi/char-grilled.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a shoyu-based tare make this celiac-unsafe; no rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The entire concept is a whole-chicken broth boiled to a gravy, finished with chicken fat and topped with pork chashu — no plant-based bowl exists.

ramen.pantheon: In the ramen-battleground of Ichijoji's Ramen Highway, Gokkei pioneered the 'doro-kei' (mud-style) chicken paitan: whole chickens boiled down until the soup is less a liquid than an edible potage that holds a spoon upright. It is the soul of Kyoto's modern tori-paitan movement — extreme body and chicken sweetness with almost no fishiness, offered white (tori-daku), red with chili (aka-daku), or black with aromatic oil (kuro-daku).

name: セアブラノ神 壬生本店 (Seaburano Kami Mibu Honten)

noodles.notes: Custom-made for the shop by Kyoto's Menya Teigaku. Famously thick — roughly udon-width — with a springy, gummy-chewy bite that grabs the back-fat and niboshi soup. A medium-thick option is also offered, the signature being the thick noodle.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tokusei se-abura niboshi soba (特製背脂煮干しそば) — the deluxe back-fat dried-sardine soba topped with the full set of chashu, ajitama and menma
ramen.chashu: Two kinds in the deluxe bowl: thin rare-style pork and a braised pork whose fat takes on a crisp, almost crackling edge — four slices total, half of each.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; pork back-fat and niboshi run through the whole bowl. No gluten-free option documented.

vegan.note: Built on a chicken-and-pork-bone clear soup blended with a dried-sardine (niboshi) dashi, finished with pork back-fat and chashu. No plant-based bowl documented.

ramen.pantheon: This bowl carries the Niigata Tsubame-Sanjo lineage — a clear chicken-and-pork soup married to a punchy cold-brewed sardine dashi, then blanketed in glistening clouds of melted pork back-fat — re-tuned to Kyoto tastes. You taste the soul in the contrast: bracing niboshi umami and round shoyu cut through fat so rich the udon-thick noodles need every bit of their springy chew to stand up to it.

name: 新福菜館 本店 (Shinpuku Saikan Honten)

noodles.notes: Medium-thick noodles with a smooth, springy (tsuru-mochi) bite that soaks up and carries the dark, savory soup.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (their one signature bowl); order it Tokudai with extra thin-sliced chashu and a side of their famous black yakimeshi.
ramen.chashu: Generous, thinly sliced braised pork chashu, firmly seasoned and tender with a well-judged balance of lean and fat — soft enough to melt and good enough to eat on its own with a drink.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui Chinese-style noodles plus a wheat-based dark soy sauce tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented, so this bowl is not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soup is built on chicken bones and pork and finished with a meat-and-soy tare, with pork chashu on top; no plant-based broth bowl exists here.

ramen.pantheon: Open since 1938 near Kyoto Station, this is widely held as the forebear of Kyoto's jet-black shoyu ramen: a soup so dark it looks fierce yet tastes mellow and clean, where chicken-and-pork stock and a Kyoto-brewed dark soy tare melt into one deep, gently sweet umami. You taste old-Kyoto town-Chinese lineage in every sip — bold color, surprisingly light body, almost no surface oil.

name: 吟醸らーめん 久保田 (Ginjo Ramen Kubota)

noodles.notes: Substantial, springy-chewy wheat noodles often compared to Sanuki udon for their thickness and bite; curly/wavy strands that cling to the thick dipping sauce on the tsukemen. Served default at a soft al dente.

ramen.signature_bowl: Miso tsukemen — the house's famous order: tightly-kneaded thick noodles with a rich, grainy miso-forward dipping sauce built on the seafood-and-poultry broth, studded with finely minced meat and sakura shrimp; add an ajitama (soft-boiled egg) for a little more. The clear "ginjō shoyu rāmen" is the elegant alternative.
ramen.chashu: A single thin slice of braised pork — tender with a gentle bite, fresh porcine flavor deepened by a savory braise; lean rather than fatty belly.
notable_toppings: minced meat and sakura shrimp (in the miso tsukemen sauce)

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus blended-soy-sauce and miso tare make every bowl wheat-laden; no rice- or konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on whole chicken and chicken bones with bonito, dried squid and dried shrimp, and bowls carry pork chashu and minced meat/sakura shrimp; no plant-only broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: This is a refined Kyoto take where the soul lives in a long-simmered whole-chicken-and-bonito-kombu broth pulled clear and brown, finished with blended soy — you taste the dashi lineage of Kansai in the soup rather than the heavy pork-back-fat shoyu Kyoto is usually known for. Its renown, though, rests on a rarer-in-Kyoto miso tsukemen whose grainy, faintly spicy dipping sauce wraps thick udon-like noodles.

name: 中華そば 高安 (Chuka Soba Takayasu)

noodles.notes: Custom-ordered straight thin noodles with a kansui (alkaline-water) ratio tuned to grip and carry the milky broth; clean slurp.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka Soba — the milky pork-and-chicken white broth with straight thin noodles, sliced pork chashu, Kujo negi and menma. The limited Suji Ramen (braised tendon) is the house's top recommendation when available.
ramen.chashu: Thinly sliced braised pork that stays moist and gives up its umami into the soup; an extra-chashu bowl (Chashu-men) is offered.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a shoyu-leaning tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Core broth is pork-bone and chicken simmered together, finished with animal tare and pork chashu; no plant-based broth is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A pillar of Kyoto's Ichijoji ramen row, this bowl pours a 24-hour pork-and-chicken broth that arrives milky white yet stays clean and unctuous rather than heavy — the smooth, naturally sweet, gameless paitan that defines the city's tori-tonkotsu lineage. Straight thin noodles and a fistful of crisp Kujo negi let that creamy soup, not seasoning fireworks, carry the soul of the bowl.

name: ラーメン荘 夢を語れ (Ramen-sou Yume wo Katare)

noodles.notes: Extra-thick flat hand-massaged (temomi) self-made noodles with good wheat flavor; default 300g, with 200g small and 400g large options. Holds firm even under the mountain of toppings.

ramen.signature_bowl: Ramen (default 300g) with the full call: yasai (vegetables), ninniku (garlic), abura (back fat), karame (extra tare). A sweet, fat-slicked non-emulsified pork-and-soy bowl buried under a mountain of bean sprouts and cabbage. A shiru-nashi (soupless) version is also offered.
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut braised pork dominated by tender red lean meat (with a slice of fat), mild in smell, cut slightly smaller than other Jiro shops but generous and rough-hewn rather than refined.

gf.note: Jiro-style wheat noodles with kansui plus a soy-sauce (wheat) tare and pork-bone broth. No gluten-free option exists; not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is pork bone with soy tare, topped with pork back fat and red-lean chashu. No plant-based broth or bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: The flagship of Kansai's Jiro lineage and the first Ramen-Jiro-inspired shop in the region, this bowl carries the unmistakable Jiro soul: a sweet, non-emulsified pork-bone-and-soy soup slicked with melted back fat, hidden beneath a towering mound of bean sprouts and cabbage you eat your way down to. Yet the broth here is a touch more harmonious and milder than its wilder siblings, letting the thick hand-massaged noodles and that pork-fat sweetness sing rather than overwhelm.

name: 山﨑麺二郎 (Yamazaki Menjiro)

noodles.notes: House-made noodles are matched to the bowl: a thin, smooth, straight noodle for the shoyu, and a flatter, thicker, higher-hydration chewy noodle for the shio. Tsukemen uses a chewier thick cut.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shio ramen (salt) — the bowl regulars rave about; the shoyu ramen is the equally celebrated alternate.
ramen.chashu: Large, generously marbled pork belly slices, low-temperature/braised so they stay tender and rich.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui house noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of every bowl is a chicken-and-dried-seafood (bonito/kelp/dried fish) dashi, finished with pork chashu. No plant-only broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A refined, crystal-clear chintan that reads as modern Kyoto tanrei rather than the city's old se-abura tradition: a gentle chicken stock laced with a deep multi-fish-and-kombu dashi and rounded by a quiet soy or salt seasoning. You taste the lineage in a soup that is delicate yet startlingly savory, carried by house-cut noodles tuned to each tare.

name: 麺屋 猪一 (Menya Inoichi)

noodles.notes: House-made noodles milled from stone-ground whole wheat flour; thin but notably firm, cut to stand up cleanly against the delicate clear dashi without muddying it.

ramen.signature_bowl: Dashi soba (chuka soba) — choose the black (kuro shoyu) for a deeper, rounder seasoning or the white (shiro shoyu/shio-leaning) for the cleanest expression of the dashi; the black is the house signature. A separate tsukemen version is also a draw.
ramen.chashu: Lean, gently cooked pork presented thin and restrained so it does not fight the seafood broth; preparation details are not firmly documented.

gf.note: Noodles are wheat-based (stone-ground whole wheat) and the tare is soy-sauce based, so the bowl is not celiac-safe. No documented rice/konjac noodle or gluten-free tare option.

vegan.note: The defining broth is 100% seafood dashi (Rausu kombu plus multiple dried fish/katsuobushi), so even the no-animal-fat base is not plant-only. No documented kombu/shiitake vegan bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto original built entirely on the soul of Japanese dashi: a crystal-clear seafood broth coaxed slowly from Rausu kombu and a chorus of dried fish, deliberately free of pork or chicken fat. You taste the lineage of the washoku dashi pot rather than any regional ramen tradition, with white and black soy-sauce versions framing that katsuobushi-driven clarity from delicate to deep.

name: 中華そば ほそかわ 花屋町店 (Chuka Soba Hosokawa (Hanayacho))

noodles.notes: Medium-thin straight noodles with a firm bite that grab the back-fat shoyu broth; firmness can be specified at ordering and a large bowl doubles the portion.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (ramen) — chicken-bone shoyu broth blanketed with fine pork back-fat, finished with a dusting of ichimi chili
ramen.chashu: Roasted pork loin, sliced thin (three slices); lean and lightly firm rather than melting fatty belly

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles in a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken bones and pork, with shoyu tare and a generous layer of pork back-fat; no plant-based bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: This is textbook Kyoto se-abura shoyu — a dark, soy-tinged chicken-and-pork broth blanketed with a snowfall of fine pork back-fat, the lineage that runs back through Ginkaku-ji Masutani where the owner trained. The looks promise heaviness, but a clean chicken-bone clarity beneath the fat lets you drink it to the bottom, with a pinch of ichimi chili for a Kyoto bite.

name: 和醸良麺 すがり (Wajo Ryomen Sugari)

noodles.notes: House-made whole-wheat (zenryufun) thick flat noodles, mochi-mochi chewy and firm, built to grab the heavy dipping broth. A yuzu-infused version of the noodles can be chosen as an aromatic alternative.

ramen.signature_bowl: Motsu tsukemen (もつつけ麺) — thick whole-wheat dipping noodles with a rich, creamy chicken-and-pork-bone broth deepened with seafood dashi and a touch of sweet-sour, served warm in a small Staub pot, crowned with charred beef offal.
ramen.chashu: No conventional chashu — the heart is motsu: prized domestic-beef offal (hoso/hosomotsu, of which only a few kilos come from a whole animal), torched/seared so it is juicy and smoky and bursts in the mouth.

gf.note: Wheat-based whole-grain noodles and a shoyu-style seasoning over a pork/chicken/seafood broth — not celiac-safe, and no rice or konjac noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork bone, chicken and fish (bonito) dashi, and the signature topping is beef offal; no plant-based broth or noodle option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto original rather than a regional classic: a creamy chicken-pork-bone tonkotsu-gyokai dipping broth (the now-ubiquitous "W-soup" lineage of Tokyo-style tsukemen) given a distinctly Kansai twist by crowning it with charred domestic-beef offal. You taste the gyokai-tonkotsu soul in the soup, but the smoky motsu is what makes this bowl its own.

name: 麺や高倉二条 (Menya Takakura Nijo)

noodles.notes: House-made noodles produced in an upstairs noodle room. Whole-grain (zenryufun) flour gives a soba-like, nutty, slightly grainy character with a smooth, silky bite; on the slimmer side for a tonkotsu-gyokai tsukemen. A citrus yuzu noodle option is also offered.

ramen.signature_bowl: Noko tonkotsu-gyokai tsukemen (thick pork-bone & seafood dipping noodles) — best ordered with the motsu (braised offal) or wagyu topping
ramen.chashu: Char-grilled (aburi) pork chashu with a fragrant seared edge; cubed/diced chashu is also worked into the dipping broth. Signature upgrades swap in tender braised motsu (offal) or thinly shredded premium wagyu beef.

gf.note: Whole-grain wheat noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare make this unsafe for celiacs; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: Core broth is pork-bone and fish (katsuo/seafood) dashi with animal proteins and char-grilled pork; no documented plant-based broth bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A standard-bearer of the modern thick tonkotsu-gyokai tsukemen movement, this bowl pairs a viscous pork-bone soup with a deep, almost gritty pounding of bonito and seafood — the seafood-and-pork soul you taste in every dip. Hidden in a tiny Kyoto machiya, it leans into a local twist of recent years: tender braised offal alongside the noodles.

name: 京都鶏白湯そば 純 (Kyoto Tori-Paitan Soba Jun)

noodles.notes: Custom medium-thick flat-cut (中太平打ち) noodles with a firm bite, chosen to grip and carry the thick foam-emulsified broth. Mazesoba uses a chewier, larger (1.5x) portion.

ramen.signature_bowl: 鶏白湯そば (Tori Paitan Soba) — the foam-style creamy chicken-bone bowl. Squeeze the lemon mid-bowl to lift it, and finish with the "dive rice" in the leftover soup.
ramen.chashu: The heart is a slab of slow-braised pork belly, lightly aburi (seared) so the edge chars and the fat melts — it collapses into tender, sweet pieces against the rich broth. A rare-seared Tajima chicken-breast chashu is also offered as an alternative.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus shoyu/miso tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken bones with added pork bone, and bowls carry pork/chicken chashu, egg and clams. No plant-based broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto take on the awa-kei (foam-style) tori-paitan movement: Kagoshima chicken bones with a touch of Miyazaki/Oita pork are force-emulsified in a blender until the soup turns into a creamy, cappuccino-like froth, so you taste pure poultry koku rather than any one regional lineage. It is a bowl built around texture and roundness — rich and mellow, with a Hokkaido-onion shio or a winter Sapporo-miso tare to season the foam.

name: 重厚軍団 (Juko Gundan)

noodles.notes: Homemade (jikasei) straight thick noodles for the tsukemen, with a chewy, wheat-forward bite that clings to the heavy dipping broth; the mazesoba uses extra-thick crinkled (chijire) noodles. Standard tsukemen portion is a generous 400g.

ramen.signature_bowl: Juko Tsukemen — thick homemade straight noodles served alongside a dense, viscous gyokai-tonkotsu dipping broth (the alternative house favorite is the J-Mazesoba topped with a fist-sized chunk of pork).
ramen.chashu: Braised pork belly (buta-bara) chashu; the mazesoba instead crowns the bowl with a fist-sized chunk of meat.

gf.note: Tsukemen and mazesoba both center on wheat-and-kansui noodles, and the broth/tare lean on pork bone, seafood dashi and a soy-based sauce — wheat throughout. No rice- or konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented; not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of every bowl is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) and fish-dashi broth finished with pork belly chashu and pork toppings. No plant-based broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A heavyweight of Ichijoji's fabled ramen street, this counter-only specialist lives up to its name (juko = 'weighty/substantial'): the signature is not a soup bowl but a tsukemen whose dipping broth is a glossy, gravy-thick fusion of pork bone and seafood dashi. You taste the modern gyokai-tonkotsu tsukemen lineage — born of Tokyo's dipping-noodle boom — rendered here at maximum density and clung to by chewy house-made noodles.

name: 天天有 本店 (Tentenyu Honten)

noodles.notes: Thin-to-medium-thin straight noodles chosen to carry the thick, faintly sweet chicken broth, which clings to the strands. Firmness is adjustable to taste, and a flat-noodle (hiramen) option appears in limited quantities.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chicken paitan ramen (chashu-men for the full version) — the lone broth at the original shop, a thick, mild, faintly sweet tori-paitan over thin straight noodles, finished with chashu, green onion and egg.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork thigh (momo) — leaner and cleanly seasoned in an old-school style, sliced tender; the chashu-men piles on thick, substantial slices.
notable_toppings: optional kotteri finish of back-fat + ichimi togarashi
beni-shoga (pickled red ginger) on the side

gf.note: Standard wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles; chicken-and-vegetable broth with no documented rice/konjac-noodle or certified GF option. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is long-simmered chicken (plus chicken-thigh chashu); no documented plant-only bowl. Not vegan.

ramen.pantheon: A 1971 pioneer of Ichijoji's ramen row, this bowl predates the tori-paitan boom: chicken and vegetables simmered for some 18 hours into a thick, ivory broth whose hallmark is an unusual, gentle sweetness rather than heavy fat. You taste that mellow, almost soothing chicken depth in every sip — a distinctly Kyoto reading of tori-paitan that feels nourishing rather than punishing.

name: 珍遊 一乗寺本店 (Chinyu Ichijoji Honten)

noodles.notes: Medium-thin, low-hydration straight noodles milled from Hokkaido wheat, chosen to grab and carry the back-fat-laced broth; firmness can be ordered to taste.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (中華そば) — the chicken-bone white broth bowl finished with shoyu tare and a generous scatter of fresh pork back-fat; the back-fat amount, scallion, and noodle firmness can all be dialed in.
ramen.chashu: Sliced braised pork chashu.

gf.note: Wheat-based ramen noodles and a soy-sauce (wheat) tare make this bowl unsafe for celiacs; no rice- or konjac-noodle alternative is documented.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a long-simmered chicken-bone broth finished with pork back-fat and pork chashu — animal products run through every layer, and no plant-based broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A founding bowl of Kyoto ramen, simmered since 1950 on the city's Ichijoji ramen street: cloudy chicken-bone broth lacquered with shoyu and a snowfall of fresh pork back-fat, so it looks heavy yet finishes clean — the kotteri-but-light se-abura shoyu lineage that defines the Kyoto north-side style. You taste that old-school chicken-and-lard soul in every sip, the back-fat melting into the soy backbone.

name: 麺屋 さん田 (Menya Sanda)

noodles.notes: House-made noodles milled from domestic wheat with a noticeable whole-wheat character; medium-thick and straight with a smooth, slurpable surface and firm, springy bite that stands up to the heavy chicken broth. The tsukemen version is iced for extra snap. Strong wheat aroma is part of the appeal.

ramen.signature_bowl: Rich chicken paitan tsukemen (濃厚鶏白湯つけ麺) — thick, concentrated chicken dipping broth with house-made wheat noodles served plain so the soup leads.
ramen.chashu: Steamed chicken breast (mune) chashu — pale, fine-grained and tender rather than braised pork; served as bite-sized slices.

gf.note: Noodles are house-made from wheat (with whole-wheat) and kansui, and the seasoning is not gluten-free. No rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The entire menu is built on a chicken-only paitan broth with chicken chashu and chicken-fat aroma oil; the mazesoba carries minced meat as well. No plant-based broth or bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto tori-paitan specialist whose soul is pure chicken: the dipping broth is chicken and water simmered down until it turns velvet-thick, yet it finishes clean rather than cloying, with a slick of golden chicken fat and a whisper of scorched garlic carrying the aroma. It sits in the contemporary nou-kou (ultra-rich) chicken-paitan lineage rather than any old gotochi school, letting the bird's umami — not pork bone or heavy oil — do all the talking.

name: らーめんや亜喜英 (Ramen-ya Akihide)

noodles.notes: Choice of medium-thick straight noodles or a medium-thin cut, both built to grab the heavy emulsified broth; the thick straight option (around 180g) has a chewy, substantial bite that stands up to the sauce-like soup.

ramen.signature_bowl: Junnoko / Tokuno rich ramen (純濃厚らーめん) — the single bowl this shop is built around: an extreme chicken-and-pork-bone broth poured at the density you choose, from "doro-doro" (thick enough to coat the ladle) to "mellow." Add garlic and/or a hit of yuzu peel at the counter.
ramen.chashu: Two cuts in one bowl: chunky aburi-style pork belly that is hung and roasted (吊るし焼き) for charred, fragrant edges that crumble apart, alongside a softer braised nibuta. Generous goro-goro chunks rather than thin slices.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a shoyu-based tare in a pork/chicken broth; nothing here is celiac-safe and no rice/konjac-noodle or GF option is documented.

vegan.note: The entire identity of the bowl is an animal broth boiled down from pork and chicken bones, finished with pork chashu; no plant-based broth or topping option exists.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto super-kotteri specialist where the broth is reduced so far it stands a ladle upright — a chicken-and-pork paitan closer to gravy than soup, the koukon end of Kyoto's rich-ramen lineage rather than the city's classic se-abura shoyu. You taste the bones themselves: hours of pork and chicken simmered into one viscous, glossy emulsion you can dial from doro-doro to mellow.

name: つけめん 恵那く (Tsukemen Enaku)

noodles.notes: House-made extra-thick (gokuboso/極太) straight noodles cut with whole-grain flour from Izawa Mills; chewy and silky (tsuru-tsuru/mochi-mochi) with a pronounced wheat sweetness that stands up to the heavy dip. Portions up to medium (around 275g) at one price.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tsukemen (つけめん) — extra-thick whole-grain noodles with a thick gyokai-tonkotsu/chicken dipping broth; the Tokusei (special) adds extra chashu, menma and a seasoned egg. Spicy (kara) and curry dip versions also offered.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork chashu, served in a doubled portion on the special bowl; rolled/sliced (not aburi-charred).

gf.note: Whole-grain wheat noodles with kansui and a shoyu-based dip make this unsafe for celiacs; no rice/konjac noodle or GF-tare option is documented.

vegan.note: The dip is built on a long-simmered pork/chicken/vegetable animal stock blended with a heavy seafood dashi (mackerel, bonito, sardine, oyster, etc.) plus pork chashu; no plant-only broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Ichijoji ramen-street specialist working in the gyokai-tonkotsu tsukemen idiom: a thick, double-soup dip where a long-cooked pork-chicken-vegetable stock is married to a deep ocean of mackerel, bonito, sardine and oyster, then finished with fish powder and oil. The soul is in that surf-and-turf concentration clinging to fat, chewy whole-grain noodles — rich enough that you finish it with a soup-wari dilution.

name: ラーメン二郎 京都店 (Ramen Jiro Kyoto-ten)

noodles.notes: Trademark Jiro-style flat, thick, low-hydration noodles — almost udon-like in heft. Boiled to a soft, springy 'mochi-mochi' chew rather than firm, so they droop slightly and drink up the fatty broth. Standard portion runs lighter than many Jiro shops (often cited around 280-300g).

ramen.signature_bowl: Ramen (small/futsu) ordered with the free 'mashi' call — niku-mashi for extra pork, plus yasai (sprouts), ninniku (garlic) and abura (back-fat) at the counter. The half-size or futsu is plenty for most; mashi the toppings, not the noodles, your first time.
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut braised pork (buta) simmered in the broth — the prized 'kami-buta': one chunk lean and meaty, another meltingly fatty and jiggly. Soft, fall-apart, deeply seasoned by the soup rather than a thin sliced loin.
notable_toppings: Kujo negi (Kyoto green onion, paid add-on)

gf.note: Thick wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a pork-and-shoyu (wheat) broth and tare. No gluten-free option exists; not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a long-boiled pork-bone broth loaded with back-fat, pork chunks and a shoyu tare. No plant-based broth is offered; not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.

ramen.pantheon: Kansai's lone direct-lineage Ramen Jiro — the unmistakable Tokyo-born 'Jiro-kei' creed of a thick, fatty, emulsified pork broth, mountains of garlicky sprouts and back-fat, and slabs of melting pork over heavy udon-like noodles. The Kyoto shop pours a notably mellow, richly emulsified soup whose deeper shoyu edge has earned it the nickname 'Kyoto Black,' a heady, garlic-charged bowl unlike anything else in the region.
sources: https://mwanderer.com/ラーメン二郎京都店に行ってきた感想/

name: 油そば専門店 ムジコ (Abura Soba Senmonten Mujiko)

noodles.notes: Chewy, hot medium-thick wavy noodles with a springy, firm bite (mochi-mochi/shiko-shiko) that grip the soy tare and oil pooled at the bowl's bottom. Reportedly supplied by a Kyoto noodle maker (Teigaku/Menya Teigaku). Free portion upsize from small through large (up to ~300g).

ramen.signature_bowl: Teiban Abura Soba (the standard brothless soy-tare abura soba) — pour in the vinegar and chili oil, mix vigorously, and finish with the fridge-cold fresh garlic. Also worth a look: the gyokai-niboshi (seafood and dried-sardine) version and the house chili-oil shibi-kara spicy bowl.
ramen.chashu: Two slices of braised pork chashu served atop the noodles.

gf.note: Abura soba is built on wheat-and-kansui noodles tossed in a soy-sauce (wheat-based) tare — no rice or konjac noodle or GF-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The tare is a lard-enriched soy sauce and the bowl is dressed with pork chashu; a seafood-dashi/niboshi variant also uses fish. No documented plant-only broth or oil. Not vegan.

ramen.pantheon: One of Kyoto's rare abura soba specialists, Mujiko serves the brothless Tokyo school of the dish: no soup at all, just hot springy noodles dredged through a pool of soy tare and lard at the bowl's base, then woken up with vinegar, chili oil and raw garlic. It is the soul of a ramen distilled to its seasoning and fat — the lineage you taste is the dry-noodle counter culture of Tokyo's student districts, transplanted into a Kyoto backstreet.

name: 本家 第一旭 本店 (Honke Daiichi Asahi Honten)

noodles.notes: Thin, straight noodles from a Kyoto noodle maker (Kondo Seimen). Firmness is customizable to taste at no charge.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka-soba / Ramen — pork-bone shoyu broth piled with sliced chashu, a mountain of Kyoto kujo-style green onion, and bean sprouts, with raw grated garlic to stir in at the table. The Tokusei (special) or Chashu-men adds a generous extra load of pork.
ramen.chashu: Braised sliced pork chashu, layered generously across the bowl as the headline element of the classic Chashu-men.
notable_toppings: raw grated garlic on the side to add to taste

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a Fushimi soy-sauce (wheat) tare; no rice/konjac noodle or GF-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Soul of the bowl is a long-simmered pork-bone broth with pork-fat sheen, soy tare and pork chashu — no plant-based broth bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: This is the wellspring of Kyoto's Takabashi-style ramen: a clear, golden pork-bone broth seasoned with unsweetened Fushimi soy sauce, deep with pork umami yet finishing light and clean rather than heavy. Crowned with a heap of fragrant Kyoto green onion and stirred with raw garlic, it tastes like the 1947 birthplace of a whole regional lineage — rich body, gentle aftertaste, eaten from dawn.

name: 中華そば ますたに 北白川本店 (Chuka Soba Masutani Kitashirakawa Honten)

noodles.notes: Old-school thin, straight, low-hydration noodles cooked on the soft side, designed to drink down easily with the lighter-than-it-looks broth. Diners can request firmer noodles (麺固め) at order.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (中華そば) — the single house bowl: chicken-bone shoyu broth blanketed with sweet pork back-fat and a generous heap of Kujo negi. Order the large (大) with extra negi; a pinch of the chili at the bottom shifts the flavor mid-bowl.
ramen.chashu: Traditional pork loin simmered until meltingly tender (horo-horo), sliced into rustic, irregular pieces.
notable_toppings: red chili / ichimi at the bottom of the bowl

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice/konjac noodle or GF-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Soul of the bowl is chicken-bone broth crowned with pork back-fat, with pork-loin chashu; no plant-broth version exists.

ramen.pantheon: This is the wellspring of Kyoto se-abura shoyu ramen — the 1948 original that taught the city to ladle sweet pork back-fat over a chicken-bone soy soup. You taste old Kyoto in it: a deep, faintly animal chicken stock that drinks far lighter than its glistening, fat-capped surface and mountain of Kujo negi suggest.

name: らぁ麺 とうひち (Ra-men Touhichi)

noodles.notes: In-house handmade straight noodles blending domestic wheat with stone-ground, whole-grain, and roasted-wheat-germ flours (no additives). Smooth, glossy mouthfeel with a springy bite and pronounced wheat aroma that stands up to the clear broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: 鶏醤油らぁ麺 (tori-shoyu / chicken shoyu ramen) — a crystal-clear chicken consommé built only from premium jidori (black chicken / Hinai-dori) and water, seasoned with a layered blend of fresh-pressed raw soy sauces and finished with fragrant golden chicken oil. Add the ajitama for the special bowl.
ramen.chashu: Two kinds: a low-temperature pork loin, lean and chewy with fat trimmed so it doesn't muddy the clear soy broth; and a thick, moist, gently-seasoned chicken breast chashu.

gf.note: Wheat-based handmade noodles with kansui and a soy-sauce (wheat) tare make this bowl unsuitable for celiac diners; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on chicken stock with chicken oil, plus pork and chicken chashu and a soy tare; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto refinement of the clear chicken-shoyu lineage: the soul lives in a long-simmered, days-aged jidori consommé so clean it drinks like a crystal-clear broth, lifted by a multi-soy-sauce tare and bright chicken oil. It tastes less like old Kyoto se-abura and more like the precise, ingredient-forward tori-paitan-and-chintan craft that has redefined Japan's top contemporary shoyu bowls.

name: らーめん セアブラノ神 壬生本店 (Ramen Seabura no Kami Mibu Honten)

noodles.notes: Specialty thick noodles from an outside maker (Tieou/Teigaku), close to udon diameter; built to stand up to the niboshi-shoyu soup and grab the back fat. A medium-thick option is also offered.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tokusei Seabura Niboshi Soba (special back-fat niboshi soba) with thick noodles
ramen.chashu: Braised pork shoulder-loin (kata-rosu), low-fat and clean, served in a neat cross-section slice

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Core soup is built on chicken/pork bones plus dried-sardine (niboshi) dashi and finished with pork back fat and pork chashu; no plant-broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is Kyoto's pioneering take on Niigata's Tsubame-Sanjo seabura-niboshi tradition: a dual soup of long-simmered chicken-and-pork chintan married to a cold-water niboshi extraction, crowned with chunky domestic pork back fat and a heap of minced raw onion. Despite the imposing "god of back fat" name, the bowl drinks surprisingly clean and umami-forward rather than heavy, with sardine depth carrying the soy tare.

name: 麺屋 龍玄 (Menya Ryugen)

noodles.notes: Thin, straight noodles that drink up the dark soy broth; firm bite that suits the jet-black Kyoto chuka soba.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba — the jet-black chicken-and-pork-bone soy sauce ramen, topped with pork loin chashu, bean sprouts, and chopped green onion (pairs famously with the shop's yakimeshi fried rice).
ramen.chashu: Braised pork loin (rosu) chashu — lean sliced loin rather than fatty belly, in keeping with the old-school Kyoto chuka-soba tradition.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-sauce tare (wheat-based); no rice or konjac noodle or gluten-free tare is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a chicken-and-pork-bone soy broth with pork loin chashu; no plant-based (kombu/shiitake/veg) bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A direct heir to Kyoto's legendary Shinpuku Saikan school, this is the city's signature jet-black chuka soba — a soy-dark broth where chicken bones and pork bone meld so the color reads fierce but the soy aroma stays measured, deep and savory rather than salty. You taste old Kyoto in every sip: an everyday, never-tiring bowl built on a lineage that defined the local style.

name: ラーメン池田屋 京都一乗寺店 (Ramen Ikedaya Kyoto Ichijoji-ten)

noodles.notes: Udon-like ultra-thick straight noodles with a firm, chewy bite and a retained core; a distinct wheat-grain aroma. Made in-house. Portions scale from petit (~180-200g) up to dai (500g), with calls to add 100g at a time.

ramen.signature_bowl: Ramen (sho/300g or chu/400g) with the free calls — yasai (piled softened bean sprouts and cabbage), ninniku (coarse raw garlic), abura (back-fat), karame (extra tare). A year-round and summer tsukemen variant is also a fan favorite.
ramen.chashu: Big chunks of soft, flaky braised pork ("horohoro buta") with a reddish hue, plus shredded pieces — both deeply soaked in a sweet-savory braise that falls apart at a touch.
notable_toppings: mountain of bean sprouts (moyashi) and cabbage (yasai)

gf.note: Jiro-style bowl is built on wheat ultra-thick noodles and a wheat-based shoyu tare over pork broth — not celiac-safe, and no rice/konjac noodle alternative is documented.

vegan.note: Broth and tare are pork-based with shoyu, finished with pork back-fat and topped with pork chashu. No plant-based broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A standard-bearer of Kyoto's Jiro-inspired scene on Ichijoji's famed ramen street: a thick, emulsified pork-and-shoyu soup slicked with back-fat, mountained with garlicky vegetables, and built around udon-thick house noodles. The soul here is the creamy, assertive tonkotsu-shoyu — you taste the Tachikawa-Jiro lineage in every viscous, garlic-charged spoonful, tightened by a dusting of chili.

name: 麺匠 たか松 本店 (Mensho Takamatsu Honten)

noodles.notes: Self-made noodles from Nagano-grown wheat stone-milled into whole-grain (zenryufun) flour, giving a brown, soba-like cast with a faint grainy bite and a smooth, clean slurp. Thin for a tsukemen, prized for the wheat aroma that comes through when dipped.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tsukemen (つけ麺) — thin stone-milled whole-wheat noodles served cold beside a thick, hot chicken-and-seafood dipping broth; eaten plain first, then brightened mid-bowl with onion, sudachi, and black shichimi. (The shop also pours a niboshi-forward "煮干し香る らぁ麺" bowl.)
ramen.chashu: Cubed braised pork chashu (gorogoro chunks) submerged in the dipping broth rather than sliced over the noodles.

gf.note: Noodles are stone-milled whole-wheat (wheat + kansui) and the dipping broth is shoyu-tare based, so it contains wheat; no rice- or konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented.

vegan.note: The dipping broth is built on chicken, pork, scallop and assorted seafood dashi, with pork chashu; no plant-only (kombu/shiitake/vegetable) bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto tsukemen house whose soul is a dense double-soup of chicken and seafood dashi — pork and scallop deepening the umami — that clings to brown, stone-milled whole-wheat noodles you slurp like fine soba. It belongs to the post-2010 wave of tori-gyokai dipping ramen rather than any regional gotōchi lineage, finished tableside with sudachi and Kyoto's Gen'yokaku black shichimi for a citrus-and-sansho lift.

name: 麺屋 優光 (Menya Yukou)

noodles.notes: Made fresh daily in the shop's own noodle factory. A proprietary blend of whole-wheat (zenryufun) flour for wheat aroma and udon flour for strong koshi — surprisingly powerful, chewy bite that stands up to the delicate clam broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Hachiku (淡竹) — clam-dashi chuka soba in shoyu, topped with three generous slices of seared chashu. Add the torched rare-pork rice bowl with ajitama for great value.
ramen.chashu: Seared (aburi) pork chashu, served as thick slices flame-torched to order; a torched rare roast-pork (rea-cha) is also offered over rice as a side bowl.

gf.note: Noodles are a wheat blend (whole-wheat plus udon flour) and the tare is Kyoto soy sauce — both contain wheat. No documented rice or konjac noodle option; not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a shellfish dashi (oyster, littleneck and asari clams) finished with pork chashu — no plant-based broth or topping option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto original from a chef of the Jinrui Mina Menrui lineage: a clear shoyu chuka soba whose soul is a sweet, rounded shellfish dashi — oyster, hamaguri and asari clams — laced with local Kyoto soy. Clam ramen is a rarity in Kyoto, and here the briny-sweet shellfish umami reads delicate yet deep, a clean counterpoint to the city's heavier se-abura traditions.

name: あいつのラーメン かたぐるま 本店 (Aitsu no Ramen Kataguruma Honten)

noodles.notes: House-made (jikasei) flat straight noodles, high-hydration, smooth and slippery (tsuru-tsuru) with a chewy, mochi-like bite that catches the thick broth well. Tsukemen portions step up small/medium/large.

ramen.signature_bowl: Daigutton (Kokuton) Ramen — the foam-system rich tonkotsu-and-seafood bowl; or the Ultra Tsukemen, ordered with the rare chashu add. Lunch is served shio, dinner shoyu.
ramen.chashu: Signature low-and-slow rare pork chashu, oven-roasted with salt and pepper and finished with a light char/aburi on the surface; dramatic pink rose-colored centers, thick and meaty (a generous fan of slices in the chashu bowl).

gf.note: House-made wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat shoyu tare at dinner and a pork-bone/seafood broth — no rice or konjac noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Core broth is whipped pork-bone, chicken, and seafood (gyokai) with pork fat and rare pork chashu throughout. No plant-based broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A foam-system (awa-kei) bowl from a master who trained at Kyoto's revered Appareya: a pork-bone, chicken and seafood broth whipped to a creamy, aerated cloud so the koku hits soft and rich rather than heavy. The lineage shows in the daytime shio's wild, spice-forward punch and the evening shoyu's fish-powder depth, ladled over slippery house-made flat noodles.

name: 京つけめん つるかめ 六角 (Kyo Tsukemen Tsurukame Rokkaku)

noodles.notes: House-made (jikasei) noodles using domestic whole-wheat (zenryufun) flour. High-hydration (takasui), notably mochi-mochi and chewy with real heft and weight on the chopsticks for the dipping bowls; in the soup ramen they read as flatter, medium-thin and silky-smooth. Free oomori (large) at the same price.

ramen.signature_bowl: 魚介醤油つけ麺 (gyokai-shoyu tsukemen) — chewy whole-wheat house noodles served cold with a thick, concentrated chicken-pork-and-seafood dipping broth finished with yuzu.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork belly, two slices, on the firmer side and seasoned to taste of the pork itself rather than a heavy soy glaze.

gf.note: House-made noodles are wheat (whole-wheat) plus kansui and the broth carries a shoyu tare and pork/chicken/fish dashi; no rice- or konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken, pork and seafood (gyokai) dashi with pork-belly chashu; no plant-based kombu/shiitake broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A flagship of Kyoto's own modern tsukemen movement: hefty, chewy whole-wheat house noodles meet a thick, glossy dipping broth where pork and chicken body lead and a wave of seafood dashi follows, lifted at the finish by a fragrant snap of yuzu. The soul here is that gyokai-tonkotsu "W-soup" richness — bold and salty yet clean, an original Kyoto take rather than any inherited regional lineage.

name: ラーメン荘 地球規模で考えろ 伏見本店 (Ramen So Chikyu Kibo de Kangaero (Fushimi Ho)

noodles.notes: Hefty, snappy, low-hydration thick noodles with a bouncy, chewy 'buri-buri patsu-patsu' bite and visible wheaty edges — the muscular Jiro-style noodle. Portions are huge: 'sho' (small) 200g, 'nami' 300g, 'dai' 400g, with extra noodle add-ons available.

ramen.signature_bowl: Buta Ramen (pork ramen) — order it 'nami' size and, when asked, call your toppings: 'yasai-ninniku-abura' (extra vegetables, raw garlic, back-fat) with 'karame' for a stronger tare hit. The Buta Double piles on even more of the shop's prized fatty pork.
ramen.chashu: Big, soft slabs of braised pork — a finely-textured leaner piece alongside outrageously fatty belly chunks (jokingly called '80% fat'), so meltingly tender they go marshmallow-soft in the hot soup. The fat is the draw, not a flaw.
notable_toppings: mountain of boiled vegetables (cabbage + bean sprouts / moyashi)
ichimi togarashi chili (standard on the ramen)

gf.note: Wheat thick noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare and pork-bone broth — no rice/konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is offered. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The entire bowl is built on pork-bone broth, pork back-fat, and big slabs of pork chashu, with a shoyu tare. No plant-based broth or topping bowl exists here.

ramen.pantheon: A flag-bearer of Kansai's Jiro-inspired scene: a thunderous, non-emulsified pork-bone-and-soy soup whose clear-but-mighty broth carries raw pork umami rather than creamy emulsion. Crowned with a mountain of garlicky vegetables and glistening back-fat over muscular thick noodles, it's the full 'yasai-ninniku-abura' ritual — a bowl you don't so much eat as conquer.

name: 貝だし麺 きた田 (Kaidashimen Kitada)

noodles.notes: Thin-to-medium straight noodles with a smooth surface and a soft, plump, slightly sticky bite from a touch of extra hydration; built to carry and absorb the delicate shellfish soup rather than to chew firm.

ramen.signature_bowl: 貝だし麺 (Kaidashi-men) — the shellfish-dashi salt ramen; the special version adds extra scallop and comes dressed with chashu, ajitama and wonton.
ramen.chashu: A trio of thin-sliced meats rather than a single cut: char-grilled (aburi) pork shoulder loin and pork belly alongside a slice of lean chicken breast, all lightly seared and laid over the soup.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a seasoning tare that includes soy/wheat; the wonton wrappers are wheat too. No rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented — not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The entire identity of the bowl is a shellfish broth (clam, scallop, hamaguri) finished with pork and chicken chashu and a wonton with pork filling. No plant-only broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A rare Kyoto take on the modern shellfish-dashi school: the soul of this bowl is a broth drawn purely from clams, hamaguri and scallop, gently salt-seasoned so the briny-sweet shellfish liquor reads clean and delicate rather than rich. It belongs to the new wave of single-ingredient kaidashi shio ramen rather than any old-school regional lineage, a quiet contrast to Kyoto's usual heavy se-abura shoyu.

name: ゑびす屋 (Ebisuya)

noodles.notes: Straight Chinese-style noodles, on the softer side, served at a medium thickness — accounts differ slightly between Kyoto-thin and slightly-thick, but consistently straight and tender rather than firm or wavy.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tori-gara shoyu ramen (鶏ガラしょうゆ) — the house bowl: a clear chicken-bone broth crowned with melted back-fat and a sharp soy edge. Often ordered with the fried-rice (yakimeshi) set, and 'negi-large' for a mound of fresh scallion.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork loin, hand-cut into thick slices so tender they nearly crumble apart; seasoned lightly to let the meat itself come through rather than a heavy soak.
notable_toppings: abundant green onion (negi) — upgradable to negi-large

gf.note: Standard wheat-and-kansui Chinese noodles plus a soy-sauce (wheat-based) tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a chicken-bone broth finished with pork back-fat and pork chashu; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is textbook 'gansō' Kyoto ramen — a clear chicken-bone broth made deep and sweet by a slick of melted pork back-fat (se-abura) over a confident soy tare, the se-abura-chaccha lineage that defines the city's old-school bowls. The fat reads rich on the lips while the soup itself drinks surprisingly clean, a Kyoto balancing act of indulgence and restraint.

name: 拳ラーメン (Kobushi Ramen)

noodles.notes: Chu-hoso (thin-to-medium) straight noodles from the long-established Teikaku (棣鄂) noodle maker; pronounced wheat fragrance with a supple, clean-biting texture that suits the delicate clear broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: 京鴨とノドグロ煮干しそば (Kyoto-duck & nodoguro-niboshi soba) with ajitama — a golden, glass-clear bowl where duck umami opens and dried-sardine nodoguro depth follows, finished with a yuzu-vinegar table condiment to brighten the soup mid-bowl.
ramen.chashu: Two proteins: low-temp pork shoulder chashu, tender and lean, served alongside a delicate poached chicken-breast ham — a refined pair rather than a fatty belly slab.

gf.note: Wheat noodles from a conventional noodle maker plus shoyu-based tare; no documented rice or konjac noodle or GF-tare option. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: A genuine plant-based bowl is documented: a Rausu-kombu kake-soba built only from kombu (kelp) and water with a light non-animal tare and plant oil, created for vegetarian/foreign diners. Confirm current availability, as it may be limited or seasonal.

ramen.pantheon: A modern-original Kyoto bowl that trades the city's classic se-abura shoyu heritage for haute restraint: a crystal-clear soup where Kyoto duck and prized nodoguro dried sardine meet, tasting closer to a fine consomme than a typical noodle broth. Its soul is layered, lingering dashi clarity rather than richness — niboshi depth handled with delicacy.

name: セアブラノ神 伏見剛力 (Seabura no Kami Fushimi Goriki)

noodles.notes: The signature se-abura shoyu bowl runs firm, low-hydration noodles — a finer, almost Hakata-leaning straight cut that stands up to the fatty broth, with a medium-thick option also offered. The separate 神二郎 jiro bowl swaps in its own ultra-thick crimped (wavy) noodles.

ramen.signature_bowl: 背脂中華そば (se-abura chuka soba) — the Kyoto back-fat soy-sauce bowl: a chicken-and-pork stock laced with dried-sardine dashi, seasoned with sweet-savory Kyoto Gokko shoyu tare and blanketed in melting pork back-fat. Add the 黒バラ海苔 (black laver) for a mid-bowl flavor shift. The bold can instead order the 神二郎, the shop's regularized Jiro-style mountain.
ramen.chashu: Two thick slices of low-temperature rare-cooked pork; the regular menu has at times also run a soft braised pork-belly chashu, and the 神二郎 bowl is dressed with rare chashu.

gf.note: Standard wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-based soy-sauce tare; the dried-fish/pork/chicken broth and back fat are also animal-derived. No rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is documented — not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Built on chicken bones, pork knuckle and dried-sardine dashi, finished with pork back-fat and pork chashu. No plant-based broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is Kyoto's own se-abura shoyu soul — a clear chicken-and-pork stock deepened with dried sardine, sweetened by local Gokko soy tare, then cloaked in a snowfall of melted pork back-fat that clings to firm, fine noodles. The Fushimi branch pushes harder than its siblings, planting a full Jiro-style mountain alongside the classic bowl for those who want the back-fat lineage at maximum volume.

name: 麺匠 一粒万倍 寺町店 (Mensho Ichiryu Manbai Teramachi)

noodles.notes: Thin, low-hydration straight noodles with whole-wheat flour worked in, boiled firm for a crisp, snappy (patsu-patsu) bite reminiscent of Hakata-style thin noodles. The branch-exclusive tsukemen instead uses thick, wavy, bran-flecked noodles served cold and firmly set.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tokusei Shoyu Ramen — the dark, savory soy bowl over the clear duck-and-Daisen-chicken broth (the shio version is the lighter alternative). The thick seafood-pork-bone tsukemen, including a spicy version, is a branch-exclusive draw.
ramen.chashu: Two kinds: a low-temperature rare pork shoulder-loin chashu, moist with a balance of lean and fat umami and a faint smokiness, plus a tender low-temperature chicken-breast chashu.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice/konjac noodle or gluten-free option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on duck and chicken with chicken-fat aroma oil and pork/chicken chashu; the tsukemen adds pork bone and fish powder. No plant-based broth is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto bowl that trades the city's old pork-back-fat shoyu lineage for a refined, clear duck-and-chicken chintan: the soul lives in a glistening golden soup of duck and Daisen-dori (Tottori) chicken, deep and aromatic yet free of any gamey edge and lifted by fragrant chicken oil. It is tanrei (light-and-clear) ramen at its most elegant, finished tableside with black pepper, sansho, or vinegar to taste.

name: 麺屋 優光 河原町 (Menya Yukou Kawaramachi)

noodles.notes: Springy medium-thick wavy noodles incorporating whole-grain flour, with a satisfying chew that carries the broth well. The shellfish and shio bowls present them in a clear, refined soup; the duck tsukemen pairs them with a concentrated dipping broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: 貝らーめん (Shellfish Ramen) — a light, refined Kyoto-shoyu broth drawing oyster, asari clam, and shijimi clam umami; add the rare seared chashu. The duck-fat tsukemen (鴨つけ麺, limited daily) is the standout alternative.
ramen.chashu: The heart is a rare, lightly seared (aburi) pork chashu — soft, pink, and gently charred at the edges; a thin-cut braised pork is also offered, and bowls can be loaded with multiple slices.
notable_toppings: yuzu/citrus accent on the shio bowl

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles with a Kyoto soy-sauce (wheat) tare throughout; no rice or konjac noodle option and no gluten-free tare is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on animal/seafood dashi — shellfish (oyster/clam), bonito-and-kombu, chicken, or duck fat — plus pork or duck toppings. No plant-only broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: This is Kyoto's clear-soup counterpoint to the city's famous se-abura richness: a delicate kaidashi (shellfish-dashi) shoyu where oyster, asari, and shijimi umami are floated on local Kyoto soy sauce, so you taste the bivalve sweetness rather than fat. Alongside it sits a deeply aromatic duck-fat tsukemen built on kombu-and-bonito, marking the shop as a modern Kyoto refined-shoyu specialist.

name: 濃厚京鶏白湯らーめん めんや 美鶴 (Nōkō Kyō Tori Paitan Rāmen Menya Mitsuru)

noodles.notes: Medium-thick straight noodles chosen to stand up to the thick, clinging chicken broth; an extra-rich (super nōkō) version pours an even more viscous soup over the same noodles.

ramen.signature_bowl: Nōkō tori paitan rāmen (rich chicken paitan) — choose white (shio) or black (shoyu); a super-rich version delivers an even thicker, gravy-like soup.
ramen.chashu: Chicken chashu — moist, juicy, lightly seasoned pieces of poultry (no pork used) that soak up the broth.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare option; no documented rice or konjac noodle, so not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken and aromatic vegetables finished with bonito (katsuobushi) dashi, and toppings are chicken chashu and tsukune — no plant-based bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto tori-paitan specialist: pure chicken-and-vegetable bones whipped into a creamy, collagen-thick white soup, then deepened with Kyoto katsuobushi dashi so a quiet seafood hum runs beneath the poultry richness. Choose shio for the cleanest expression of that chicken sweetness or shoyu for a darker, rounder finish — and reach for the Gion black-shichimi to cut the body with citrus and pepper.

name: 珍遊 河原町六角店 (Chinyu Kawaramachi Rokkaku)

noodles.notes: Medium-thin, low-hydration straight noodles milled from fragrant Hokkaido wheat; they start crisp and firm at the bite and soften into a chew as they drink up the fatty soup.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka-soba (中華そば) — the house bowl: a chicken-rich, milky soy broth crowned with scattered back-fat, with the amount of se-abura adjustable to taste.
ramen.chashu: Braised roast-pork chashu, sliced and laid over the noodles.

gf.note: Wheat noodles and a wheat-based soy tare put this beyond celiac-safe; no rice- or konjac-noodle alternative is offered.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a chicken-bone broth finished with pork back-fat and topped with pork chashu — no plant-based broth or topping option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A founding voice of the Kyoto se-abura shoyu lineage, simmering whole chickens, feet and bones into a milky-rich soup that is sweetened with dark Kyoto soy and showered with melting back-fat. It is the classic Kyoto paradox in a bowl — it looks decadently heavy, yet the finish pulls back clean and light.

name: らーめん 鶴武者 (Ramen Tsurumusha)

noodles.notes: Milled and cut in-house every day on the noodle machine at the entrance, blending rare Kyoto-grown 'Kyo-komugi' wheat with Hokkaido flour. Chewy, springy and aromatic of wheat.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chicken paitan ramen (鶏白湯らーめん) — a salt-based bowl of creamy Omi-chicken soup rounded out with pork bone and a seafood-kombu dashi; the plain らーめん and seasonal tsukemen round out the menu.
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut pork shoulder loin, gently low-temperature cooked so it stays rare, tender and pink, seasoned only with salt to let the meat's own flavor lead.
notable_toppings: soft-boiled ajitama from fresh Kyoto eggs

gf.note: Noodles are house-milled wheat (Kyoto + Hokkaido flour) with kansui, and the tare/soup carry soy and dashi. No rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is documented; not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Soul of the bowl is animal- and fish-derived: Omi chicken, pork bone, plus scallop/dried-shrimp/katsuo-style seafood dashi, with chicken-fat aroma and pork chashu. No documented plant-only broth bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A family-minded Kyoto kitchen that treats the bowl as cooking rather than chemistry — additive-free soup of Shiga's Omi chicken and pork bone laced with a kombu-and-shellfish seafood dashi, served either as a creamy-yet-clean salt paitan or a lighter clear bowl. It is a modern, ingredient-driven house style rather than a classic gotochi lineage, its soul tasted in that layered chicken-and-sea umami carried on house-milled Kyoto-wheat noodles.

name: 麺逢 KATAJIKENAI (Men-Au KATAJIKENAI)

noodles.notes: Specialty noodles supplied by Menya Teigaku, a Kyoto noodle maker known for ramen and tsukemen. Generally medium-thin and high-hydration, boiled deliberately soft to marry with the soup and release a wheaty aroma; the chicken-paitan side is paired with a firmer, thinner cut for a satisfying bite.

ramen.signature_bowl: Kai-dashi (shellfish) Soba — a clear, intensely savory clam-driven broth; the creamy Tori Paitan Soba is the equally celebrated second pillar, also offered as a limited tsukemen.
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut, low-temperature rare roast pork (belly/loin) served barely seasoned so the meat's own umami melts into the broth.
notable_toppings: house basil-flavored oil as a flavor-changer

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus wheat-based tare; no rice, konjac, or gluten-free noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Both signatures are animal-based — chicken paitan and a clam/shellfish-and-fish dashi — with rare pork chashu standard. No plant-broth (kombu/shiitake/veg) bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern, two-souled Kyoto bowl from a chef who trained at Osaka's celebrated Tori-soba ZAGIN: on one side a clear broth wrung from clams and shellfish that tastes of the sea — light yet quietly addictive — and on the other a velvety, blender-whipped chicken paitan with no barnyard funk. A dab of house basil oil is the signature flourish, and the lineage you taste is contemporary tori-paitan-meets-kaidashi rather than any old gotochi school.

name: ラーメンムギュ VOL.1本店 (Ramen Mugyu Vol.1 Honten)

noodles.notes: Signature bowl uses flat hand-massaged (hira-uchi te-momi) noodles with a chewy, springy bite; the rich tonkotsu-tori soba is paired instead with thin straight noodles, and the maze-style bowl with chewy thick noodles.

ramen.signature_bowl: Onibara Shiro (おにばら 白) — a clear golden Nagoya Cochin chicken broth seasoned with a light shio/thin-soy tare, finished with finely minced onion folded into the soup itself rather than scattered on top. A black version (Onibara Kuro) swaps in Kyoto-style re-brewed soy for deeper sweetness, and a separate rich, cloudy chicken-and-vegetable tonkotsu-tori soba rounds out the lineup.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork belly finished firm rather than melt-soft; excess fat is trimmed so the slices keep a meaty chew and restrained sweetness — the shop's proudest element (also sold over rice as the Mugyu-don). The signature bowl carries roughly four slices.
notable_toppings: minced onion blended into the broth
optional nira (chive) paste with mustard seasoning

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus soy-based tare; no rice-noodle, konjac, or gluten-free tare option is documented.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on Nagoya Cochin chicken broth and topped with braised pork belly; no plant-only (kombu/shiitake/vegetable) broth bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto chicken-clear specialist whose soul is pure Nagoya Cochin: a refined golden chintan where minced onion is dissolved into the soup for natural sweetness and a delicate, oniony crispness instead of being piled on as a garnish. It sits in the contemporary jidori-shio lineage rather than Kyoto's heavy se-abura tradition, letting the bird and a whisper of light soy carry the bowl.

name: ラーメン荘 地球規模で考えろ ソラ (Ramen-so Chikyu Kibo de Kangaero Sora)

noodles.notes: Flat, thick, springy chewy noodles (平打ち太麺) with a crisp 'puri-patsu' bite, classic Jiro lineage. Free noodle increases (200g/300g/400g) at a fixed topping price.

ramen.signature_bowl: Buta Ramen (pork ramen) — order with the call for yasai (vegetables), ninniku (garlic), abura (back-fat) and karame (extra tare): the full Jiro experience with a mountain of bean sprouts and slabs of pork.
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut, heavily seasoned braised pork (buta) slabs — tender, fatty, generously portioned in the pork-ramen build.
notable_toppings: one-flavor chili powder over the veg

gf.note: Wheat-based thick noodles and a shoyu (wheat) tare; a fat-laden Jiro bowl with no documented rice or konjac noodle option. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Pork tonkotsu broth, pork back-fat, shoyu tare and pork chashu throughout. No plant-based broth option documented; the 'vegetable' call is bean sprouts piled into a meat soup.

ramen.pantheon: A full-throttle bowl in the Ramen Jiro lineage: a thick, glossy pork-tonkotsu shoyu soup slicked with melted back-fat, buried under a towering mound of bean sprouts and raw garlic, with flat chewy noodles built to stand up to it all. The seasoning stays restrained so the porky dashi depth carries the bowl — comfort and excess in equal measure.

name: 麺飯食堂 てら田や (Menhan Shokudo Teradaya)

noodles.notes: Straight medium-thick, low-hydration noodles built for nodogoshi (throat-clearance) over heavy chew, with a clean, slightly firm bite that suits the clear soup; the same noodle is used for both the chuka-soba and the se-abura ramen.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tokusei Ramen (special back-fat shoyu ramen) — pork clear broth crowned with generous se-abura, loaded with extra chashu, three handmade wonton, and a soft-set ajitama; the Tokusei Chuka-soba (pork clear broth + fish dashi) is the cleaner alternative.
ramen.chashu: Large, slightly thick-cut braised pork slices — moist and tender, soaking up the soy broth, with full pork sweetness; available as a chashu-men topped variant.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles and a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on pork clear broth, with fish dashi in the chuka-soba and pork back-fat and chashu throughout. No plant-based broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A faithful heir to Kyoto's beloved se-abura chacha lineage: a soy-forward pork chintan dusted with sweet pearls of back-fat that melt into a mellow, gingery depth — the same hometown soul you taste in the city's classic Omiya-area shops. The companion chuka-soba leans cleaner, marrying that pork clarity with a quiet wave of fish dashi.

name: 頑固麺 (Gankomen)

noodles.notes: Medium-thick noodles (some descriptions straight, some slightly wavy) built to grip the near-sludgy broth; sourced from a noodle maker, served firm by default. A few special bowls use extra-thick flat high-hydration noodles.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tori-tonkotsu ramen — the thick, creamy chicken-and-pork-bone bowl in shoyu tare that the shop is known for. A niboshi tori-tonkotsu (dried-sardine version) and limited tsukemen also rotate through.
ramen.chashu: Large, generously cut rare-style chashu — tender, low-temperature pork left pink in the center with crisp rendered fat edges.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare make this bowl unsuitable for celiacs; no rice- or konjac-noodle alternative is documented.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a long-simmered chicken-and-pork-bone broth finished with chicken fat and topped with pork chashu; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern-original Kyoto thick-soup bowl: long-boiled chicken and pork bones emulsified into a near-sludgy, glossy paitan, then seasoned with a deeply aromatic soy tare so the richness lands full but clean. It belongs to the contemporary kotteri tori-tonkotsu wave rather than any one regional lineage, with the umami of the bones — not the seasoning — carrying the soul.

name: 麺処 虵の目屋 (Mendokoro Janomeya)

noodles.notes: Homemade Japanese wheat noodles. The tori paitan is paired with a medium, flat, smooth noodle prized for its slick throat-feel; the lighter chicken-niboshi soba gets a thinner, straight noodle with a crisp bite.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tori Soba (clear 100% jidori chicken shoyu soba); the rich Tori Paitan is the other half of the house's identity, with a Shio Tori Soba and a chicken-niboshi soba rounding out the lineup.
ramen.chashu: Two proteins: low-temperature-cooked chicken plus pork, finished over charcoal to add a smoky, savory aroma; thinly sliced pork loin is served rare alongside tender chicken breast.

gf.note: Noodles are homemade wheat-and-kansui and the tare is soy-based; nothing rice/konjac-noodle or GF documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on chicken bones and whole birds (and a chicken-niboshi version adds dried sardine), with chicken/pork chashu; no plant-broth bowl exists.

ramen.pantheon: A modern-original Kyoto chicken specialist where a French-trained chef coaxes everything from 100% local jidori — just bones, whole birds, and water — into either a clear, soy-sharpened tori soba or a creamy, concentrated tori paitan. You taste pure poultry rather than any regional lineage, finished with the smoke of charcoal-grilled chashu.

name: 京都千丸 しゃかりき 本店 (Kyoto Senmaru Shakariki Honten)

noodles.notes: Extra-thick, chewy wavy noodles (極太ちぢれ麺) built to grab and hold the thick dipping broth; springy, mochi-mochi bite. Upsize available.

ramen.signature_bowl: 濃厚魚介つけそば (Nōkō Gyokai Tsukesoba) — extra-thick wavy noodles dipped in a thick, concentrated fish-and-pork dipping broth, with the optional 超濃厚 (super-rich) upgrade.
ramen.chashu: Two kinds served together: a moist braised pork chashu cut from a meaty, juicy portion, plus a chicken chashu — both kept tender rather than dry.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a shoyu (wheat) tare and pork/fish broth; no rice or konjac noodle option documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on a pork-bone and chicken double soup loaded with niboshi and katsuo fish dashi, plus pork/chicken chashu. No plant-based broth documented.

ramen.pantheon: In a city where ramen means glossy se-abura shoyu, this shop carved its own lane: a thick gyokai-tonkotsu W-soup that marries pork-and-chicken body with the deep, slightly bitter punch of niboshi (maiwashi, sanma, sagoshi) and a finishing dust of fish powder. You taste that fish-forward double-soup lineage most in the tsukemen, where extra-thick noodles drag up a dip you stretch out with a hit of katsuo soup-wari at the end.

name: 本家 第一旭 たかばし本店 (Honke Daiichi Asahi Takabashi Honten)

noodles.notes: Custom medium-gauge straight noodles from a local seimen (Kondo) maker, designed to bind cleanly with the clear pork-shoyu broth. Firm but not low-hydration.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chashumen (チャーシューメン) — the standard chuka-soba piled with extra sliced pork; ask for a mound of nine-jo green onion (free negi-mori) and red (lean) or white (fatty) chashu.
ramen.chashu: Braised sliced loin/leg from rare domestic female pigs (twice-farrowed sows), lean and deeply savory rather than fatty; diners may request "aka" (red/lean) or "shiro" (white/fatty) cuts at no charge.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a raw soy-sauce (wheat) tare; no rice/konjac noodle or GF-tare option is offered. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The broth is 100% pork bone and the bowl is built on pork chashu and rendered pork fat; no plant-based broth or topping option exists.

ramen.pantheon: This is the wellspring of Kyoto ramen: a clear, golden 100%-pork-bone broth seasoned with raw, unpasteurized Fushimi soy sauce and glossed with the lard that rises naturally off the kettle. Deceptively light at first sip, it reveals a deep, gentle pork-shoyu soul beneath a snowy heap of nine-jo green onion — the 1947 archetype that spawned Kyoto's whole pork-shoyu lineage.

name: 麦の夜明け (Mugi no Yoake)

noodles.notes: House-milled noodles cut chuhoso (medium-thin) and straight, made from Yamaguchi-prefecture 'Setokirara' wheat. The owner came from the flour-milling trade, and the noodle work is a point of pride: a smooth, springy strand with clean bite, tuned to carry the delicate clear broth without overpowering it.

ramen.signature_bowl: Hotate to Sansho no Chuka Soba — scallop-and-sansho chuka soba: a pale, clear triple-soup of jidori chicken, pork and dried scallop seasoned with a white-soy/salt tare, finished with the floral citrus tingle of Wakayama grape sansho. The special (tokusei) adds the full topping spread.
ramen.chashu: Two proteins: a low-temperature-cooked pork (rosu/loin style, served as tender pink slices) alongside braised duck breast — a refined, restraint-forward pairing rather than a fatty braise.
notable_toppings: torch-seared Hokkaido raw scallop marinated in soy (the signature crown)
grape sansho pepper / sansho-kombu paste added to taste

gf.note: House-made wheat noodles ('Setokirara' wheat) plus a soy-based tare and wonton skins make this bowl wheat-throughout; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is an animal-and-seafood broth (chicken, pork, dried scallop) with seafood-driven umami, topped with scallop, duck and pork. No plant-only (kombu/shiitake/veg) broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern-original Kyoto bowl that rewrites chuka-soba in a fine-dining key: instead of the city's classic pork-back-fat shoyu, it ladles a crystal-clear chicken-pork-and-scallop soup whose sweetness is pulled forward by a whisper of white-soy salt. The defining signature is Wakayama grape sansho — a floral, citrus-numbing lift that turns a delicate seafood broth into something unmistakably its own.

name: ギオンダックヌードル (Gion Duck Noodles)

noodles.notes: Extremely thin (~1.1mm) handmade straight noodles made daily from German rye whole-grain flour, giving a distinctive grain-forward texture; clings to the duck broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Duck (kamo) ramen: a clear, deeply aromatic broth built on premium Kishu/Wakayama brand duck, served with duck loin and thigh chashu and finished tableside with organic grape sansho. Eat plain first, then stir in the sansho for a bright, citrus-fragrant lift.
ramen.chashu: Duck chashu, offered as loin or thigh — thick-cut, tender slices of brand Kishu duck rather than the usual pork.

gf.note: Handmade noodles are made from German rye flour (wheat-family gluten grain) and the seasoning is a shoyu-based tare, so the bowl is not celiac-safe. No rice or konjac noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The soul of every bowl is duck broth and duck chashu, and the optional siphon dashi adds bonito/kombu/shiitake. No plant-only broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A modern, French-trained original rather than any regional gotochi style: where most ramen leans on pork or chicken, here the soul is duck — a clear, elegant kamo shoyu broth perfumed with the bird and lifted by citrusy grape sansho. The rye noodles and tableside fruit-and-wine tsukemen sauces make it one of Kyoto's most refined, chef-driven bowls.

name: 新宿めんや風花 (Shinjuku Menya Fuhka)

noodles.notes: House-made thick, wavy (chijire) noodles with a springy, chewy bite that grows softer and more delicate as they steep in the broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tennen Shio Ramen (natural salt ramen) — a transparent golden salt broth seasoned with Ishigaki Island sea salt, with house-made thick wavy noodles and hot braised pork chashu.
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut braised pork belly, simmered until exceptionally tender and fatty; unusually served piping hot rather than chilled, so its fat melts into the broth and deepens its umami as you eat.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles; salt broth is built on a wheat-free shio tare, but the noodles themselves are not gluten-free and no rice/konjac noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken, pork bones, and fish/shellfish dashi (dried shrimp, ago, katsuo, scallop); pork chashu throughout. No plant-only bowl documented.

ramen.pantheon: A specialist shio-ramen house whose soul is a crystal-clear golden broth — chicken and pork bones woven with dried shrimp, flying fish, bonito and a whisper of scallop, finished with natural Ishigaki sea salt. It trades the heavy se-abura of typical Kyoto ramen for delicate, layered umami you taste as a gentle, clean wave rather than a salty punch.

name: ねこまた (Nekomata)

noodles.notes: Flat, broad ribbon-like noodles likened to kishimen, with a chewy, springy bite that clings to the shoyu tare and oil at the bottom of the bowl. Brothless abura soba, served without soup.

ramen.signature_bowl: Ganso (original) shoyu abura soba — flat noodles dressed in a shoyu-based tare and aromatic oil; mix thoroughly from the bottom, then add rayu chili oil and vinegar to taste. Also offered in spicy-miso (kara-miso) and ebi-shio (shrimp salt) variations.
ramen.chashu: Angular-cut (sai-no-me / cubed) braised pork chashu tossed through the noodles rather than laid in slices.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare make this brothless dish unsafe for celiacs; no rice- or konjac-noodle alternative is documented.

vegan.note: The tare and oil carry bonito (katsuo) and the bowl includes pork chashu and fish cake; no plant-based version is documented. Worth asking, but assume not vegan.

ramen.pantheon: A soup-less bowl in the Tokyo-born abura soba lineage — billed as one of the Kansai region's pioneering oil-noodle specialists at the foot of Kiyomizu-dera. Here the soul lives not in a broth but in a glossy pool of shoyu tare and fragrant oil at the bottom of the bowl, which you toss up through thick chewy noodles and finish with your own hit of vinegar and chili.

name: 広東料理 鳳泉 (Kanton Ryori Hosen)

noodles.notes: Nostalgic thin, straight noodles that drink up the gravy. This is not a soup-ramen noodle but an ankake (thickened-gravy) lo-mein style; the soul is the karashi (Japanese mustard) that is worked through the dish, giving a piri-piri sharpness against a sweet-salty soy gravy.

ramen.signature_bowl: Karashi Soba / Ebi-Kashiwa Soba (からしそば・エビカシワソバ) — thin straight noodles under a thick, sweet-salty soy ankake gravy laced with Japanese mustard, eaten lo-mein style (撈麺) rather than as a soup.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-based (wheat) ankake gravy thickened with starch; no rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The signature dish is built on a chicken-based gravy with shrimp and chicken; no plant-broth/animal-free noodle bowl is documented at this Cantonese-Kyoto shop.

ramen.pantheon: This is not soup ramen at all but the soul of Kyoto-Chinese (京風中華): the famous Karashi Soba, a lineage dish carried over from the legendary Houmai, where thin straight noodles are bound in a glossy sweet-salty soy gravy and cut through with a bold streak of Japanese mustard. You taste the city's gentle, refined Chinese cooking in the ankake — chicken and dried shiitake umami rather than a punchy ramen tare.

name: らーめん千の風 四条河原町店 (Ramen Sen no Kaze Shijo-Kawaramachi)

noodles.notes: Standard medium chuka-soba style noodles paired with the creamy broth; tsukemen uses a thicker dipping noodle. Exact gauge not documented.

ramen.signature_bowl: Kyo no Shio (京の塩) — the creamy yet light salt-tare tonkotsu in a milky-white broth, topped with torched chashu
ramen.chashu: Aburi (torched/grilled) chashu — surface charred for a fragrant, caramelized edge while the interior stays soft and melt-in-the-mouth.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus shoyu/miso tare; the soy-sauce bowl is explicitly soybean-and-wheat based. No rice or konjac noodle and no GF-tare option is documented, so not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broths are built on pork bones and/or bonito-and-kombu seafood dashi, and bowls carry pork chashu. A 'su-ramen' (plain) exists but its broth is still animal/fish-based, and no documented plant-only (kombu/shiitake/veg) bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A self-styled "gentle, natural" Kyoto bowl that softens the tonkotsu idea into a creamy-white yet remarkably clean soup — rich on the tongue but free of the heavy back-fat slick, finished with fragrant torched chashu. Alongside it sits a clear bonito-and-kombu chintan ("Sen no Shio") that leans into Kyoto's love of delicate dashi, so the shop straddles milky comfort and crystalline restraint rather than belonging to any one regional lineage.

name: 篠田屋 (Shinodaya)

noodles.notes: Custom-ordered (tokuchu) thin, straight noodles with moderate firmness; finished over the broth pot so they reach the bowl piping hot.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (中華そば) — the old-school clear chicken-bone soy noodle bowl that, alongside the curry-ankake 皿盛, is one of the shop's two signature plates.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a soy-based tare put gluten in the bowl; no rice- or konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented at this classic shokudo.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a chicken-bone broth with a soy tare; no plant-only (kombu/shiitake/veg) broth is offered, so it is not vegan.

ramen.pantheon: A bowl frozen in time: this is the gentle, crystal-clear chicken-gara soy "Chinese noodle" of a century-old Kyoto town shokudo, the kind of unfussy chuka soba that has all but vanished elsewhere. You taste the lineage of pre-war shop-style ramen in its quiet, dashi-forward soup — simplicity, not richness, is the point.

name: 山の家 (Yamanoya)

noodles.notes: Firm-cooked Chinese-style wheat noodles (固めの中華麺) with a crisp bite and chewy mochi-like texture; the noodles carry real flavor of their own.

ramen.signature_bowl: 中華そば (Chuka Soba) — the classic clear shoyu Chinese-noodle soup, finished with a generous shower of black pepper at service.
ramen.chashu: Thin-sliced braised pork chashu (roasted/braised loin-style), in the modest old-school shokudo manner.

gf.note: Chinese-style wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-based (wheat) shoyu tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or GF option is documented at this traditional shokudo.

vegan.note: Traditional Showa-era shokudo broth and shoyu tare with pork chashu, fish-cake kamaboko, and animal-stock soup; no documented plant-based bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A bowl of pure Showa nostalgia: this is old-Kyoto machi-chuka soba — a clear, soy-tinged soup that is gentle yet quietly deep, the kind you could eat every day. The fish-cake kamaboko alongside the sprouts and pork marks it as a neighborhood shokudo's chuka soba rather than a specialist's ramen, and the heavy crack of black pepper at the finish is its signature flourish.

name: 四川亭 (Shisentei)

noodles.notes: Straight medium-thin (中細) noodles sourced from the Teigaku (定額) noodle maker, a supplier used by several well-regarded Kyoto shops; they cling well to the sesame-rich sauce. In the soup-less version the noodles are dressed rather than soaked.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shiru-nashi tantanmen (汁なし担担麺) — soup-less dandan noodles piled with green scallion and a snow of huājiāo/sansho over chunky minced meat, with a notably spicier sesame sauce than the soupy bowl; the brothy 担々麺 with a creamy sesame Chinese soup is the other house pillar. Selectable spice levels (up to five).
notable_toppings: bok choy (chingensai, on the soupy bowl)
