gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui Chinese noodles and a sesame/Chinese-soup tare built on standard seasonings; no rice- or konjac-noodle or documented gluten-free option. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Every bowl carries minced meat and a meat-forward Chinese broth/sesame tare; no plant-broth (kombu/shiitake) version is documented. The mince could in theory be omitted but the soup base would remain non-vegan — ask only if you must, expect no.

ramen.pantheon: A Sichuan-specialist's take on tantanmen rather than any Japanese gotōchi ramen: the soul here is a sesame-paste sauce — koku first, heat a beat behind — laid over a creamy Chinese soup, while the soup-less sibling trades broth for an addictive mince-and-scallion tangle finished with a numbing dusting of huājiāo. It tastes of Chengdu more than Kyoto, the màlà tingle balanced so it stirs appetite rather than scorches.

name: ラーメン 大栄 本店 (Ramen Daiei Honten)

noodles.notes: Medium-thick noodles with a smooth, springy bite (tsuru-tsuru, shiko-shiko) that cling to the broth. Most accounts describe a low-hydration straight noodle, though one report calls them slightly wavy; sourced from a local noodle maker rather than house-made.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chashu-men (チャーシュー麺) in shoyu, large size — a clear pork broth piled with thin-sliced pork heaped like a chashu-don. The miso version is the prized cold-weather alternative.
ramen.chashu: Loin pork simmered/braised nyu-buta style, sliced thin and stacked generously (10-plus slices on a chashu-men); tender and soft, soaking up the broth rather than charred or aburi.
notable_toppings: table condiments: chili-miso, grated garlic, white pepper

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles in a shoyu/miso tare that both carry wheat; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built purely from pork bone and pork meat, and every bowl is dressed with pork chashu. No plant-based broth or topping option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A Kyoto stalwart since the early 1980s, this bowl reads as the lighter, clearer face of the Kyoto pork tradition: a glistening, clean pork-bone soup whose soul is rendered meat rather than fat, finished with shoyu (or a tangy-sweet miso). You taste the lineage in a broth that drinks easy yet runs deep, crowned with a chashu-don's worth of pork and a tangle of local Kujo negi.

name: 中國菜 大鵬 (Chuugokusai Taihou)

noodles.notes: Springy, gently crimped medium noodles in the soup tantanmen with a bouncy puri-puri bite. The separate shiru-nashi (soupless) version is built on thin Kagawa-grown udon rather than ramen noodles, dressed in Sichuan chili and sansho.

ramen.signature_bowl: 担々麺 (tantanmen) — a sesame-rich, chicken-stock-based bowl with sansho-forward spiced minced pork and balanced chili oil; or the shiru-nashi (soupless) tantanmen on thin Kagawa udon.

gf.note: Tantanmen rides on wheat-kansui noodles and the soupless version on wheat udon, with soy and sesame-based seasonings throughout. No celiac-safe option is documented.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a chicken-stock base with spiced minced pork; no documented plant-broth, meat-free tantanmen. As a made-to-order Sichuan kitchen, worth asking, but nothing vegan is on record.

ramen.pantheon: This is tantanmen as told through a Sichuan kitchen rather than a ramen counter: a deeply sesame-laden, chicken-stock soup where the heat comes not from brute chili but from the citrusy, tongue-numbing tingle of sansho woven into the minced pork. It tastes of a half-century-old Kyoto machiya Chinese house that sources its spices from China itself, so the mala aroma reads authentic rather than tamed.

name: 西麻布カレーつけ麺 なんだっ亭 (Nishiazabu Curry Tsukemen Nandattei)

noodles.notes: Flat (hira-uchi) medium-thick noodles from the renowned Asakusa Kaikarou maker; slightly wavy with a chewy bite that grips and carries the creamy curry broth. On the thinner side for tsukemen.

ramen.signature_bowl: Curry tsukemen (カレーつけ麺) — flat Kaikarou noodles dipped in a mild, coconut-rounded beef-and-seafood curry broth; finish by adding the small rice (shime-meshi) into the leftover soup. Also offered as curry ramen (carry-rāmen) and curry abura-soba.
ramen.chashu: Thick slab of pork braised low and slow for about seven hours until meltingly tender — the bowl's standout, falling-apart soft.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a curry tare/roux; no rice-noodle or gluten-free option is documented. Curry roux itself is typically thickened with wheat flour.

vegan.note: Broth is built on beef cheek and seafood dashi, and the bowl centers braised pork chashu; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto specialist devoted to one idea — curry as ramen soul — where a beef-cheek stock is married to a fish-forward dashi and rounded with coconut milk into a light, fragrant, gently spiced curry. It owes nothing to the old gotōchi regional schools; the lineage you taste is the W-soup curry-tsukemen movement, finished by tipping rice into the bowl to chase every last drop.

name: 一盌 清右衛門 (Ichiwan Seiemon)

noodles.notes: Thin, lightly wavy noodles from a respected Kyoto noodle maker; good bite and a clear wheat flavor, sized to carry the delicate dashi-forward broth without overpowering it.

ramen.signature_bowl: 京醤油らぁ麺 (Kyo-shoyu raamen) — the Kyoto soy-sauce bowl: a clear, gentle chicken-and-dashi broth seasoned with locally brewed Kyoto soy sauce.
ramen.chashu: Pork chashu from Mie-raised Iga-mochi pork, braised with washoku technique (cooked with Japanese tea to tame any porky odor) for moist, tender, refined slices.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a soy-sauce (wheat) tare; no rice/konjac noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The broth is built on chicken and fish dashi (mackerel/bonito and kombu) with pork chashu; no plant-only bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: Built by a former kaiseki chef, this bowl treats ramen like a clear Japanese soup: chicken broth married to bonito-mackerel-kombu dashi and seasoned with gentle, locally brewed Kyoto soy sauce at roughly half the usual saltiness. It sits apart from Kyoto's famous heavy se-abura shoyu lineage — a delicate, dashi-forward original that you taste as restraint and clean umami rather than fat.

name: 京都担担麺 金鵄楼 本店 (Kyoto Tantanmen Kinshiro Honten)

noodles.notes: Handmade thick, irregularly crimped flat noodles sourced from Sano in Tochigi Prefecture — unusual for Kyoto tantanmen, which typically uses thin noodles. Chewy and springy (mochi-mochi) with a rough, textured surface that grips the creamy sesame soup and the minced-meat sauce. Best eaten at normal firmness rather than hard.

ramen.signature_bowl: 汁あり担担麺 (shiru-ari / soup tantanmen) — the rich creamy sesame bowl. The shiru-nashi (soupless) version is the alternative.
notable_toppings: finely ground pork niku-miso (minced-meat sauce pooled at the bottom of the bowl)

gf.note: Handmade wheat-and-kansui noodles and a sesame-based tantanmen seasoning make this unsafe for celiacs; no rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The bowl is built on creamy sesame (and likely soy milk) rather than an obvious fish or bone stock, and aromatics/chili/negi/niku-miso are customizable on an order sheet — but the meat sauce is ground pork and no plant-only broth is documented. You would have to ask whether they can omit the meat and confirm the soup base contains no animal stock; treat as not confirmed.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto take on Sichuan-style tantanmen built on a pale, lusciously creamy sesame broth — silky enough that regulars suspect soy milk — rather than the chili-soaked heat many expect. The thick, handmade crimped Sano noodles are a deliberate break from Kyoto's usual thin tantanmen strands, and you tune the rayu and sansho yourself, so the sesame koku stays the soul and the spice rides on top.

name: 盛華亭 (Seikatei)

noodles.notes: No ramen noodles are served. The only noodle dish is gomoku yakisoba — thin, crisp pan-fried noodles topped with a vegetable-rich ankake (starch-thickened) sauce, not a soup bowl. No documented soup-ramen noodle exists here.

ramen.signature_bowl: This is a Kyoto-style Beijing-cuisine town-Chinese restaurant, not a ramen shop — it serves no ramen bowl. The kitchen's heart is its gentle, vegetable-forward cooking: the closest noodle dish is gomoku yakisoba (crisp fried noodles under a light vegetable ankake). Other signatures are the homemade-wrapper sesame gyoza, kashiwa (chicken) karaage, harumaki spring rolls, kurozu su-buta, and gomoku fried rice.
notable_toppings: gomoku (mixed vegetable) ankake on the yakisoba

gf.note: A wheat-based town-Chinese kitchen — fried noodles, gyoza/spring-roll wrappers and soy-based seasonings all contain wheat. No gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented.

vegan.note: Vegetable-forward Kyoto-style cooking with restrained oil, but soups lean on chicken bone and most dishes use animal protein or soy seasonings; no certified plant-only broth or dish is documented. A diner would need to ask which vegetable preparations can be made without meat/dashi.

ramen.pantheon: Not a ramen house at all but a quiet residential-Kyoto town-Chinese kitchen practicing kyo-fu chuka — Beijing cookery softened by Kyoto restraint, where oil is held back and salt-and-sake seasoning lets vegetables speak. If you came for ramen you have walked into the wrong genre; what is poured here are clear Chinese soups and an ankake fried-noodle plate, not a tare-and-broth ramen bowl.

name: 中華のサカイ 本店 (Chuka no Sakai Honten)

noodles.notes: The hot 中華そば uses a plain, everyday yellow Chinese noodle that suits the nostalgic broth — distinct from the shop's famous custom-made reimen noodle (a thick, chewy, faintly gardenia-yellow specialty strand). Exact thickness/shape of the chuka-soba noodle is lightly documented.

ramen.signature_bowl: The house icon is the year-round 焼豚冷麺 (yakibuta reimen) — chilled noodles under a creamy, tangy mustard-vinegar-and-broth dressing. For a hot bowl, order the 中華そば: a clear, gentle chicken-gara shoyu Kyoto machi-chuka soba.
ramen.chashu: House roasted pork (yakibuta), the same sliced pork that crowns the famous reimen — lean, savory roast pork rather than a fatty rolled belly braise.

gf.note: Wheat-kansui Chinese noodles and a soy-based shoyu/sauce throughout; no rice- or konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Chicken-bone broth, roasted pork, and (on the reimen) a homemade egg mayonnaise dressing are core to the dishes; no documented plant-only broth or topping set.

ramen.pantheon: A Showa-era Kyoto machi-chuka institution open since 1939, where the chuka soba is the old-school clear chicken-gara shoyu broth that locals call simply "Chinese noodles" — light, faintly sweet, and deeply nostalgic rather than modern or assertive. Its true claim to fame, though, is the chilled noodle (reimen) it has served year-round since the 1950s, draped in a creamy mustard-vinegar dressing found nowhere else.

name: にぼ魂ラーメン 祇園本店 (Nibodamashii Ramen Gion Honten)

noodles.notes: Thick, flat, crinkled noodles (太麺の平打ち縮れ麺) that grab and carry the bitter niboshi soup. Free large-portion (omori) upgrade offered.

ramen.signature_bowl: Special Nibodamashii Ramen (特製にぼ魂ラーメン) — the niboshi soba dressed with rare marbled chashu, ajitama, and nori (around 1,200 yen). The plain Nibodamashii Ramen runs about 1,000 yen.
ramen.chashu: Rare-style chashu (レアチャーシュー) cut with moderate fat marbling, springy and tender rather than fall-apart braised — a low-temp pork slice that stays pink and juicy.
notable_toppings: ajitama (7-min soft-boiled flavored egg, runny yolk)

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a shoyu-style tare; no rice-noodle, konjac, or gluten-free option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The entire bowl is built on a dried-sardine (niboshi) fish broth and topped with pork chashu and egg. No plant-based broth or vegan bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A devout niboshi-soba that lets the dried sardines speak in full voice — the soup carries the deliberate, unmasked bitterness of pure niboshi rather than smoothing it away, the hallmark of the hardcore dried-sardine school. Set just off Gion's lantern-lit lanes and open till dawn, it's a clean, sardine-forward clear soup made to be the soulful finish to a Kyoto night.

name: 元祖らーめん大栄 (Ganso Ramen Daiei)

noodles.notes: Custom-made thin noodles from a dedicated noodle maker (Kondo Seimen), matched to the clear broth for smooth texture and good soup pickup.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chashu-men (shoyu) — the clear pork-bone soy bowl piled with thin-sliced Iga-mochibuta pork loin chashu.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork loin from Iga-mochibuta brand pork: blocks seared hard over high heat to render off excess fat and odor, then rested in a long-maintained house soy tare, sliced thin to order and heaped generously over the bowl.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-sauce (wheat) tare; no rice/konjac noodle or gluten-free tare documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built solely from pork and pork bones, the tare and chashu glaze are pork/meat-based, and chashu tops the signature bowl; no plant-based broth offered.

ramen.pantheon: A local-favorite take on Kyoto's pork-bone shoyu lineage, but brewed clear rather than the city's heavy back-fat style: simmered only from fresh Kyoto pork and bones into a bright, odor-free soup where soy depth and clean pork umami carry the bowl. You taste old-Kyoto comfort without the grease — a refined, never-tiring version of the genre.

name: 東龍 北白川本店 (Tonryu Kitashirakawa Honten)

noodles.notes: House-made egg noodles (tamago chijiremen), curly and wavy to grip the thick creamy broth. The Taiwan-style bowl gets a flatter, slightly thicker handmade noodle.

ramen.signature_bowl: 東龍そば — a creamy, salt-seasoned pork-and-chicken-bone broth thickened with a long-simmered medley of vegetables (an early Kyoto example of the "veji-pota" vegetable-potage style), served with the house wavy egg noodles.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork belly cut from Kyotamba Kogen-buta (a Kyoto-brand highland pork) — soft, fatty rolled belly rather than lean loin.
notable_toppings: for the Taiwan-style bowl: minced spiced pork and bean sprouts

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui house egg noodles plus a salt tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or documented gluten-free option. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The signature broth is built on pork bone, chicken carcass and chicken feet (momiji), and bowls are dressed with pork chashu — no documented plant-only broth.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto original rather than a gotochi classic: where the old city tradition leans on glossy se-abura shoyu, Tonryu pours a porridge-thick, milky pork-chicken-and-vegetable shio broth that drinks almost like a savory potage — one of the area's pioneering "veji-pota" bowls. The vegetables don't just season the soup; simmered down with the bones they become its creamy body, so you taste the garden as much as the pig.

name: つけ麺 夢人 (Tsukemen Yumenchu)

noodles.notes: House-made in small daily batches from a proprietary flour blend with visible wheat germ flecks (the shop's motto is 'wheat impact'); angular extra-thick noodles with a smooth, chewy bite that stand up to the dip. Shop closes when the day's noodles sell out.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tsukemen — thick house-made noodles served with a hot, concentrated fish-and-pork-bone dipping broth. The most popular order is the Kogashi (scorched) tsukemen, finished with a fragrant burnt oil; the owner himself recommends the Gyokai Tantan tsukemen for a richer, sesame-deepened dip.
ramen.chashu: Two styles appear across the menu: thick, fork-tender braised slices that "melt like butter," and a moist low-temperature rare chashu (pork). Three generous slices come standard.

gf.note: Wheat is the whole point here — the house noodles are flour-based with kansui and the broth is finished with a soy-based tare. No rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of the dip is a fish-and-pork-bone broth simmered for days, with pork chashu and animal fats throughout. No plant-based broth or noodle is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A modern gyokai-tonkotsu tsukemen in the Tokyo-born thick-dip lineage, transplanted to a quiet Yamashina back street: days-simmered pork bones married to a wave of seafood umami, brightened by a whisper of citrus and acidity so the richness lands clean rather than leaden. You taste that fish-and-bone marriage in the dip, and the kitchen's obsession with its own germ-flecked noodles is what makes the bowl sing.

name: 油そば ねこまた (Abura Soba Nekomata)

noodles.notes: Flat, ribbon-like noodles compared to kishimen, with a notably chewy, mochi-mochi bite that grips the oil and tare when mixed.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shin-Ganso Abura Soba (original shoyu) — brothless flat noodles dressed in a soy-based tare and aromatic oil; mix thoroughly from the bottom so tare, oil, toppings and noodles unite. Spicy-miso and shrimp sea-salt versions also offered.
ramen.chashu: Cubed/diced pork chashu, cut into bite-sized pieces so it folds into the noodles as you mix.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-sauce tare make this brothless bowl unsafe for celiacs; no rice- or konjac-noodle alternative is documented.

vegan.note: Pork chashu and a bonito/fish-flavored oil and tare run through every variation; no documented plant-based bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A brothless abura soba shop billed as one of the Kansai region's first dedicated to the style: instead of soup, chewy flat kishimen-like noodles are tossed in a concentrated soy tare and savory oil, then sharpened by a scatter of cold raw onion. The soul lives in the mix rather than the simmer — Tokyo-born abura soba transplanted into Kyoto's temple district.

name: ラーメン藤 京都本店 (Ramen Fuji Kyoto Honten)

noodles.notes: Thin-to-medium straight, low-hydration noodles supplied by the shop's own affiliated noodle maker (Kondo Seimen). They drink up the soup, soften fairly quickly, and give off a wheaty aroma with a smooth finish.

ramen.signature_bowl: The standard Ramen (the classic bowl) — a clear pork-and-soy broth crowned with a generous mound of Kyoto kujo scallions, fresh bean sprouts, and several thin slices of pork.
ramen.chashu: Thin-sliced, lightly marbled pork (the same pork simmered to build the broth), laid on in many slim, soup-soaked sheets rather than thick blocks — tender and meant to be wrapped with the scallions and sprouts.

gf.note: Wheat-kansui noodles and a soy-based (wheat) tare; an animal pork broth. No rice/konjac noodle or gluten-free option is documented — not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth and tare are built on pork (and pork fat), with pork chashu standard. No plant-based broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A living piece of Kyoto's old-school ramen lineage since 1972: a clear pork-and-soy soup where the shoyu sings out front and a quiet sweetness of back-fat hums beneath, finished Kyoto-style with a mountain of kujo scallions and crisp sprouts. You taste the city's classic clear-tonkotsu-shoyu soul rather than the heavy, cloudy tonkotsu of Kyushu.

name: ラーメンとまぜそばのお店 Lee (Ramen to Mazesoba no Omise Lee)

noodles.notes: Noodles from the long-established Kyoto maker Teikaku (麺屋棣鄂). For mazesoba a distinctive Y-cross-section 'wing' noodle whose grooves grab the sauce; for ramen a medium-thick, slightly flat cut served firm (katame). Brisk, springy bite that gives the bowls real snap.

ramen.signature_bowl: Keema Curry Mazesoba (キーマカレーまぜそば) — brothless wing-noodles tossed with house keema curry, crowned with egg yolk, melting cheese, house-made bacon, back-fat and parsley, with finishing rice (shime-gohan) to mop up the leftover sauce. Garlic added to taste.
ramen.chashu: Shoulder-loin (kata-rosu) chashu, leaner and light in flavor with little fat; the curry mazesoba instead leans on house-made bacon and back-fat as its meaty heart.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen/mazesoba noodles plus shoyu-based sauces and curry roux; the curry mazesoba also carries bacon and dairy. No documented rice- or konjac-noodle or GF option — not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on chicken-pork or fish/niboshi stock with pork back-fat, bacon, chashu and egg yolk; the curry mazesoba adds cheese. No documented plant-based broth or bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto original rather than a regional throwback: born from a former curry shop, Lee fuses two worlds — a creamy, consommé-scented chicken-and-pork paitan and a punchy keema-curry mazesoba — so you taste the kitchen's curry roots in the bowl as much as any ramen lineage. A quieter chicken-niboshi soba rounds out the range with a sweeter shoyu kaeshi and an animal-fish balance for purists.

name: Nippon Ramen 凛 KYOTO (Nippon Ramen Rin Kyoto)

noodles.notes: Made fresh in-house daily from a blend mainly of Hokkaido wheat; prized for a smooth, slippery (tsuru-tsuru) texture that carries the clear chicken broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: 醤油らぁ麺 (shoyu ramen) — a crystal-clear chicken chintan with golden chicken oil; or the 醤油ちゃあしゅう麺 to feature both chashu. The shio version showcases the same clear broth with a lighter, more delicate seasoning.
ramen.chashu: Dual chashu: a moist, tender braised mochi-pork loin alongside a separate chicken chashu — lean and gentle rather than fatty belly.
notable_toppings: ajitama (Hinai-jidori soft-boiled egg, on wonton bowl)

gf.note: Noodles are handmade from Hokkaido wheat and the tare uses honjozo brewed soy sauce (wheat-based); standard bowls are not celiac-safe and no rice/konjac noodle or GF-tare option is documented.

vegan.note: The soul of every bowl is a clear soup built from premium jidori chicken (Hinai-jidori, Nagoya Cochin, etc.) with chicken oil and pork/chicken chashu; no plant-broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A transparent, golden-flecked bowl in the Sapporo-born tanrei (light-and-clear) lineage, transplanted to Kyoto: its soul is a luxurious chicken chintan layered from several heritage jidori breeds and seasoned with honjozo raw soy sauce, so you taste refinement and restraint rather than heaviness. This is connoisseur's chicken shoyu/shio — clarity, fragrance and depth held in perfect balance.

name: 麺屋 やまひで 京都聖護院店 (Menya Yamahide Kyoto Shogoin-ten)

noodles.notes: House-made medium-thick straight noodles with a pronounced wheat fragrance, strong koshi and a chewy, mochi-mochi bite engineered to grab and hold the thick mince-and-tare dressing.

ramen.signature_bowl: Taiwan Mazesoba (台湾まぜそば) — 200g of chewy house-made noodles dressed with spicy-savory ground-pork mince, a raw egg yolk at the center, garlic chives (nira), scallion, nori, fish powder and raw garlic; you mix it all vigorously, then finish the leftover sauce with the complimentary oimeshi rice.
ramen.chashu: No chashu — the protein heart is taiwan mince: a spicy-savory wok-fried ground-pork crumble seasoned with chili and garlic.

gf.note: The dish is built on wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a shoyu/fish-powder mince sauce; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented.

vegan.note: Core elements are pork mince, raw egg yolk and fish powder; no documented plant-based broth or tare version.

ramen.pantheon: A Nagoya-born Taiwan mazesoba: a soupless bowl where chewy house-made noodles are bound by a fiery garlic-and-chili pork mince, raw egg yolk, fish powder and garlic chives, all stirred together until the dressing coats every strand. The lineage shows in the punchy garlic-chili-gyofun trinity and the ritual oimeshi rice that mops up what the noodles leave behind.

name: つけ麺 京都わたなべ (Tsukemen Kyoto Watanabe)

noodles.notes: Three choices: 太麺 (thick, chewy/mochi-mochi), 中太麺 (slightly slimmer, smooth-slurping), and 平打ち太麺 (flat thick noodle with firm bite and wheat flavor). The thick 太麺 is the standard for the dip. The shop's former name was Watanabe Seimen (a noodle-maker), pointing to a noodle-forward identity.

ramen.signature_bowl: 濃厚魚介つけ麺 (Nōkō Gyokai Tsukemen) — thick noodles dipped in the concentrated pork-and-chicken-bone broth blended with seafood tare; the shop lets you pick noodle type and one of five dip blends (rich seafood, vegetable-potage, spicy minced-meat, curry, or shrimp), with rich seafood being the flagship.
ramen.chashu: 味付き焼豚 (seasoned braised/roast pork), served as several slices atop the noodles; can be upgraded with a chashu add-on. Exact cut/preparation not documented.

gf.note: Tsukemen noodles are wheat-and-kansui and the dip relies on a shoyu-style seafood tare over pork/chicken bone stock; no rice/konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The broth is built on pork-and-chicken bone stock plus seafood (gyokai) dashi, so the standard bowls are not vegan. A ベジポタ (vegetable-potage) dip blend exists, but it is still served over the same animal-based base unless modified — would need to ask whether a fully plant-based broth and noodle (no katsuo/pork) can be prepared; not documented as a true vegan offering.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto take on the rich gyokai-tonkotsu dipping noodle: pork and chicken bones simmered long into a thick, clingy broth, then married to a concentrated seafood tare so the dip coats every strand of the chewy house noodle. You taste the W-soup lineage of contemporary tsukemen — the deep animal body and the punch of dried fish working as one — with the playful twist that you choose your own noodle and dip blend.

name: 中国四川料理 駱駝 (Chugoku Shisen Ryori Rakuda)

noodles.notes: Chinese-style wheat noodles that the rich sesame-and-chili soup clings to well; exact gauge and hydration not documented.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tantanmen (担々麺) — a soup-style Sichuan dandan noodle in a rich sesame-paste broth fired with real chili and tongue-tingling huajiao (Sichuan pepper).

gf.note: Wheat noodles plus a sesame-and-soy-based Sichuan seasoning make this unsafe for celiacs; no gluten-free option is documented.

vegan.note: The tantanmen carries ground-pork niku-miso and a meat/chicken-stock-leaning broth typical of authentic Sichuan cooking; no plant-based version is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is tantanmen as cooked by a true Sichuan kitchen rather than a ramen shop — a deep, sesame-thick soup where the heat is not just hot but numbing, the genuine ma-la lift of huajiao and dried chili that defines Chengdu cooking. You taste the Sichuan lineage in every clinging, fragrant spoonful, a world apart from the milder Japanese tantanmen.

name: 中華そば らんたん (Chuka Soba Rantan)

noodles.notes: Custom-ordered chuhoso (medium-thin) noodles with whole-wheat flour in the blend; smooth, slurpable texture that pairs cleanly with the clear broth. The kombu-water tsukemen uses a separate specialty noodle with tapioca for extra chew.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tokusei jukusei shoyu chuka soba (special aged-soy chuka soba) — the flagship clear chicken-and-fish bowl; the aged-soy ginger version and the shellfish-dashi shio soba are the other pillars, with a katsuo-kombu-water tsukemen for dipping.
ramen.chashu: Two kinds: a large slice of low-temperature pork loin rare chashu, tender with a balance of fat and lean, plus a moist, gently poached domestic chicken-breast chashu.
notable_toppings: table katsuobushi / kombu vinegar / sansho / chili for self-seasoning

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus an aged-soy-sauce (wheat) tare run through every bowl; the whole-wheat custom noodles and tapioca tsukemen noodles are still wheat-based. No rice or konjac noodle option is documented — not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: All broths are built on chicken plus katsuo/niboshi/shellfish dashi, and bowls carry pork and chicken chashu; the shio uses scallop and clam. No documented plant-only (kombu/shiitake) bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A refined Kyoto take on the Japanese-style clear chuka soba, where you taste the lineage in a tanrei soup that marries domestic-chicken depth with katsuo and niboshi sea-umami, lifted by an aged-soy tare and a whisper of yuzu. It sits in the elegant, dashi-forward modern-classic camp rather than Kyoto's heavier se-abura tradition — clarity and aroma over fat.

name: 京都二条 麺屋 啜る (Kyoto Nijo Menya Susuru)

noodles.notes: Whole-grain (zenryūfun) noodles, cut slightly short, with a notably mochi-mochi chewy bite and a pronounced wheat fragrance; they grip the clear broth well.

ramen.signature_bowl: 熟成醤油らぁ麺 (Aged-Soy Shoyu Ramen) — a clear chicken-and-seafood dashi married to richly fragrant matured soy tare. The deluxe (tokusei) version layers on three kinds of chashu, an ajitama, and nori; partway through you can stir in a sachet of katsuobushi to lift the dashi aroma even further.
ramen.chashu: Three different chashu styles are served on the deluxe bowl; the heart is roast pork-style cuts (the kitchen also showcases A5 wagyu roast beef on its separate premium bowl, signaling a roast/low-temp approach rather than a heavy braise).
notable_toppings: side sachet of katsuobushi to finish

gf.note: Noodles are wheat-based (whole-grain flour + kansui) and the matured soy tare is wheat-derived; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: A dedicated ヴィーガン白湯らぁ麺 is built on a soymilk and aromatic-vegetable broth (no katsuo/pork), and a vegan tantanmen is also offered. Confirm the noodles and tare contain no animal/fish products when ordering.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto original rather than the city's classic back-fat-and-soy school: its soul is a crystal-clear chintan of domestic chicken bound to katsuobushi and niboshi, so you taste a delicate Japanese dashi lineage rather than a regional gotōchi style. Aged soy tare gives it depth and a soy-sauce perfume that lingers over the clean, layered umami.

name: 麺屋 一空 (Menya Ikku)

noodles.notes: Wheat-forward noodles that drink up the glossy broth. The paitan and clear bowls use straight medium-thickness noodles with a clean wheat aroma; the tsukemen and mazesoba switch to thick, high-hydration noodles with a gummy, elastic chew (roughly 200g) built to carry the concentrated dip.

ramen.signature_bowl: Noukou tori paitan ramen (rich chicken white soup), available in shoyu or shio — the milky bowl with chicken-fat and back-fat sheen is the shop's heart. Clear chicken shoyu/shio bowls are the lighter alternative.
ramen.chashu: Two kinds in one bowl: pork shoulder-loin cooked low-and-slow (around 36 hours) into a moist rare slice, and tender low-temperature chicken breast. The pork is juicy with soft fat; both are served rare rather than braised-dark.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus shoyu-based tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of every bowl is an 8-hour chicken (Nagoya Cochin and other local breeds) paitan finished with chicken fat and pork back-fat, topped with pork and chicken chashu. No plant-broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto tori-paitan specialist that simmers Nagoya Cochin and local chickens for eight hours into a milky, concentrated soup laced with chicken fat, back-fat, and a whisper of garlic and ginger — you taste the whole bird in the bowl. It plays both sides of the chicken spectrum, offering the same poultry pulled clear into a delicate chintan shoyu or shio when you want lighter.

name: 麺屋 聖 ～kiyo～ (Menya Kiyo (Ichijoji))

noodles.notes: House-made daily on-site, blended with whole-wheat flour for a chewy, springy (mochi-mochi) bite with good body; medium thickness with a gentle curl that carries the clear seafood-shoyu broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Gift (ギフト) — the mellow shoyu chuka soba built on a clear clam (asari) and flying-fish (tobiuo) dashi over Kyoto-style soy tare
ramen.chashu: Extra-thick (about 2cm) slow-braised pork belly, simmered over two hours until tender and juicy; cut to your preferred thickness at the counter.

gf.note: House-made wheat noodles (with whole-wheat flour) plus a wheat-based soy tare — not celiac-safe. No rice/konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented.

vegan.note: The broth is built on clam and flying-fish seafood dashi and finished with pork fat, with braised pork-belly chashu. No plant-based broth or topping option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A refined, seafood-forward take on Kyoto chuka soba: instead of the city's classic heavy se-abura shoyu, this bowl pulls a clear, golden broth whose soul is the gentle sweetness of clams (asari) braced with flying fish, then rounded with Kyoto soy and a whisper of pork fat. Elegant and tanrei rather than rustic — a modern original standing apart on Ichijoji's famously hard-fought ramen street.

name: 名前のないラーメン屋 (Namae no Nai Ramen-ya)

noodles.notes: Whole-grain (zenryufun) noodles with a fragrant wheat aroma and firm, satisfying chew; the kitchen is known for working organic burdock (gobo) into the dough. Straight cut that clings well to the thick rich broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: The rich (noko) seafood-chicken paitan ramen, or the spice-laced curry tsukemen — both crowned with luxurious grilled motsu. The shop runs a modular build-your-own system: pick light (tanrei) clear chicken, rich, or blended (juso) broth, then a noodle format and a protein (motsu, chashu, or wagyu).
ramen.chashu: Standard option is a clean, reliable braised pork chashu, but the shop's signature heart is generously piled grilled motsu (pork/beef giblets) — torched for melting texture, sweetness, and no funk; wagyu beef is also a selectable protein.
notable_toppings: rice + grated cheese risotto finish for tsukemen

gf.note: Whole-grain wheat noodles with kansui and a wheat-based shoyu seasoning; nothing rice- or konjac-based is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broths are built on chicken bones and seafood dashi, and proteins are motsu, chashu, or wagyu. No documented plant-only kombu/shiitake/vegetable bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A nameless, signless basement hideaway in Kyoto's Kiyamachi-Sanjo that plays in the modern-original key rather than any gotochi lineage: its soul is a glossy, deeply emulsified seafood-and-chicken paitan whose dashi depth you taste in every clinging spoonful. The luxury of torched giblets, a curry-tsukemen with a cheese-risotto finish, and burdock-laced whole-grain noodles mark it as a chef-driven Kyoto original (a sibling of the Takakura-Nijo / Sugari kitchens), not a regional revival.

name: 広東御料理 竹香 (Kanton Goryori Takeka)

noodles.notes: Not a ramen shop. This is a long-established Cantonese restaurant whose noodle offerings are Chinese-style dishes, not ramen: yakibuta-iri soba (Chinese chuka-men with roast pork), yakisoba (stir-fried noodles), seasonal hiyashi soba (chilled), and a winter gomoku ankake soba (noodles under a thickened vegetable-and-pork gravy with bamboo shoot and cabbage). No noodle shape/thickness/hydration is documented.

ramen.signature_bowl: No ramen is served. The closest noodle dishes are Cantonese-style preparations such as gomoku ankake soba (noodles napped in a clear, gently thickened vegetable-and-pork gravy) and yakibuta soba (roast-pork noodles). The house signature dishes are non-noodle Cantonese plates.

gf.note: Noodle dishes use wheat-based Chinese noodles and standard sauces; no gluten-free or rice/konjac noodle option is documented. Not a setting built for celiac-safe dining.

vegan.note: A Cantonese house known for deliberately omitting garlic and lard and for light, vegetable-forward Kyoto-style cooking; some vegetable dishes may suit, but noodle gravies typically include pork and the kitchen does not document a verified plant-only broth, so confirm directly.

ramen.pantheon: This is not a ramen house but a long-standing Gion Cantonese kitchen, and its noodles reflect that lineage: gentle, clear-tasting Kyoto-Chinese cooking that famously forgoes garlic and lard, so its soba arrive under a light ankake gravy rather than in a tonkotsu or shoyu ramen broth. For ramen as a genre, this address is out of scope.

name: ラー麺 陽はまた昇る 伏見稲荷駅前本店 (Ra-men Hi wa Mata Noboru Fushimi Inari Ekima)

noodles.notes: House-made noodles from domestic wheat, with a pronounced wheat fragrance and a firm, chewy bite that clings to the thick broth. Most accounts describe a thin-to-medium straight cut for the standard bowls; the tsukemen is served with thicker, heartier house noodles.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tori-tonkotsu ramen (とりとんこつ) — the house double-soup of chicken bones and pork bones simmered long and hard into a thick, creamy bowl.
ramen.chashu: Black-pepper-rubbed low-temperature rare pork chashu — lean rather than fatty, the cured pepper crust framing concentrated, tightly-packed pork flavor in tender rosy slices.

gf.note: Wheat house-made noodles plus a brewed-soy-sauce tare; no rice/konjac noodle or gluten-free option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on chicken-and-pork bone or beef-bone broth (the fish variant adds katsuo/seafood dashi) with animal-protein chashu. No plant-based broth is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto chicken-pork paitan: chicken and pork bones cooked for hours into one velvety, thick soup where neither animal dominates, yet it lands clean rather than cloying. It sits in the contemporary tori-tonkotsu lineage rather than old-school Kyoto se-abura shoyu — and a fish-laced variant lets you taste a seafood-deepened version of the same soul.

name: 京都中華 ハマムラ 府庁前店 (Kyoto Chuka Hamamura (Fuchomae))

noodles.notes: Thin straight noodles served under a thick ankake (starch-bound) sauce rather than in a loose soup; one source notes the noodles are seared on one side before the sauce is ladled over them.

ramen.signature_bowl: Karashi soba (からしそば) — thin straight noodles bound in a light, glossy ankake sauce over chicken-and-kombu stock, loaded with pork and vegetables and finished with a fierce sheet of hot Japanese mustard (karashi). A Kyoto-Chinese specialty served as a dinner item; an evening-only offering at this location.
ramen.chashu: No braised or grilled chashu in the ramen sense; the protein is sliced pork simmered into the ankake topping alongside the vegetables.

gf.note: Wheat noodles plus a soy-based, starch-thickened ankake sauce; nothing rice- or konjac-noodle and no GF tare is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The signature bowl is built on chicken-gara stock with sliced pork in the ankake; no documented kombu/shiitake plant-only broth bowl. The kitchen does run a vegetable-forward, oil-controlled style, so a modified veg dish could be worth asking about, but nothing vegan is confirmed.

ramen.pantheon: This is Kyoto-Chinese, not gotochi ramen: a house that has cooked the genteel, oil-restrained Chinese of the old geisha quarters since 1924, where chicken and kombu do the work that garlic and lard do elsewhere. Its karashi soba is the soul of that lineage — a delicate, almost dashi-clear ankake draped over thin noodles, then jolted awake by a roaring hit of hot mustard, refinement and shock in one bowl.

name: ラーメン また此処で (Ramen Mata Koko de)

noodles.notes: Extra-thick, wavy self-made noodles with a strong wheat aroma and substantial chew; ~300g in the regular size. Slurpable rather than punishingly firm despite the bulk.

ramen.signature_bowl: Ramen (並ラーメン) - 300g of thick wavy noodles in an emulsified sweet pork-bone shoyu broth, crowned with the Jiro mountain of bean sprouts and cabbage, back-fat (seabura), and grated garlic. Call your toppings at the counter (mashi for extra veg/fat/garlic). A tsukemen version is also offered.
ramen.chashu: Thick, melt-in-the-mouth braised pork (buta), served 2 slices standard with a 5-slice option; tender, gelatinous edges in the Jiro tradition.

gf.note: Jiro-style bowls are built on wheat: thick wheat-and-kansui noodles and a wheat-based shoyu tare over a pork broth. No rice/konjac noodle or GF option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Core of the bowl is a pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth with pork back-fat and braised pork; shoyu tare and pork are pervasive. No plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A faithful piece of the Ramen Jiro universe transplanted to Kyoto by a chef who spent over a decade in a celebrated Jiro-style kitchen: a sweet, emulsified pork-bone soup buried under a mountain of bean sprouts, cabbage, back-fat and garlic. What sets it apart is a distinctly Kyoto restraint in the shoyu seasoning, dialing back the usual Jiro saltiness so the broth's natural pork sweetness leads.

name: あぶらそば 油珉 (Abura Soba Yumin)

noodles.notes: House-made noodles from 100% domestic wheat, mochi-mochi chewy with a springy bite. The standard abura soba and spicy version share a common house noodle; the back-fat vegetable bowl uses a separate extra-thick, wavy, jiro-style noodle with a wari-wari chew. Base portion around 180g, scalable up to roughly 540g.

ramen.signature_bowl: 背脂ヤサイあぶらそば (Se-abura Yasai Abura Soba) — the brothless back-fat vegetable bowl on the thick jiro-style noodle; the shoyu-leaning standard あぶらそば is the lighter all-rounder.
ramen.chashu: A single thick-cut slab of tender braised pork chashu tops the signature vegetable bowl.

gf.note: Brothless abura soba built on wheat-and-kansui house noodles with a shoyu-forward (wheat) tare; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Bowls are dressed with pork back fat, braised pork chashu and a savory shoyu tare; no documented plant-only build. The vegetable variant still rides on back fat and meat, so it is not vegan.

ramen.pantheon: A brothless Kyoto abura soba where the soul lives in the tare-slicked noodle rather than a soup: a shoyu-forward sauce with a faintly creamy sweetness, finished tableside with vinegar, garlic and chili oil. Its flagship leans into the city's back-fat heritage and a jiro-style mountain of cabbage, sprouts and lard on a thick wavy noodle — a "soupless Jiro shio" you mix, not sip.

name: 台湾まぜそば専門店 脳天喜。 (Taiwan Mazesoba Senmonten Noutenki (Kyoto Me)

noodles.notes: Two noodle choices: a chunky medium-thick noodle blending whole-grain and wheat flour, or a rippled "wing" noodle (from a renowned Kyoto noodle maker) whose wavy surface grips the spicy mince and sauce. The wing noodle is the popular pick for how well the ingredients cling.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tonporo Taiwan Mazesoba (東坡肉台湾まぜそば) — brothless mixed noodles with spicy Taiwan mince, crowned with a slab of melting braised pork belly. Mix everything, then finish with a rice ball (追い飯) dunked into the leftover sauce.
ramen.chashu: The heart is not a typical ramen chashu but Chinese-style 東坡肉 (Dongpo/tonporo) braised pork belly — slow-cooked by a Chinese chef until quivering-tender, sweet-savory, with the star anise kept gentle for the Japanese palate. A diced-pork rice bowl and ajitama-chashu version are also offered.
notable_toppings: 10 table condiments incl. Aomori garlic-vinegar and niboshi-vinegar

gf.note: Both noodle options are wheat-based (whole-grain/wheat blend and wing noodle), the Taiwan mince and tare carry soy/wheat seasoning, and the rice finisher shares the same sauce. No gluten-free noodle or tare is documented.

vegan.note: Core dish is built on pork mince, braised pork belly, raw egg yolk and fish powder; the side ramen is chicken paitan. No documented plant-based broth or topping set.

ramen.pantheon: A Kyoto temple to Nagoya-born Taiwan mazesoba — the brothless, garlic-and-chili mince bowl you toss together yourself — but with a twist: a Chinese chef tops it with quivering Dongpo braised pork belly instead of the usual char siu. Choose your noodle, mix in the raw yolk, then dunk a rice ball into the lingering spicy-savory sauce for the classic two-act finish.

name: 力餅食堂 柿仲 (Chikara Mochi Shokudo Kakichu)

noodles.notes: Thin, straight noodles that slide smoothly and easily into the mouth — light and unfussy, matched to the delicate broth rather than a chewy modern ramen noodle.

ramen.signature_bowl: 中華そば (chuka soba) — the house bowl: a pale, gentle light-shoyu soup with thin straight noodles, served the old-shokudo way for under 1000 yen.
ramen.chashu: Sliced braised pork chashu, cut surprisingly thick for such a light bowl — a homely, tender slice rather than an aburi/char-grilled or melt-and-collapse style.

gf.note: Standard wheat-and-kansui chuka noodles in a soy-sauce (wheat) broth; this is a traditional town shokudo with no documented rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option.

vegan.note: The bowl is built on an animal/dashi base, soy tare, fish-cake kamaboko and pork chashu; no plant-broth (kombu/shiitake/veg) version is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is chuka soba in its most nostalgic, home-cooked form: the kind of pale, gentle light-shoyu bowl an old Kyoto neighborhood eatery has ladled out for generations, closer in spirit to a clear udon-soba broth than to any modern ramen specialist. Its soul is restraint — a clean, kind soup you could eat every day without tiring of it, dressed with two slices of kamaboko, menma and bright Kujo negi.

name: 中華そば 六 (Chuka Soba Roku)

noodles.notes: Thin, straight custom-made noodles with a firm bite, chosen to define against the silky clear broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (中華そば) — a crystal-clear duck-forward chukasoba built on a five-bone stock; optionally finished with the table-side trio of pork-and-water-chestnut wontons.
ramen.chashu: Pork chashu cooked low in a convection oven to stay moist and juicy rather than braised soft, served refined and clean to match the delicate soup.
notable_toppings: side wontons (pork, onion, water chestnut, celery)

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles and a shoyu-style tare; no rice/konjac noodle or gluten-free seasoning is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is drawn from duck, chicken, venison, beef and pork bones with animal-fat finishing oil and pork chashu; no plant-only bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A chef-driven, modern-original take on chukasoba: instead of one signature bone, the soup is a layered consommé pulled from duck, chicken, venison, beef and pork and rounded with porcini, dried shiitake, tomato and longan, so you taste depth without weight. It reads less like a regional gotochi ramen and more like a refined-Chinese-kitchen interpretation of the clear-soup Kyoto bowl, where finesse and aromatics carry the lineage.

name: 京・清水 油そば ねこまた (Kyo-Kiyomizu Abura Soba Nekomata)

noodles.notes: Flat, wide kishimen-like noodles with a chewy mochimochi bite, built to grab and hold the tare and oil pooled at the bottom of the bowl. You mix vigorously from the bottom so noodles, oil, tare, and toppings become one.

ramen.signature_bowl: Ganso (original) shoyu abura soba — the brothless house bowl: thick flat noodles dressed in sesame oil and a soy-based tare, finished at the table with chili oil and vinegar to taste. Spicy-miso and ebi (shrimp) shio versions are the other two pillars.
ramen.chashu: Cubed/diced roasted pork tossed through the noodles rather than laid as sheets, so it folds into each bite as you mix.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-soy tare; abura soba is fundamentally a wheat dish with no documented rice/konjac-noodle or GF-tare option.

vegan.note: Pork chashu, soy-based tare and likely fish-derived seasoning run through every bowl; no documented plant-based version. The ebi (shrimp) and naruto fish cake further rule it out.

ramen.pantheon: A brothless bowl in the Tokyo-born abura soba lineage, set down in the lanes below Kiyomizu: there is no soup, so the soul lives in the marriage of nutty sesame oil and a punchy soy tare clinging to thick, chewy flat noodles. You build the flavor yourself with chili oil and a splash of vinegar, and the dish reveals itself only after a hard mix from the bottom.

name: コムギノキラメキ 上桂前田町本店 (Komugi no Kirameki Kamikatsura Maedacho Hont)

noodles.notes: Made fresh in-house at a glassed-in noodle factory using a blend of Hokkaido/Nagano/Australian wheat. The chicken paitan ramen gets medium-thick noodles with firm koshi to stand up to the rich broth; the Taiwan mazesoba and Jiro-style 小麦のチカラ use very thick (around 3mm), short-cut straight noodles with a dense chew.

ramen.signature_bowl: 鶏白湯らーめん (chicken paitan ramen) — order it shoyu to show off the concentrated chicken; the Taiwan mazesoba (台湾まぜそば) is the co-flagship, with the Jiro-style 小麦のチカラ for big appetites.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork chashu in large slices that drape across the bowl; the thick-cut tender, fatty pork belly piled on the 小麦のチカラ mazesoba is the bolder, fattier version.

gf.note: Wheat-based handmade noodles (kansui) are the house specialty and a shoyu tare is used; nothing rice/konjac-based is offered, so not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is simmered chicken, topped with pork chashu (and ground pork on the mazesoba); no documented plant-based broth or topping option.

ramen.pantheon: A tori-paitan house in the modern Kyoto idiom: the soup is built from chicken alone, simmered long into a milky, umami-dense bowl that still drinks surprisingly clean, dressed with deliberate restraint so the bird stays center stage. Alongside it sits a thick, chewy Taiwan mazesoba and a Jiro-leaning, back-fat-heavy noodle plate, all riding house-milled, in-house-cut noodles.

name: 中華 六六 (Chuka Rokuroku)

noodles.notes: Medium-thick, straight, translucent noodles of the old-school machi-chuka type — soft and not the focus of the bowl; the soup and toppings carry it.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (中華そば) — the house clear soy-sauce noodle bowl
ramen.chashu: A thick, single cut of braised pork chashu with a notably good lean-to-fat balance.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-sauce (wheat) tare in a townhouse Chinese kitchen with no documented rice/konjac-noodle or GF option — not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: A meat-stock soy-sauce broth with pork chashu and no documented plant-based bowl; not vegan.

ramen.pantheon: This is a town-Chinese chuka soba rather than a specialist's ramen: a clear, gently sweet soy-sauce soup whose soul is the perfume of scallion and the mellow sweetness of slow-cooked onion, served the old machiya way. It leans nostalgic and homey — light and easy-drinking, defined more by its tender housemade-style menma and a generous slab of chashu than by any regional ramen lineage.

name: キラメキノトリ (Kirameki no Tori)

noodles.notes: House-made noodles from the group's own noodle factory using 100% Hokkaido wheat; medium-thick straight cut with a chewy, mochi-mochi bite that clings to the creamy broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tori paitan ramen, shio version (the salt tare best showcases the chicken umami, finished with lemon); the night-only Taiwan mazesoba is the other house specialty.
ramen.chashu: Chicken thigh chashu — tender and moist, served with a seared skin for a bit of char and bite (no pork in the signature bowl).

gf.note: Noodles are wheat (100% Hokkaido wheat) plus kansui, and the shoyu/miso tare add wheat; no rice- or konjac-noodle or GF option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a long-simmered chicken paitan with chicken chashu and chicken fat; no plant-based broth or vegan bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern tori-paitan specialist whose soul is a creamy, deeply emulsified chicken broth simmered to a milky richness yet finished clean enough to drink to the bottom. Order it shio with a squeeze of lemon and you taste the lineage of the contemporary chicken-paitan movement rather than any one regional tradition — pure poultry umami carried by chewy Hokkaido-wheat noodles and a skin-seared chicken chashu.

name: 御旅飯店 (Otabi Hanten)

noodles.notes: Standard old-school 中華そば noodles in the neighborhood-Chinese idiom; specific gauge, shape and hydration are not documented.

ramen.signature_bowl: 中華そば (the house Chinese soba) — a chicken-bone shoyu bowl; note the shop's true headliner is actually the 御旅丼 rice bowl, with the noodle dishes a beloved sideline. Also offered as a mini-ramen add-on (+¥200) to a rice set.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles and a wheat-based soy tare; no rice/konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is documented at this neighborhood Chinese diner. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The bowl is built on a chicken-bone broth with a soy tare; no plant-based (kombu/shiitake/vegetable) broth option is documented. Not vegan.

ramen.pantheon: An everyday Kyoto 町中華 (neighborhood Chinese) bowl rather than a destination ramen: a clear chicken-bone shoyu soup, seasoned bold and on the stronger side, in the homey Showa-era 中華そば tradition you find in the back-streets near Funaokayama and Daitoku-ji. You taste the lineage of the all-purpose Chinese kitchen — the same stock that anchors its famous rice bowls — rather than a single regional school.

name: いいちょ ラーメン (Iicho Ramen)

noodles.notes: Thin, straight noodles from a local Kyoto noodle maker, with a slightly soft, traditional mid-century bite (細めのストレート). Made by an outside noodle factory, not in-house.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shoyu chuka soba (醤油ラーメン) — the chicken-and-pork blended soy-sauce bowl; add chashu for the chashu-men, and pair with the shop's signature fried rice (yakimeshi) as a set.
ramen.chashu: Thin-sliced pork chashu braised for about half a day in the shop's own Sawai soy-sauce tare before serving (薄切りチャーシュー).

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based soy-sauce (shoyu) tare; no rice/konjac noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken and pork bones with a soy tare and pork back-fat, and chashu tops the bowl. No plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A traditional Kyoto-style soy bowl that carries the lineage of the long-gone Iwakura ramen: a chicken-and-pork blend simmered to a faintly clouded, deeply chicken-scented broth, seasoned with a punchy artisanal Kyoto soy sauce and finished with melting back-fat. You taste the old Rakuhoku neighborhoods in every sip — comfort-food shoyu rather than flashy modern ramen.

name: 麺屋 誉 (Menya Homare)

noodles.notes: Custom noodles from the venerable Kyoto maker Teigaku (棣鄂), founded 1931 — medium-thick, mostly straight with a very subtle wave, cut slightly thinner than typical Hakata noodles. Springy, chewy and built for slurping, with enough body to stand up to the heavy soup. Firmness is adjustable to order.

ramen.signature_bowl: 特製濃厚豚骨醤油ラーメン (Tokusei rich tonkotsu-shoyu ramen) — the deluxe bowl loaded with both chashu styles, an ajitama, greens and nori.
ramen.chashu: A two-style pairing: smoky low-temperature braised pork with deep savory richness, plus a pastrami-style cured loin crowned with cracked black pepper — both cooked sous-vide-tender.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-sauce (wheat) tare over a pork-and-chicken broth; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a long-simmered pork-bone and chicken broth dressed with shoyu tare, pork back-fat/chicken oil and pork chashu — animal-derived throughout. No plant-based broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A thick, velvety pork-and-chicken paitan lacquered with a bold soy tare — this bowl leans into the Yokohama Iekei spirit of muscular tonkotsu-shoyu, but pushes the richness and salt-forward punch harder than Kyoto's gentler local style. You taste the deep, faintly sweet collagen of slow-rendered pork bones, sharpened by a generous soy backbone and a table-side hit of grated garlic and niboshi vinegar.

name: 和麺ぐり虎 京都本店 (Wamen Guriko Kyoto Honten)

noodles.notes: Special noodle of 100% domestic wheat; medium-thin straight cut renewed in April 2025 to be slightly thicker for a more satisfying bite. Notable for a pronounced wheat aroma, supple texture, firm clean bite and smooth throat-feel.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tori-shio ramen (chicken-salt) — the house signature; also celebrated for the tai-shio ramen made with whole domestic wild red sea bream. Order the 特製 (special) version to get the full topping set.
ramen.chashu: Low-temperature-style braised pork chashu, moist and tender, releasing more meat umami the longer you chew.

gf.note: Wheat noodles are 100% domestic wheat plus kansui, and the dashi/tare layer in bonito (katsuo) and other umami — not celiac-safe. No rice or konjac noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: Broths are built on chicken, sea bream, clam/shellfish dashi, and bonito flakes, with pork chashu — no documented plant-based bowl. The shiitake/kombu in the tare sit atop animal stocks, not a standalone vegan broth.

ramen.pantheon: A polished modern-Japanese kaiseki take on shio ramen, where the soul lives in a crystal-clear chicken broth deepened with delicate clam dashi — or, in the sister bowl, the sweet umami of whole wild red sea bream. The salt tare, aged with Okinawa and Niigata sea salts plus kombu, shiitake and bonito, gives a soft, refined salinity that tastes unmistakably of careful Japanese dashi craft rather than any single regional lineage.

name: 麺や 鳥の鶏次 KYOTO 四条河原町店 (Men-ya Tori no Keiji KYOTO Shijo Kawaramachi)

noodles.notes: Smooth, slightly flat medium-thick noodles with good elasticity and strong broth pickup; sources vary between straight and gently wavy. Built to carry the aerated, creamy chicken broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: 鶏そば (塩) — chicken shio paitan, the core bowl that best showcases the pure chicken broth. Flavored variations include 鶏次レッド (chili oil), 鶏次ブラック (black-garlic mayu), 鶏次グリーン (matcha), 鶏そばフロマージュ (cheese), and トリュフそば (truffle).
ramen.chashu: Two kinds of house-made low-temperature chicken rare chashu — tender, juicy, odor-free thigh and breast; mild-flavored so it complements rather than competes with the broth.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a shoyu-tare option mean these bowls are not celiac-safe. No rice/konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented.

vegan.note: The entire identity is chicken: broth is domestic chicken and water only, tare and chashu are chicken-based, so all bowls are animal-derived. No plant-broth option is offered (the shop is notably popular with Muslim diners precisely because everything is chicken, not pork).

ramen.pantheon: A foam-style tori paitan in the modern Osaka lineage — fresh domestic chicken and water simmered over eight hours, then blitzed tableside-fresh into an aerated, cappuccino-like froth so creamy and sweet it tastes almost dairy-rich, with no vegetables or seafood to dilute the pure poultry soul. The shio reads cleanest, while playful black-garlic, chili-oil, matcha, cheese and truffle riffs push the genre into dessert-like indulgence.

name: 中華料理 大鵬 (Chuka Ryori Taiho)

noodles.notes: The dry tantanmen rides on thin, non-kansui straight noodles (described as Kagawa-style fine udon) rather than typical kansui ramen noodles, chosen to soak up the sesame-chili sauce pooled at the bottom of the bowl. Toss top-to-bottom before eating. Thickness/shape for the soup tantanmen is not separately documented.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tantanmen — order the shiru-nashi (dry) tantanmen for the house's most distinctive bowl; a soup tantanmen is also offered. (Note: this is a Sichuan town-Chinese kitchen whose true icons are teridon and Omi-beef mapo tofu, with tantanmen as its standout noodle dish.)

gf.note: Wheat noodles throughout (the dry tantanmen uses non-kansui wheat udon-style noodles) plus a soy/sesame-based Sichuan sauce; no rice-noodle or gluten-free option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The tantanmen is built on minced pork and a dashi-backed broth, and the kitchen's repertoire leans heavily on pork, Omi beef and chicken. No documented plant-broth, meat-free bowl.

ramen.pantheon: This is no ramen-counter bowl but a Sichuan kitchen's tantanmen: a sesame-rich, faintly sweet broth wired tight with dashi and a whisper of vinegar, then lit up by sweat-coaxing mala heat and the citrus-numbing perfume of huajiao. The prized dry version pools that chili-sesame sauce beneath slick thin noodles, peanuts and minced pork — closer to Chengdu's street dandan than to any Japanese gotochi lineage.

name: 京都和麺 諭吉本舗 (Kyoto Wamen Yukichi Honpo)

noodles.notes: Self-made noodles cut fresh daily, with a choice of thin, thick, or hand-kneaded (te-momi) styles matched to the soup; the thin straight cut is the default for the wafu chuka soba.

ramen.signature_bowl: Wamen (和麺) — the wafu chuka soba built on a chicken-and-shellfish dashi; premium versions crown it with a seared A5 wagyu steak finished tableside and a wash of truffle oil.
ramen.chashu: On the premium bowls the headline protein is seared A5 wagyu steak rather than traditional braised pork; the standard chuka soba's pork chashu is not clearly documented.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a shoyu-leaning tare make this unsafe for celiacs; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The broth is built on chicken bones blended with clam/shellfish dashi, and bowls feature egg and wagyu, so there is no plant-based option.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto wafu original rather than a regional throwback: its soul is a delicate double soup of chicken-gara cut with clam-and-shellfish dashi, seasoned light with shio and shoyu so the marine umami stays clear and elegant. The shop then gilds that restrained Japanese base with luxury flourishes — a tableside-seared A5 wagyu steak and a perfume of truffle oil — pushing the bowl toward a near-French extravagance.

name: 麺屋 坂本 (Menya Sakamoto)

noodles.notes: Noodles sourced from the famed Tokyo mill Asakusa Kaika-ro (浅草開化楼) — rarely seen in Kansai — slightly flat, chewy straight noodles with substantial bite, chosen to carry the delicate clear broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tori Chuka Soba (鶏中華そば) — clear chicken-and-seafood shoyu ramen; also celebrated for the chilled shio soba (塩冷やしそば) in warmer months, finished with sudachi citrus.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork chashu served in the Tokyo style; specific cut (loin vs belly) not documented.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice-noodle or gluten-free option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken plus fish dashi (niboshi, katsuo/saba-bushi, kombu) with pork chashu; no documented plant-based broth or topping set.

ramen.pantheon: A clean-soup (chintan) shop in the refined Tokyo tradition: a base of whole heritage chickens (momiji-dori, sakura-dori) layered with niboshi, bonito, mackerel flakes and Rausu kombu, then a slightly sweet shoyu kaeshi or a month-aged shio tare. You taste the lineage in the clarity — the chilled shio version is praised as elegant enough to recall a kaiseki broth, a delicate counterpoint to Kyoto's heavier se-abura tradition.

name: びし屋 (Bishiya)

noodles.notes: In-house noodles milled from Hokkaido domestic wheat; thick, straight (near hira-uchi/flat) for the tsukemen with strong wheat fragrance and a chewy, elastic bite that stands up to the thick broth. The ramen uses a slightly slimmer medium-thick straight noodle. Tsukemen portions chosen 200-300g.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tonkotsu-shoyu ramen — the house bowl — or the pork-bone-and-seafood (tonkotsu-gyokai) tsukemen with thick handmade noodles and yuzu-dashi finishing rice.
ramen.chashu: Generous thick-cut braised pork slices, soft and tender with notably high-quality, well-rendered fat; four substantial pieces in a typical bowl.

gf.note: Wheat noodles are milled in-house from domestic wheat, and the broth is a pork/seafood/chicken stock with a soy (wheat-based) tare. No rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is documented; not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork bone, chicken bones and fish/seafood dashi, topped with pork chashu. No plant-based broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: In the dense ramen corridor of Ichijoji, this is a thick, concentrated pork-bone-and-soy bowl whose soul is a deeply rendered tonkotsu rounded out with chicken and a clear current of seafood umami — rich and faintly sweet, yet scrubbed of any barnyard pork funk. Though its thick straight noodles, spinach and nori echo the look of Yokohama iekei, the kitchen pushes its own modern blend: a sharper soy edge, a gyokai sweetness, and a thick-noodle tsukemen on house-milled Hokkaido wheat finished with yuzu-scented dashi rice.

name: 中華そば 双鳩 (Chuka Soba Soukyu)

noodles.notes: Medium-thin straight noodles with a crisp bite and pronounced wheat flavor, chosen to bind tightly with the clear shoyu broth in the old-school chuka soba idiom.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (中華そば) — a nostalgic chicken-and-pork-bone soy sauce bowl accented with ginger; upgrade to the chashu-men for a generous layer of pork.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork belly (nibuta) — tender, fatty rolls of bara cut, sliced over the bowl in the classic Showa style.
notable_toppings: spicy miso condiment on the side

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken and pork bones with a shoyu tare, and the bowl is dressed with pork belly and back-fat; no plant-based broth or topping option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A loving revival of a beloved neighborhood chuka soba that once stood on this Mibu corner, the bowl pours a clear chicken-and-pork shoyu broth brightened by ginger and rounded with a gentle melt of pork back-fat — the unmistakable Kyoto soul of light-yet-savory se-abura shoyu. It tastes like the everyday Showa-era ramen that locals built their afternoons around, simple and quietly addictive.

name: ラーメン 霽レ空 (Ramen Haresora)

noodles.notes: Medium-thin noodles. The chuka soba uses flat, rectangular-cut noodles that give a pleasant tongue-feel and clean bite; the negi ramen uses medium-thin straight noodles that the thick, viscous pork-shoyu broth clings to well.

ramen.signature_bowl: Negi Ramen — a rich pork-bone-and-chicken shoyu bowl crowned with a mound of seasoned shiraga-negi (fine white-scallion threads); for a lighter classic, the chuka soba is a clear chicken-and-soy bowl.
ramen.chashu: Thin-sliced braised pork belly on the Negi Ramen; the chuka soba carries three thin slices of conventional braised chashu.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles and a soy-based (wheat) shoyu tare run through every bowl; no rice- or konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broths are built on chicken bones plus seafood/dried-fish dashi, with pork-back-fat and braised-pork toppings throughout. No documented plant-only (kombu/shiitake/veg) bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A Nijo neighborhood shop with two souls in one room: a clear, nostalgic chicken-and-soy chuka soba in the gentle Kyoto-meets-Tokyo tradition, and a richer pork-bone shoyu Negi Ramen finished with a snowfall of seasoned white-scallion threads and a slick of back-fat that nods to Kyoto's love of se-abura shoyu. You taste the kitchen's careful, additive-light hand in the balance of soy depth and clean stock.

name: 担担 四条本店 (Tantan Shijo Honten)

noodles.notes: Thin-gauge noodles with springy elasticity that grip the thick sesame broth well; a thick-noodle (futomen) substitution is offered.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chashu tantanmen — the creamy sesame tantanmen, optionally dressed up with chashu; a cold (hiyashi) version appears in summer.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork chashu offered as an add-on; the bowl's protein heart is its seasoned ground pork (niku-miso style) stirred into the broth.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a sesame-paste tantan base built on Chinese seasonings; no rice/konjac noodle or gluten-free option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Sesame-cream broth carries an animal (chicken/pork) backbone and the bowl is finished with seasoned ground pork; no documented plant-only broth. Not vegan.

ramen.pantheon: A Kyoto take on tantanmen that prizes a deeply creamy, sesame-rich umami over fire — built by a chef out of the Cantonese kitchen, so the soup drinks more like a refined Chinese broth than a chili-oil gut-punch. Heat is left to the diner via table condiments, letting the nutty sesame and savory ground pork carry the soul of the bowl.

name: 京都らーめん おおの (Kyoto Ramen Ono)

noodles.notes: Thin, firm noodles that soak up the sharp, dark shoyu broth so the seasoning lands just right; served kime (on the firmer side).

ramen.signature_bowl: Oono Ramen (おおのラーメン) — the sharp, dark-shoyu version crowned with melted back-fat (se-abura) and a heap of green scallions; the milder 京都ラーメン is the gentler sibling of the same broth.
ramen.chashu: Tender braised/roasted pork (焼豚), soft sliced loin/belly that yields into the fatty broth.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice or konjac noodle alternative is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The broth is built on chicken and pork bones, finished with pork back-fat and pork chashu — no plant-based bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: This is Kyoto's hometown soul-food: the se-abura chaccha-kei bowl where a cloudy chicken-and-pork broth is hit with a sharp, sweet-free dark shoyu and blanketed in melted back-fat. You taste the old Kyoto lineage in that punchy-yet-mellow soup — rich and fatty up front, the shoyu cutting clean behind it, the way the city's ramen has tasted for generations.

name: らー麺創房 ぱこ (Ramen Soubou Pako)

noodles.notes: Straight medium-width noodles in the signature shio bowl; thinner, firmer noodles paired with the niboshi-forward chuka soba to stand up to the punchy broth. No documentation that noodles are made in-house.

ramen.signature_bowl: Gokujo Shio Ramen (premium salt ramen) — a clear chicken-and-fish broth seasoned with shio tare; the niboshi-forward Cho-Niboshi Chuka Soba is the other pillar, and a shio tsukemen rounds out the lineup.
ramen.chashu: Thinly sliced braised roast pork (four to five slices), moist and tender with a deep, well-developed flavor.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a shoyu-component tare; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken and niboshi/seafood dashi with pork chashu and chicken fat throughout; no plant-only bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This Saiin shop chases clarity over fat: a limpid Kyoto-red-chicken and dried-sardine broth tuned bright with a delicate shio tare, the kind of restrained, dashi-driven bowl that lets the niboshi's umami sing. Its alter ego, a punchy niboshi chuka soba, pushes that dried-fish lineage to the front with an aroma that hits before the first slurp.

name: さいラーメン (Sai Ramen)

noodles.notes: Straight medium-thin noodles from a local Kyoto noodle maker, with a smooth surface and a firm, slightly snappy bite that holds up in the thick broth; regulars suggest ordering them on the firmer side.

ramen.signature_bowl: Wagyu-suji ramen — the thick chicken-and-pork paitan crowned with slow-braised wagyu beef tendon and a sweet Korean-style chili-and-sesame soy accent; the plain ramen and the karaage-and-rice set are the other ways regulars work the bowl.
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut, fall-apart braised pork chashu (some bowls served lightly seared/aburi for an aromatic char), paired with the shop's signature slow-stewed pork cartilage (nankotsu) so tender it needs no chewing — a soki-soba-like touch.
notable_toppings: karaage and rice on the set
free spicy chive oil at the counter

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles and a soy-based tare; the broth is built on long-simmered chicken and pork bones. No rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is documented.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a heavy chicken-and-pork bone paitan, dressed with pork chashu, cartilage and beef tendon. No plant-based broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A thick, collagen-rich chicken-and-pork paitan carrying the DNA of Ichijoji's famed Takayasu, where the chef spent fifteen years at the stockpot — you taste that lineage in the pottari, almost gravy-like sweetness of the soup. This is Kyoto's heavyweight, modern kotteri tori-tonkotsu rather than the city's older thin se-abura shoyu, pushed even richer with a deeper animal punch than the parent shop.

name: ラーメン たぬき屋 (Ramen Tanukiya)

noodles.notes: Noodles from the renowned Kyoto maker Menya Teigaku (麺屋棣鄂). The aged-tonkotsu bowl uses a medium-thick straight noodle with a firm, substantial bite to stand up to the thick broth; the lighter chuka-soba uses a thinner straight cut with a pleasant wheat aroma and crisp snap.

ramen.signature_bowl: Jukusei Tonkotsu Ramen (aged pork-bone ramen) — the kitchen's limited specialty, capped at about 20 bowls each at lunch and dinner. Go DX for extra chashu and a half ajitama, and consider the house "tanuki" meat-miso or keema-style curry topping stirred in.
ramen.chashu: Thick, roughly hand-cut chunks of braised pork belly rather than thin slices — soft and crumbly with fatty seams that melt into a sweet pork-fat richness, glazed in a soy-based tare. The lighter chuka-soba instead gets a tender, well-seasoned chicken chashu.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles from a Kyoto noodle maker plus a soy-based (wheat) tare run through every bowl; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is documented.

vegan.note: The soul of the menu is long-simmered pork-bone and chicken broth with pork-belly and chicken chashu; no plant-based broth or vegan bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto crowd-pleaser that refuses one lane: its limited-run star is a creamy, potage-thick pork-bone soup, simmered hard then rested a full day so the umami deepens without any barnyard funk, finished with a forward hit of garlic that rises through the nose on the first sip. Alongside it the kitchen runs a clean, almost nostalgic chicken-shoyu chuka-soba and a much-loved mazesoba, so one counter quietly covers light-and-clear to rich-and-rib-sticking.

name: 拉麺 へんてこ (Ramen Henteko)

noodles.notes: Medium / medium-thin noodles with a chewy, springy bite that lets the wheat flavor read through; sources differ on whether they run straight or gently wavy. Cooked a touch firmer for takeout orders.

ramen.signature_bowl: Henteko Shoyu (へんてこ醤油) — the chicken-paitan shoyu bowl. Variants worth knowing: the niboshi-charged "煮干しへんてこッ" and the seafood "魚介へんてこッ."
ramen.chashu: Sources differ: some describe thick chicken chashu blanched shabu-style to shed excess fat while keeping a juicy bite; others describe house slow-braised pork belly, served three generous slices. Either way it's a soft, fatty, melt-leaning cut rather than a lean loin.
notable_toppings: playful fish-shaped snack crackers (おっとっと) on the seafood bowl

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice-noodle, konjac, or gluten-free option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The broth is built on long-simmered chicken bones and feet, with chicken fat and meat chashu; no plant-based (kombu/shiitake/veg) bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A tori-paitan house from the Fukakusa-Sumizome backstreets of Fushimi, where chicken feet and bones are boiled hard for hours into a milky, gently rich soup that drinks mellow rather than heavy. Its soul is the playful, research-minded spirit of the shop — a clean chicken-cream base finished with shoyu and bent toward niboshi or seafood riffs, a modern Kyoto original rather than any one regional lineage.

name: せんぼんぐらばー館 (Senbon Gurabaa-kan)

noodles.notes: Distinctive thick yellow Nagasaki-champon noodles with a chewy, springy bite that holds up against the loaded broth and toppings; these are champon noodles (made with a unique alkaline lye water), not standard ramen noodles. The sara-udon by contrast uses hair-thin noodles deep-fried crisp.

ramen.signature_bowl: Nagasaki champon (長崎ちゃんぽん) — a milky, umami-deep bowl piled with stir-fried pork, shrimp, squid, chikuwa, naruto, cabbage, bean sprouts, onion and carrot over thick chewy champon noodles. (The sara-udon, with hair-thin crispy fried noodles under a chicken-stock veg-and-seafood ankake, is the equally famous companion specialty.)

gf.note: Champon and sara-udon noodles are wheat-based, and the stir-fried sauce/broth typically carries soy and seafood; no rice- or konjac-noodle or documented GF option exists here.

vegan.note: The signature champon broth is built on pork and chicken bones plus seafood (shrimp, squid), and the bowl is loaded with pork and seafood; no plant-only broth or vegan bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is not a ramen shop but a beloved old Nishijin town-Chinese counter whose soul is Nagasaki champon — a Kyushu-born dish where pork-and-chicken bones, the sweetness of stir-fried cabbage and the brine of shrimp and squid melt into one milky, gently salted broth. You taste Nagasaki's port-city Chinese lineage rather than any Kyoto ramen tradition: a single warming bowl that is meal, soup and noodle all at once.

name: なるかみ (Narukami)

noodles.notes: Thin-to-medium straight noodles that play a supporting role to the soup; sources describe them as crisp and springy, sitting low-to-medium hydration with a clean bite.

ramen.signature_bowl: Se-abura (back-fat) shoyu chuka-soba — the house chicken-bone soy bowl scattered with pork back fat and a peppery hit of ichimi chili. Pair it with the famous ebi-meshi (shrimp rice) on the side.
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut braised pork belly, served a couple of slices to a bowl, with a firm, chewy texture that releases meat umami rather than melting away.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-based soy (shoyu) tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Chicken-bone broth, pork back-fat oil, and pork-belly chashu are core to every bowl; no plant-based broth or topping option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A textbook bowl of Kyoto's se-abura shoyu lineage: a chicken-bone soy soup that stays clear and surprisingly light beneath a snowfall of pork back fat, so you taste depth without heaviness. What sets this one apart is the unapologetic dusting of ichimi chili, giving the old-school Kyoto soul a bright, peppery edge.

name: 新大宮 中華のサカイ 本店 (Shin-Omiya Chuka no Sakai Honten)

noodles.notes: Custom-ordered, exclusive to the shop: notably thick, glossy-translucent and intensely springy/mochi-mochi — closer to a tsukemen-grade noodle than a typical chilled-Chinese strand, and very much the co-star of the bowl.

ramen.signature_bowl: Reimen (cold noodle) — the year-round signature; choose the chashu (yakibuta) version over the ham version. A hot chuka-soba is also offered but is a secondary item.
ramen.chashu: Sliced braised pork (yakibuta) is the standard heart; a ham version is offered as the alternate protein.

gf.note: Wheat noodles plus a vinegar/soy-leaning sesame-mustard dressing; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is documented.

vegan.note: The signature cold bowl carries pork (or ham) and an umami-rich dashi-laced dressing; no plant-only broth or topping set is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A Showa-born Kyoto town-Chinese institution whose soul lives not in a hot soup but in a chilled bowl: thick, springy custom noodles bound in a creamy sesame-mustard-vinegar dressing so beloved that regulars drink the leftover sauce. In a country where ramen means hot broth, this is Kyoto's definitive answer to what a cold noodle can be — its own lineage, found nowhere else.

name: みその橋サカイ (Misonobashi Sakai)

noodles.notes: Medium-thick straight noodles in the traditional Kyoto machi-chuka mold; the broth clings to them. (The shop's signature cold-noodle dish uses far thicker, chewy, glossy egg noodles, but the hot chuka-soba runs medium straight.)
