ramen.signature_bowl: The house is famous for its year-round cold noodles (reimen), but the hot bowl to order is the chuka-soba — a Kyoto-style pork-bone-and-chicken shoyu ramen; chashu-men adds extra roast pork.
ramen.chashu: Sliced braised/roast pork (yakibuta) in the old-school machi-chuka manner; the same roast pork that crowns the celebrated cold noodles.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-sauce (wheat) tare; the cold-noodle sauce also contains soy and egg. No rice or konjac noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork-bone and chicken stock and the bowls are dressed with roast pork; the cold-noodle sauce likewise lists chicken extract, pork extract and egg. No documented plant-based broth.

ramen.pantheon: A beloved old-guard Kyoto neighborhood Chinese kitchen near Misonobashi whose hot chuka-soba speaks pure Kyoto lineage — a clear, soy-tinged soup drawn from pork bones and chicken that coats medium-thin straight noodles. Its true legend, though, is the secret-recipe cold noodle eaten all year, a chilled bowl found nowhere else in the same form.

name: 一番星 (Ichibanboshi)

noodles.notes: Thin, straight noodles with a slightly soft, gentle bite that cling to and soak up the soy-pork broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (中華そば) — the one bowl this shop makes: a pork-bone-and-chicken soy broth over thin straight noodles, served with a side of house pickles.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork chashu, rested a full day until tender and juicy, with clearly defined lean and fat layers; the fat carries a subtle sweetness.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy-sauce tare made with wheat; no rice-noodle or gluten-free option exists here.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a pork-bone and chicken broth with a touch of pork fat, finished with chashu — no plant-based broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A nostalgic survivor of old Kyoto's noodle tradition: pork bones and chicken simmered with onion into a soy-darkened soup that drinks light and mellow yet leaves a faint clinging richness on the lips. It is the gentle, everyday face of Kyoto ramen — pork-bone soy with a whisper of fat rather than the heavy back-fat curtain of the city's louder shops.

name: 慈永 (Jiei)

noodles.notes: Chuhoso (medium-thin) straight noodles with a clean, crisp bite and clear wheat character; described as silky and supple, gripping the tea-colored broth well.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chashu Ramen — the standard ramen (around 750 yen) topped with extra slices of the tender pork belly chashu.
ramen.chashu: Long, thin slices of braised pork belly, simmered until they crumble apart at the touch of chopsticks; tender and full-flavored without being heavy.
notable_toppings: thick-cut crunchy menma with a gentle sweetness

gf.note: Standard wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles and a soy-based (wheat) tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken and pork bones with a soy tare, finished with pork back fat and topped with pork belly chashu; no plant-based broth or topping option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A nostalgic bowl in the classic Kyoto se-abura shoyu tradition: a tea-colored broth of chicken and pork bones seasoned with dark soy and floated with a thin veil of pork back fat, so you taste the old-school Kyoto lineage (a Takaraya-style soul carried over from the owner's years at a veteran Kyoto ramen-ya) in every mellow, deep-umami sip. It is the comforting, lived-in Kyoto shoyu — rich and well-developed but never cloying — that defines the city's everyday ramen culture.

name: 昆布と麺 喜一 (Konbu to Men Kiichi)

noodles.notes: Whole-grain (zenryūfun) noodles made in-house/for the shop by ramen maker Tōhichi using first-grade Hokkaido Haruyokoi wheat. The bowl (ramen) uses a medium straight whole-grain noodle; the seasonal gagome-kombu tsukemen pairs a thicker, chewier whole-grain noodle chilled in cold water for firm, elastic bite.

ramen.signature_bowl: 昆布らぁめん (Kombu ramen) — a clear shio bowl built entirely on kombu and seafood dashi, topped with the shop's hand-shaved oboro kombu. In warmer months (roughly May–October) the kitchen switches to the がごめ昆布つけ麺 (gagome-kombu tsukemen), with a sticky kombu-rich dip and thick whole-grain noodles.
ramen.chashu: For the ramen, the "chashu" is domestic young chicken breast gently cooked in kombu dashi and salt — lean, delicate, and in keeping with the no-meat-in-the-broth philosophy. The seasonal tsukemen instead uses soft, tender braised pork (nibuta).
notable_toppings: Hand-shaved oboro kombu (true kombu, shaved to order)

gf.note: Noodles are wheat-based whole-grain (Haruyokoi) men with kansui, and the tsukemen dip adds shoyu — both contain wheat/gluten, so not celiac-safe. No documented rice or konjac noodle option.

vegan.note: The broth is uniquely meat-free and additive-free, built on three kombu varieties plus seafood (bonito, dried squid, shellfish). The kombu dashi base itself is plant-derived, but the soup includes katsuo/seafood and the toppings include chicken or pork, so as served it is not vegan. A kombu-and-vegetable-only bowl is not advertised — worth asking, but do not assume.

ramen.pantheon: A one-of-a-kind bowl from a 150-year-old Kyoto kombu house: the soup is pure kelp-and-sea umami — true, Rausu, and Rishiri kombu cold-steeped in soft water, layered with bonito, dried squid and shellfish, and seasoned with nothing but salt. There is no meat and no kaeshi in the pot, so what you taste is the clean, glutamate-rich lineage of dashi itself, finished with kombu shaved over the bowl by hand.

name: 麺屋 聖〜kiyo〜 (Menya Kiyo)

noodles.notes: House-made noodles milled and cut fresh each morning at the shop's adjoining in-house noodle workshop; medium-thick with a springy, chewy, firm bite (puri/mocchiri) that grips the shoyu broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Okurimono ("Gift" / 贈り物) — the clear, dashi-forward shoyu chuka-soba, the bowl that defines the shop. The broth is finished tableside as the rich pork-fat from the chashu melts in and rounds out the soy.
ramen.chashu: The heart of the bowl: thick-cut braised pork, simmered gently for over two hours until meltingly tender, then sliced to the diner's chosen thickness right at the counter — a slab so substantial it eats more like a meat dish set in the ramen than a topping.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a soy-sauce (wheat) tare; no rice/konjac noodle or GF-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth leans on fish dashi (bonito/seafood) and the bowl is finished with pork fat and topped with braised pork; no documented plant-based broth or vegan bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A polished Kyoto take on the city's dashi-and-shoyu soul: a clear, refined soy broth layered with bonito, kombu and seafood umami, then rounded out tableside as melting pork fat enriches the soup — restrained and elegant where Tokyo shoyu is sharper. You taste Kyoto's beloved sweet, dashi-deep lineage in every sip, anchored by a slab of slow-braised chashu cut to order.

name: コムギノキラメキ〈小麦〉 (Komugi no Kirameki (Komugi))

noodles.notes: House-made noodles from the shop's glass-walled noodle workshop using 100% Hokkaido wheat. The standard paitan bowls use a smooth, chewy (tsuru-mochi) medium-thin straight noodle that suits the delicate-yet-rich soup. The Jiro-style mazesoba 'Komugi no Chikara' instead uses an ultra-thick (3mm, #10 cutter) square straight noodle with strong bite.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shio no Kirameki (salt tori-paitan) — the cleanest expression of the rich, creamy chicken-paitan soup; the soy (Shoyu no Kirameki) and miso versions share the same broth. The shop-exclusive Jiro-style mazesoba "Komugi no Chikara" is the brothless counterpart for big-noodle, back-fat cravings.
ramen.chashu: Seared chicken-thigh chashu — thick, tender slices of thigh meat with a lightly torched/seared surface, served on the paitan bowls. (The Jiro-style Komugi no Chikara mazesoba instead tops with tender pork and back-fat.)

gf.note: Noodles are house-made 100% Hokkaido wheat with kansui and the soy/miso tare contain wheat; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of every bowl is a long-simmered chicken-bone paitan, with chicken or pork chashu and animal-based tare/oil. No plant-broth bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A modern tori-paitan specialist whose soul is a long-simmered chicken broth boiled until it turns milky, creamy and clinging — the cloudy, emulsified style that defines contemporary chicken-paitan ramen rather than any old regional lineage. You taste that pure poultry richness most clearly in the restrained salt (shio) bowl, where the velvety soup, not the seasoning, carries the bowl.

name: ラーメン ビスカ (Ramen Viska)

noodles.notes: House offers a choice of thick or thin noodles. The default with the kotteri is a medium-thick, fairly straight noodle that clings well to the heavy broth; the thick option has a wavy, hand-cut-feeling, springy-chewy texture. Thin noodle also available.

ramen.signature_bowl: Kotteri pork-bone ramen — the ultra-thick emulsified tonkotsu the shop is built on. A clear assari version, an urume-sardine tsukemen, and a Jiro-style "Bis-ro" (mashimashi) are the alternates.
ramen.chashu: Lean, red-meat-forward pork chashu cooked more like a roast than a fatty braise, so it drinks up the heavy broth rather than melting into it.
notable_toppings: harissa chili paste at the table

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a shoyu (wheat) tare and a pork-bone broth; nothing rice- or konjac-based is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Built entirely on a long-simmered pork-bone broth with pork chashu and a shoyu tare; no plant-based bowl exists here.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto kotteri specialist in the Ichijoji ramen battleground, this bowl chases pure pork-bone density — a broth simmered so far down it turns velvety and potage-thick, the kind a spoon nearly stands up in, all umami and no gaminess. It is a contemporary tonkotsu original rather than a regional copy, with a clear assari counterpart and a North-African harissa kicker that signals the kitchen's playful streak.

name: 煌力 (Goriki)

noodles.notes: Semi-translucent high-hydration thin noodles sourced from a renowned Kyoto noodle maker (Menya Teikaku); springy, snappy 'puri-puri' bite that stands up to the thick sesame soup.

ramen.signature_bowl: THE 担々麺 (THE Tantanmen) — the house standard: a thick, smooth sesame-and-nut soup with freshly ground sesame and house-made chili oil, finished with fragrant Kyoto sansho. Order the tsuke-tantanmen version (served over a tabletop IH burner that keeps the dipping soup hot) for the shop's playful twist, and add the lunch rice set to finish the leftover soup as a stone-baked bibimbap-style kuppa.
ramen.chashu: No sliced chashu — the protein is a spicy miso-seasoned minced-pork soboro stirred into the soup.

gf.note: Wheat noodles with kansui and a sesame/soy-based tantan tare make this unsafe for celiacs; no rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The soup is built on animal stock and the bowl is topped with minced pork; no documented plant-broth, meat-free version.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto tantanmen specialist where the soul is the soup: a deep, velvety river of freshly ground sesame and nuts riding a savory meat stock, balanced across umami, sweetness, salt and a gentle chili heat. What sets it apart is the bright, fruity numbing perfume of Kyoto sansho instead of dried Sichuan pepper — a distinctly local, aromatic take on the genre.

name: まる担 おがわ (Marutan Ogawa)

noodles.notes: Thin, firm straight noodles supplied by the Teigaku noodle maker; they hold up against the rich, chili-oil-forward broth and tangle well with the minced-pork niku-miso. Some reviewers note a slight curl. Kaedama (noodle refills) are popular.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tantanmen (担々麺) at spice level 1 — the house standard. The umami-spicy soup is built on a fragrant, slightly bitter house-made chili oil with rich koku, dressed with niku-miso (minced pork), pak choi, bean sprouts and menma. A miso tantanmen and a vinegar-sharp sanratan (hot-and-sour) version are the main alternatives; wonton and seasonal chilled bowls also appear.
ramen.chashu: No sliced chashu — the meat is niku-miso, a savory minced-pork miso crumble spooned over the noodles in place of a chashu slice.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a miso/soy-leaning tare and minced-pork niku-miso; no rice-noodle or gluten-free option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The build centers on a minced-pork niku-miso and a meat/dashi-based broth; no plant-only (kombu/shiitake, no katsuo/pork) bowl is documented. Spice level is adjustable, but the soup and topping are animal-based.

ramen.pantheon: A yatai-style tantanmen worked out of a residential Nishikyo driveway, this is a Japanese-tuned dandan bowl whose soul is its house-made chili oil — fragrant, faintly bitter and built for koku rather than searing heat. With adjustable spice and a savory minced-pork niku-miso over pak choi and sprouts, it sits in the everyday, addictive-comfort lane of Japanese tantanmen rather than the numbing Sichuan or shiru-nashi schools.

name: らぁ～めん京 祇園本店 (Raamen Miyako Gion Honten)

noodles.notes: Signature low-temperature aged thin noodles (低温熟成細麺), developed in-house over years. Described as mochi-mochi/springy yet smooth (もちっとプリっとした歯切れの良い食感), they coat well with the emulsified Gion chicken broth. Larger portion (omori) available.

ramen.signature_bowl: Miyako Ramen (みやこらぁ～めん) — the house chicken-paitan bowl, the "Gion white broth" topped with ajitama, scallion, and menma. Gion-honten exclusive: the white-miso version blending Kyoto's traditional Saikyo white miso into the chicken paitan; an extra-rich kotteri (特濃こってり) option is also offered.
ramen.chashu: Long-braised pork chashu cut thick, chosen for a good balance of fat and lean; sweetly seasoned, very tender and meltingly soft in the mouth.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a shoyu/miso tare make this unsafe for celiacs; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented.

vegan.note: The soul of every bowl is a long-simmered chicken broth finished with pork back-fat and pork chashu; no plant-based (kombu/shiitake/vegetable) broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto take on tori-paitan: a creamy, emulsified chicken broth — the shop's "Gion white broth" — built on chicken and plenty of vegetables, then lifted with a touch of pork back-fat so it stays deep yet drinkable rather than heavy. It nods to Kyoto's love of rich, fatty soup while threading its own house identity, even folding the city's traditional Saikyo white miso into a signature variant.

name: ラーメン 麺対軒 (Ramen Mentaiken)

noodles.notes: Medium-thick round noodles with a soft, tender surface rather than firm and chewy; the jet-black broth clings to and fully coats them. The noodles play a supporting role to the soup.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shoyu ramen (醤油ラーメン) — a lacquer-black soy bowl — best taken as the famous set with the matching jet-black fried rice (yakimeshi). The clear shio ginger ramen (生姜ラーメン) is the standout alternative.
ramen.chashu: Thin slices of lean braised pork loin, minimal fat — modest, old-school town-Chinese style rather than a thick fatty centerpiece.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles and a wheat-based dark soy tare; no rice-noodle, konjac, or gluten-free options are documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken and pork bones with pork chashu and a meat-forward soy tare; no plant-based broth bowl is offered. A vegetable ramen exists on the menu but sits in the same animal broth.

ramen.pantheon: A 1975-founded neighborhood town-Chinese counter pouring Kyoto's signature lacquer-black shoyu ramen — the Shinpuku-lineage bowl whose soup looks like ink yet drinks gentle, the dark soy hitting first before a quiet chicken-and-pork-bone umami rises underneath. The same broth tints the legendary black fried rice that turns this into one of Kyoto's classic ramen-plus-meshi sets.

name: らーめん杉千代 (Ramen Sugichiyo)

noodles.notes: Low-hydration thin straight noodles with a chewy, mochi-like bite that holds up against the clinging back-fat broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chashu-men (the standard ramen topped with extra braised pork belly) — the cloudy back-fat shoyu bowl that built the shop's reputation.
ramen.chashu: Pork belly braised until meltingly tender, collapsing under chopsticks.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles and a wheat-based soy (shoyu) tare make this bowl unsafe for celiac diners; no rice- or konjac-noodle alternative is offered.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a pork-and-chicken bone broth finished with pork back fat and braised pork belly — no plant-based option exists.

ramen.pantheon: A revived legend of the Kyoto se-abura tradition: a milky, pork-and-chicken bone broth seasoned with soy and dusted with melted back fat, yet drinking far lighter than its opaque white surface suggests. A default scattering of ichimi chili gives the heritage Kyoto bowl a quiet warmth.

name: 鶏そば223 (Torisoba 223 (Tsutsumi))

noodles.notes: Thin, low-hydration noodles with a slightly crimped/hand-kneaded wave, made by Kyoto noodle-maker Teigaku (棣鄂). They have a clean bite on arrival and soften gracefully as they drink up the chicken broth; good slurp and throat-feel.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tori-shio soba — the clear chicken salt ramen. Order "ミラノの朝陽 (Milano no Asahi)" for the cleaner, lighter version, or "フィレンツェの夕日 (Firenze no Yuhi)" for the deeper, richer koku bowl. Both are the same chicken-shio soul at different intensities.
ramen.chashu: Not pork — the protein is sliced tori-hamu (low-temperature chicken-breast ham), salt-cured and seasoned with herbs/spice in a French-leaning style. Tender, moist, gently pink, served as a few slices.
notable_toppings: house olive sauce condiment (clam, basil, shallot, olive oil, black pepper) for mid-bowl flavor change

gf.note: Wheat-kansui ramen noodles from a noodle-maker; not celiac-safe. No rice or konjac noodle option is documented. The shio tare itself is salt-based, but the noodles rule it out.

vegan.note: Broth is essentially 99% chicken/jidori and chicken-feet stock, and the protein is chicken ham. No plant-only broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A Kyoto chicken-shio specialist that breaks from the city's usual se-abura shoyu lineage: the soul here is a painstaking clear jidori-and-chicken-feet broth, collagen-silky yet clean, seasoned by a salt tare its chef honed studying French cuisine (a three-salt blend of white, pink and black). The French-Italian flourishes — chicken-breast ham, a clam-and-basil olive sauce, a knob of butter — make it a refined modern tori-soba rather than any regional classic.

name: 麺屋 しな埜 (Menya Shinano)

noodles.notes: Medium-thin straight noodles cooked slightly firm (yaya-katame) to cut through the aerated chicken broth; some bowls use a medium-thick wavy noodle.

ramen.signature_bowl: Ajitama Awa-hakutou ramen (foam chicken-paitan with seasoned egg) — the aerated, cappuccino-creamy chicken paitan finished with a Japanese-style soy/umami edge.
ramen.chashu: Rare, low-temperature-cooked pork (shoulder loin), sliced thin — two slices laid over the bowl rather than a fatty braised or char-grilled cut.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a soy-based tare; no rice-noodle, konjac, or gluten-free option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is a long-simmered chicken-bone-and-whole-chicken paitan with chicken-fat aroma and pork chashu; no plant-based broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto tori-paitan that whips its slow-simmered chicken-bone-and-whole-chicken broth into a cappuccino-like foam, so the soup lands creamy and feather-light on the tongue rather than heavy. Its soul is the twist of finishing that rich white broth with a Japanese soy-and-umami edge, giving a clean, refined aftertaste that sets it apart from the dense, gravy-thick tori-paitan that made the city famous.

name: 中華そば アッキー (Chuka Soba Akky)

noodles.notes: Medium-thin (chuuboso) straight noodles supplied by a Kyoto noodle maker, with springy bite and smooth slip. The mazesoba bowl instead uses thick, flat, chewy crimped noodles.

ramen.signature_bowl: Seabura Black Chuka Soba (back-fat black soy ramen) — a 100% pork broth with a dark, deep tamari-shoyu tare and a generous slick of back fat; the lighter Seabura Tonkotsu Shoyu is the southern-Kyoto-style sibling.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork belly (sanmai-niku), tender and juicy, cut in large pieces to play up pork umami and aroma.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based tamari shoyu tare over a pork broth — not celiac-safe, and no rice/konjac noodle alternative is documented.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on a 100% pork broth with pork back fat and pork-belly chashu; no plant-based broth option is offered.

ramen.pantheon: This bowl lives in the Kyoto se-abura shoyu tradition: a 100% pork broth kept clear and clean-finishing rather than cloudy, married to a dark, deep tamari-soy tare and crowned with a melt of back fat. You taste southern-Kyoto lineage in the soup — soy sharpness cut by sweet pork fat, rich but never heavy.

name: あんびしゃす花 (Ambitious Hana)

noodles.notes: Signature extra-thick (gokubuto) noodles with strong bite, often likened to Sanuki udon; a thick or medium-thick option is offered, and a 400g large portion is the same price as regular. Some accounts mention tapioca-blended noodles for extra chew.

ramen.signature_bowl: Koku-uma noukou tsukemen (rich concentrated dipping noodles) — the thick seafood-and-pork dipping broth served in a hot stone pot kept actively simmering at the table so it never cools, paired with the shop's signature extra-thick noodles. Finish with a soup-wari (dashi dilution).
ramen.chashu: Large, thick slices braised until melt-in-the-mouth tender, kakuni-style (resembling braised pork belly) with a deep, sweet-savory flavor.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen/tsukemen noodles and shoyu-based broth; no documented rice or konjac noodle or GF tare option. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork/chicken bones and seafood (gyokai) dashi with braised pork chashu; no documented plant-based broth option.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto tsukemen specialist in the rich tonkotsu-gyokai (pork-and-seafood double-soup) lineage that swept Japan in the 2000s, but with its own signature theatrics: the concentrated dipping broth arrives in a hot stone pot, simmering at your table so the last mouthful is as scorching as the first. Pair that intense, slightly sweet soup with the shop's udon-thick noodles and you taste a bowl built for the heavy-hitter crowd rather than the delicate sea-abura shoyu Kyoto is usually known for.
sources: https://muchi2.com/2018/08/25/京都で話題のつけ麺店で食べる/

name: タンポポ (Tanpopo)

noodles.notes: Smooth, slurpable straight noodles; descriptions range from slightly thin to medium, cooked on the softer side to soak up the back-fat shoyu broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Ramen blanketed with the shop's signature red togarashi (chili) powder over a pork-and-soy broth thick with floating back-fat — order it as the classic bowl, optionally with a side of yakimeshi (fried rice). Chashu-men for those who want extra pork.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork belly, simmered until it crumbles apart at the touch of chopsticks, releasing a sweet, rich pork fattiness.
notable_toppings: red togarashi (chili) powder blanketing the surface

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles with a wheat-based soy (shoyu) tare and a pork broth; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Pork-bone broth, soy tare, pork back-fat and braised pork-belly chashu are central to every bowl; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A standard-bearer of Kyoto's se-abura shoyu lineage: a clean pork-and-soy soup made indulgent by a thick raft of melted back-fat, its gentle shoyu carrying that mellow porky sweetness rather than fighting it. The shop's signature flourish — a fiery-looking cap of red togarashi powder that tastes far milder than it appears — has made this long-running neighborhood bowl a Kyoto institution.

name: ラーメン ゆう (Ramen Yu)

noodles.notes: Medium, mostly straight noodles with a firm, springy 'patsu-patsu' bite that drink up the salty soy broth; some sources describe them as thinner. Slight curl noted in places.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shoyu chuka soba (soy-sauce ramen), upgraded to the chashu-men for the extra belly slices
ramen.chashu: Tender, melt-in-the-mouth braised pork belly, several slices per bowl, lightly salted to play against the salty broth; some bowls feature leaner loin cuts

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat soy-sauce tare make this bowl unsafe for celiacs; no rice or konjac noodle alternative is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork bones and chicken with pork-fat richness and pork chashu; no plant-based broth option is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A nostalgic neighborhood bowl in the Kyoto soy-sauce lineage: pork bones and chicken simmered into a deep, savory, lightly fatty broth seasoned with a punchy shoyu tare, the kind of unpretentious chuka soba locals have leaned on for decades. Its soul is the balance of porky umami and assertive soy salt, slicked with rendered fat and crowned by famously tender melt-away chashu.

name: 京一 本店 (Kyoichi Honten)

noodles.notes: Smooth-textured (tsurun) house noodles that slip easily; the curry-chukasoba uses custom-made thinner noodles chosen to cling to the thick curry-an while resisting sogginess.

ramen.signature_bowl: Curry Chukasoba (カレー中華) — house ramen noodles drowned in a thick, dashi-rich Japanese-style curry an, gently spiced and sweetened by braised wagyu beef. The plain Chukasoba (中華そば) is the classic clear-soup alternative.
ramen.chashu: Thin-sliced braised pork-belly chashu (about three pieces) in the standard chukasoba.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; the curry an is also flour-thickened. No rice- or konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented.

vegan.note: The clear soup is built on chicken bones blended with udon dashi (katsuo/kombu) and dressed with pork-belly chashu and fishcake; the curry version is sweetened with braised wagyu beef. No plant-broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A survivor of postwar Kyoto's neighborhood shokudo culture, this is honest machi-chuka 中華そば: a clean, amber chicken-gara soup softened with the kombu-and-katsuo dashi of an udon shop, finished with a gentle shoyu tare. Its famous curry-chukasoba buries those same noodles under a thick, dashi-forward Japanese curry an — comfort food rather than a specialist's bowl.

name: 廣東餐館 鳳飛 (Kanton Sankan Houhi)

noodles.notes: Thin, supple noodles chosen to cling to the thickened gravy of the karashisoba; in the plain chuka-soba they sit soft and pliable in a clear chicken-kombu broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Karashisoba (からしそば / 撈麺) — thin noodles under a glossy chicken-and-kombu ankake gravy loaded with pork, small shrimp, bamboo shoot and pepper, finished with a sharp lift of Japanese mustard (karashi).
ramen.chashu: The karashisoba carries sliced pork simmered into the gravy rather than a sit-on-top chashu; the gomoku ramen adds yakibuta-style roasted pork slices. No aburi or smoked loin — restrained Kyoto-Chinese pork.

gf.note: Wheat-kansui noodles and a soy-based seasoning; the karashisoba gravy is also thickened with wheat-adjacent starch and built on a shared kitchen — no documented rice/konjac-noodle or GF option.

vegan.note: The broth and gravy are built on chicken bones (with kombu) and the noodle dishes carry pork and shrimp; no documented plant-only bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A direct descendant of the fabled Houmai school of Kyoto town-Chinese, this kitchen speaks in the gentle, dashi-forward dialect of 京中華 — chicken bones quietly married to kombu, almost no garlic, very little surface oil. Its soul is the karashisoba: a clear-souled chicken-kombu lineage thickened into a silky ankake and lit by a clean stripe of yellow mustard, restraint that lets every shrimp, pork slice and bamboo shoot taste of itself.

name: なぶら (Nabura)

noodles.notes: Flat, crimped thick noodles (ramen) sourced from an established Kyoto noodle maker; the tsukemen uses a chewy whole-wheat flat noodle. Built to grip the dense saba broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Saba ramen (鯖ラーメン) — the thick, condensed mackerel-and-kombu broth crowned with saba tatsuta-age
ramen.chashu: No pork chashu; the protein centerpiece is saba tatsuta-age (lightly battered, fried marinated mackerel) set atop the bowl in place of a meat slice.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen and tsukemen noodles, plus a battered fried-mackerel topping (tatsuta-age uses flour), make this unsafe for celiacs. No rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The entire identity of the bowl is a mackerel (saba) and kombu dashi, and the signature topping is fried fish. Despite using no pork or land-animal stock, it is fundamentally fish-based and not vegan.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto original built by a 35-year washoku chef: instead of pork or chicken, the soul here is a single fish — saba — simmered with kombu into a dense, potage-thick broth with no chemical seasoning, no fish powder, and no animal stock at all. You taste pure mackerel umami rounded by kelp, a clean-yet-rich bowl that stands apart from Kyoto's usual se-abura shoyu lineage.

name: 麺ビストロ Nakano (Men Bistro Nakano)

noodles.notes: Flat, high-hydration wavy noodles with a soft, mochi-like chew that drink up the light broth; pale rather than the usual yellow kansui color. The black-shoyu bowl reads a touch more medium-thick and springy. One source described thin noodles, but most accounts point to flatter medium strands.

ramen.signature_bowl: 白しょうゆ麺 (White Shoyu Men) — a clear, refined French-style chicken-and-seafood broth seasoned with pale white tamari shoyu
ramen.chashu: Light chicken chashu — gentle and low-fat, tuned to sit under the delicate white broth rather than dominate it. The retro black-shoyu bowl instead uses an old-school grilled pork (yakibuta) style.

gf.note: Chinese-style wheat-and-kansui noodles throughout, and the shoyu tare is wheat-based. No rice/konjac noodle or GF-tare option is documented; not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broths are built on French chicken fond (fonds de volaille) plus fish/seafood stock and bushi dashi, with chicken or pork chashu. No plant-only (kombu/shiitake/veg) bowl is documented. The seasonal creamy potage noodles still use dairy and a chicken-stock base.

ramen.pantheon: A modern-original Kyoto creation from a French-trained chef who builds his soup like a classical kitchen — a clear chicken fond de volaille married to Japanese bushi dashi, then seasoned with pale white tamari shoyu so the bowl drinks like an elegant consommé rather than a ramen. You taste the French-Japanese lineage in the soup: refined, herb-touched, and a world away from Kyoto's usual back-fat shoyu.

name: 真打 英多朗 (Shinuchi Eitaro)

noodles.notes: Straight thin noodles made from domestic wheat with deliberately low kansui content, custom-made by a noodle maker the shop courted for years; clean finish with good grip on the broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Yuzu Ramen (ゆずラーメン) — a refined chicken-and-bonito shoyu bowl finished with abundant back-fat and a generous squeeze of fresh yuzu.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork shoulder loin (kata-rosu) so tender it crumbles when lifted with chopsticks; sourced from a noted Kyoto butcher.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a soy-sauce (wheat) tare; this is a noodle house (also serving udon), so no gluten-free option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken bones and bonito first-dashi, with pork back-fat and braised pork chashu throughout; no plant-based broth or topping option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: Born from a veteran udon master's own dashi craft, this bowl marries a seven-hour chicken-bone stock with bonito first-dashi and a soy tare, then lights it up with fresh yuzu — its soul is the Kyoto se-abura shoyu lineage softened and brightened, where sweet pork back-fat and citrus acidity meet over a clear, fragrant soup. It is less a regional copy than a Kyoto original: the depth of a noodle shop's broth wearing a coat of yuzu.

name: 亜喜英 (Akihide)

noodles.notes: Substantial chewy (mochi-mochi) extra-thick straight noodles, around 180g standard, built to stand up to and grab the spoon-coatingly thick broth; some accounts say it eats almost like tsukemen. A thinner noodle is also mentioned at times.

ramen.signature_bowl: Jun-nōkō ramen (the single ultra-rich house bowl) — choose your soup density from mellow (maroyaka) up to dorodoro (sludge-thick), and optionally add garlic or a hit of yuzu fragrance.
ramen.chashu: Two kinds in one bowl: a hanging-roasted/char-grilled (tsurushi-yaki, aburi) pork with fragrant charred edges, plus a braised (nibuta) pork — both thick-cut and falling-apart tender.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a shoyu-leaning tare over a long-simmered pork/chicken broth — no rice, konjac, or gluten-free option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The entire identity is a pork-bone and chicken-bone broth with pork chashu and pork fat — no plant-based broth or bowl exists here.

ramen.pantheon: A Kyoto temple of gokunō (ultra-thick) kotteri, where pork and chicken bones are boiled down until a spoon stands upright in the bowl — broth so concentrated it reads more like a sauce than a soup. This is not old-school Kyoto se-abura shoyu but a modern obsession with sheer density, the lineage you taste being pure rendered bone reduced to its richest possible form.

name: ラーメン あかつき (Ramen Akatsuki)

noodles.notes: Special-order thin, straight noodles with firm chew (koshi); aged. A slim noodle that carries the back-fat-glossed soup.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chashu ramen (or the loaded "Akatsuki Special") — the standard pork-bone-and-chicken shoyu bowl crowned with extra back-fat and sliced chashu; sizes run from kids/chibi up to jumbo.
ramen.chashu: Tender braised pork laid over the bowl — described as fatty, melt-in-the-mouth slices fanned out like flower petals; sources vary between loin and belly, both braised rather than grilled.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles in a soy-sauce (wheat) tare over a pork/chicken broth — no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Soul of the bowl is a pork-bone and chicken broth finished with pork back-fat and braised pork chashu — no plant-based broth or topping option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A neighborhood institution of Kyoto's signature se-abura shoyu lineage: a pork-bone-and-chicken soy broth, simmered overnight and rested a full day, then glossed with sweet pork back-fat that pools on the surface yet drinks far lighter than its creamy look suggests. The clean koku of that soup over slim straight noodles is the taste of old Kita-Shirakawa.

name: 特製ラーメン 大中 本店 (Tokusei Ramen Daichu Honten)

noodles.notes: Custom-milled slightly-thin straight noodles (from a local noodle maker) built to resist softening and to grip the heavy broth. Firmness is one of the free customization choices.

ramen.signature_bowl: Daichu Ramen — the rich pork-bone-and-chicken shoyu tonkotsu; the "Tokuno" extra-concentrated version is the move for devoted kotteri fans, with thin straight noodles, a selectable pork cut, and free add-ons (bean sprouts, scallion, egg, kimchi).
ramen.chashu: Braised Kyushu pork with selectable cuts — melt-on-the-tongue belly (bara), shoulder-loin (kata-rosu), and lean loin (rosu); relatively lean, soaking up the sauce-thick broth. Diners can choose or combine cuts.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles and a soy-sauce (wheat) tare; no rice/konjac noodle or GF-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is pork bone plus chicken frames simmered 24 hours, with pork chashu and an optional fish-pork variant. No plant-based broth bowl exists here.

ramen.pantheon: A Kyushu-style tonkotsu transplanted to a Fushimi railway underpass: pork bones and chicken frames are cracked in-house and boiled hard for a full day into a white, potage-thick broth so dense a chopstick can stand in it, then seasoned with shoyu for a soy-tonkotsu soul. It drinks rich and clinging yet finishes surprisingly clean — among the most kotteri bowls in all of Kyoto.

name: 元祖熟成細麺 香来 壬生本店 (Ganso Jukusei Hosomen Kourai Mibu Honten)

noodles.notes: The shop's namesake: jukusei hosomen, thin noodles aged about four days for a firm, glossy bite with a clean throat-feel. Other bowls (Korai-men, miso tsukemen) use flat or thick noodles, but the flagship shoyu bowl is built on these aged thin straight noodles.

ramen.signature_bowl: Ramen (the standard chuka-soba): a chicken-and-pork shoyu broth simmered around twelve hours with aromatic vegetables, finished with a scattering of pork back-fat (se-abura chatcha), poured over the house aged thin noodles.
ramen.chashu: Thin-sliced braised pork loin chashu; on the chashu-men it is fanned petal-like around the rim in the old Kyoto presentation, and a richer toro-niku (fatty cut) version is offered as a limited topping.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui aged noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is chicken and pork bone simmered with vegetables, the tare is shoyu, and back-fat and chashu are pervasive. No plant-only bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is Kyoto's beloved se-abura shoyu lineage in the round, descended from the old Takaraya/Daio school: a deep chicken-and-pork soy soup blanketed with floating pork back-fat for a sweet, clinging richness over an otherwise clear base. The house twist is the aged thin noodle that gives the bowl a brisk, glossy bite where most Kyoto back-fat shops lean heavier and chewier.

name: 中華そば笑麺 (Chuka Soba Shomen)

noodles.notes: Thin, straight low-hydration noodles built to carry the back-fat-laden aged soy soup; described as a bowl you never tire of even daily.

ramen.signature_bowl: 中華そば (chuka soba) — chicken-bone soy ramen crowned with melted back fat; upgrade to the 笑麺 / chashu-men for extra pork and ajitama.
ramen.chashu: Melt-in-your-mouth braised pork chashu, tender and softly stewed rather than char-grilled.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based aged soy tare; no rice/konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Soup is built on chicken bones with pork back fat and braised pork chashu; no plant-based broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A textbook bowl of Kyoto's beloved se-abura shoyu lineage: a chicken-bone broth darkened with aged soy sauce and blanketed in glistening, collagen-rich pork back fat. The fat reads rich to the eye yet the soup underneath stays clean and moreish — exactly the kind of nostalgic, never-tiring Kyoto noodle the neighborhood has leaned on since 1993.

name: 麺屋 一麺専心 (Menya Ichimen Senshin)

noodles.notes: Medium-thick, high-hydration wavy noodles from the Kyoto maker Teigaku (Teikaku); chewy and slurpable, built to drag up and cling to the thick broth, with throat-feel prioritized over bite.

ramen.signature_bowl: Ultra-rich tonkotsu ramen (超濃厚豚骨らーめん) — pork bone and water only, around 1 kg of bone per bowl; the chicken paitan (鶏白湯) is the lighter, collagen-rich second pillar.
ramen.chashu: Tender braised pork belly, richly fatty so the fat melts juicily in the mouth; the tonkotsu bowl is piled generously, with one thick slice plus several thin slices.
notable_toppings: side plate of warm vegetables (cabbage, bean sprouts, carrot, onion) to add as you like

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a shoyu (wheat) tare; nothing rice- or konjac-noodle based is offered, so this is not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on long-simmered pork bone or chicken broth with pork belly chashu and a shoyu tare; no plant-only broth is offered. The complimentary warm vegetable plate is the only animal-free element.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto bone-broth specialist that does the rare double act of an ultra-thick pork-bone soup — boiled down from roughly a kilo of bone per bowl until it turns gluey and viscous, in the spirit of Kyoto's famously dense kotteri lineage — alongside a smoother, collagen-rich chicken paitan. The pork tonkotsu, not Hakata-thin but heavy and emulsified, is the bowl that defines the shop's soul.

name: 麺屋 鼎（KANAE） (Menya Kanae)

noodles.notes: Thick, wide, curly Jiro-style noodles from the Teian maker, made specifically for jiro-kei — chewy with good elasticity, built to drag up fat and seasoning. Said to be relatively approachable for Jiro beginners.

ramen.signature_bowl: KANAE no Jiro (鼎の二郎) — a Jiro-style bowl piled with yasai (bean sprouts, some cabbage), pork and abura, finished with premium wagyu (Kobe/Omi) beef fat. Choose shio or shoyu and soup or no-soup (mazesoba) at the ticket machine; call your garlic/yasai/abura. Shio is the quieter, fat-forward recommendation.
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut braised pork slices in the standard bowl (two pieces); the shop also leans into beef, with long-simmered gelatinous beef achilles featured as a signature protein in some bowls.

gf.note: Jiro-style wheat-and-kansui noodles plus shoyu/shio tare and a pork-and-beef fat broth — no documented rice or konjac noodle, not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Built on pork tonkotsu with rendered wagyu beef fat, pork chashu and beef achilles — no plant-based broth option documented.

ramen.pantheon: A Kyoto take on the Tokyo-born Jiro lineage — the mountain of bean sprouts, garlic and glistening fat is pure jiro-kei — but the soul here is the swap of ordinary pork back-fat for premium wagyu (Kobe/Omi) beef fat, which lends the rich tonkotsu base a sweeter, more elegant, less leaden finish. You taste that beefy refinement riding over the usual Jiro stomp.

name: 新進亭 一乗寺店 (Shinshintei Ichijoji)

noodles.notes: Thin (chu-hoso) straight noodles with a crisp, slightly chewy bite, cooked to a firm finish; they carry the mellow white-miso soup without overwhelming it.

ramen.signature_bowl: 白味噌ラーメン (Shiro-miso / white miso ramen) — the house origin bowl: a mellow, gently sweet Kyoto white-miso soup lifted by a whisper of chili and Sichuan pepper.
ramen.chashu: Sliced braised pork chashu, served alongside seasoned ground pork that melts into the soup; the bowl's protein heart is as much the minced meat as the sliced chashu.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-fermented miso tare make this bowl unsafe for celiacs; no rice-noodle or gluten-free option is documented.

vegan.note: The standard bowl carries pork chashu and seasoned ground pork; no documented plant-only (kombu/shiitake) broth or animal-free version. A vegan diner would need to ask, with no guarantee the miso base is free of animal dashi.

ramen.pantheon: Often credited as the original white-miso ramen, this Ichijoji standby trades Sapporo's punchy lard-and-garlic miso for Kyoto's softer soul — a mellow, faintly sweet Saikyo-style white miso soup whose richness is rounded rather than aggressive. A flicker of chili and Sichuan pepper keeps the sweetness honest, and the whole bowl tastes unmistakably of Kyoto's gentle palate.

name: 龍鳳 (Ryuho)

noodles.notes: Yellow kansui (alkaline) wheat noodles, served straight-cut. In the signature karashi soba they are tossed with prepared mustard (neri-garashi) before the thick ankake gravy is ladled over.

ramen.signature_bowl: Karashi soba (からしそば) — straight yellow noodles tossed with hot Japanese mustard, blanketed in a gentle, delicate shoyu ankake (thick starchy gravy) loaded with vegetables and seafood-chicken.
ramen.chashu: No braised ramen-style chashu in the signature bowl; the protein is sliced chicken and shrimp folded into the gravy. The shop's fried rice does use diced char siu, but the soba bowl is chicken/seafood-forward.

gf.note: Yellow kansui wheat noodles, a wheat-based shoyu gravy, and a flour-thickened ankake all carry gluten. No rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented; not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The light gravy is built on chicken and seafood (shrimp, likely katsuo/Chinese stock), and bowls carry chicken and shrimp. No documented plant-only broth option.

ramen.pantheon: This is not ramen in the broth-and-tare sense but the soul of 京都中華 — the gentle Cantonese-derived town-Chinese cooking born from the legendary Houmai (鳳舞) lineage. Its karashi soba lays straight yellow noodles under a mild, translucent shoyu ankake of chicken, shrimp and vegetables, then jolts the whole bowl awake with a nose-tingling hit of mustard — delicate Kyoto restraint with one deliberate spike of heat.

name: 濃厚らーめん 驚麺屋 (Noko Ramen Kyomenya)

noodles.notes: Default bowl uses crimped, medium-thick wavy noodles built to grab and carry the gluey broth; firmness is adjustable to order. Kaedama (refill) noodles are thin and straight for a contrasting bite.

ramen.signature_bowl: 驚濃とんこつ (Kyono Tonkotsu) — the ultra-concentrated pork-bone bowl, simmered with roughly three times the bones of an ordinary tonkotsu into a doro-kei (thick, gluey) soup. Tune it to taste with kaedama, extra negi, and your choice of back-fat level.
ramen.chashu: Tender, lightly sweet braised pork — meaty rather than fatty, simmered so it carries a gentle sweetness without greasiness.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a pork-bone broth seasoned with a wheat-based tare; no gluten-free noodle or tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The entire menu is built on a heavy pork-bone-and-seafood (bonito/anchovy) broth with pork chashu and back-fat; no plant-based broth or bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A Kyoto outpost of the doro-kei (mud-thick) tonkotsu school — the bowl chases the ultra-concentrated, throat-coating pork-bone style of Nara's Mutetsupo lineage rather than Kyoto's own sé-abura shoyu tradition. The soul lives in a soup boiled down with triple the bones, layered in recent years with a stronger bonito-and-anchovy dashi so the seafood threads through the pork richness without turning harsh.

name: 家系ラーメン斎藤家 一乗寺店 (Iekei Ramen Saitoya Ichijoji)

noodles.notes: Sakaiseimen-style thick, straight, short-cut noodles — the textbook Iekei choice. Sturdy and chewy, built to stand up to the rich tonkotsu-shoyu broth and carry it upward; firmness is customizable (kcatame on request).

ramen.signature_bowl: Chashu-men (チャーシューメン) — the full Iekei bowl with extra braised pork. Order it with the classic three adjustments: noodle firmness (men no katasa), broth richness (aji no nosa), and oil amount (abura no ryo). A char-grilled pork rice bowl makes the ideal side.
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut braised pork — tender, generously sliced (about four pieces), seasoned to pair beautifully with rice. A premium extra-thick chashu topping is available.
notable_toppings: free condiments: grated ginger, crushed garlic, vinegar, pepper, chili paste

gf.note: The noodles are wheat-and-kansui and the tare is a soy-sauce (wheat) base over a pork-and-chicken bone broth — not celiac-safe. No rice- or konjac-noodle alternative is documented.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is a pork-bone-and-chicken broth finished with chicken oil and soy tare, topped with braised pork — animal-based throughout. No plant-broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A faithful slice of Yokohama Iekei that traces its soul back to the Yoshimuraya lineage — a creamy pork-and-chicken bone broth bound by a sharp soy tare and a glossy slick of chicken oil, the genre's signature trinity. Rare for the Kansai region, it lets Kyoto diners taste the genuine Kanto Iekei pedigree, leaner and cleaner than expected yet deep in body.

name: 本気つけ麺山下 (Honki Tsukemen Yamashita)

noodles.notes: Whole-wheat (zenryu-fun) thick noodles with a notably chewy, mochi-like bite and smooth slip — sturdy enough to carry the heavy dipping broth. Sourced from an unusual specialty maker.

ramen.signature_bowl: 魚介醤油つけ麺 (gyokai-shoyu tsukemen) — thick whole-wheat noodles served cold beside a concentrated, fragrant bonito-and-niboshi dipping broth finished with bold shoyu and a lift of yuzu. (The other half of the menu is a creamy 胡麻豆乳担々つけ麺 — sesame-soymilk tantan with house chili oil and meat miso.)
ramen.chashu: Moderately thick-cut braised pork shoulder loin — moist and tender, with a good balance of fat and lean. Aburi (seared) chashu is available as a paid add-on.

gf.note: Wheat-based thick noodles (with whole-wheat flour) and a wheat-containing shoyu dipping broth make this unsuitable for celiac diners; no rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The signature broth is built on bonito and dried sardine with pork chashu; the second option leans on meat miso. No documented plant-only broth, so neither bowl is vegan.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto tsukemen specialist that narrows its whole focus to two dipping bowls and pours everything into the soup: the flagship pulls deep, almost jammy umami from bonito and dried sardine, lacquered with assertive shoyu and brightened by yuzu so the richness never tips into fishiness. You taste a contemporary gyokai-tsukemen lineage here rather than any single regional dialect — heavy, glossy broth made to cling to fat, chewy whole-wheat noodles to the very last drop.

name: 拉麺 夢 (Ramen Yume)

noodles.notes: House-made (jisei-men), built on crowdfunded noodle-making equipment. Slightly thinner and rounder with gentler waviness than the classic flat, brutally thick Jiro noodle — an intentionally more approachable, eat-easy take on the style.

ramen.signature_bowl: 小豚ラーメン (Ko-buta Ramen) — the 300g 'small' bowl piled with extra braised pork; portions also run petit 200g / regular 400g / large 500g. Free call (mashi) for vegetables, back-fat and garlic at the counter.
ramen.chashu: Thick-cut braised (nikomi) pork that turns meltingly tender and shreds apart in the mouth; two generous slices, cut larger and more rustic than a typical neat chashu.

gf.note: Jiro-style bowl: house-made wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a soy (shoyu) tare and pork broth — wheat throughout. No rice/konjac noodle or GF-tare option documented.

vegan.note: Pork-bone broth, soy tare, pork back-fat and braised pork are the core of every bowl. No plant-broth option documented.

ramen.pantheon: A Jiro-inspired bowl with a softer soul — the pork-bone broth carries real porky umami and a sweet roundness rather than the genre's usual brutal heaviness, its soy edge sharpened by a fine layer of melted back-fat. Run by a former nurse who trained at Jiro-kei shops, it reads the mountain-of-pork-and-sprouts ritual through a gentler, more approachable lens while keeping the lineage unmistakable.

name: しらかわ (Shirakawa)

noodles.notes: Thin noodles with a surprisingly springy, chewy bite — described as somen-like but with added mochi-mochi resilience that stands up well to the back-fat soup.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (中華そば) — the back-fat shoyu Kyoto bowl; upgrade to the chashu-men for a fuller helping of pork belly.
ramen.chashu: Thin-sliced braised pork belly, three slices to a bowl, on the firmer side with a clean, classic soy-braise flavor rather than fatty melt.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-containing soy (shoyu) tare; no rice/konjac noodle or GF-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork and chicken bones, the bowl carries pork back-fat and pork-belly chashu, and the tare is soy-based with animal stock. No plant-broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is Kyoto's home-grown soul food: a back-fat-and-soy chuka soba poured over a pork-and-chicken broth, in the lineage of nearby Masutani that fathered the city's se-abura shoyu style. What sets this bowl apart is the splash of vinegar worked into the soup itself, rounding the fat into something mellow and clean rather than heavy.

name: らぁ麺 櫻井 (Raamen Sakurai)

noodles.notes: Medium-thick wavy noodles with a chewy, mochi-like elasticity, chosen to grip the chicken broths. The shop pairs different noodles to its clear versus paitan bowls.

ramen.signature_bowl: 鶏白湯しょうゆ (chicken paitan shoyu) — the creamy, espuma-foamed chicken paitan finished with soy tare; the shio and a miso paitan are also offered, alongside lighter clear-broth 鶏出汁 shoyu/shio bowls.
ramen.chashu: Two cuts: tender poached domestic chicken breast and braised Kyoto-pork shoulder loin (kata-rosu) that renders a rich, fatty sweetness.
notable_toppings: white negi (scallion, from the shop's own farm)

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus wheat-based shoyu/miso tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free option is documented.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on 100% chicken broth with chicken/pork chashu; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto chicken house that puts the bird front and center: a 100% domestic-chicken, additive-free kitchen offering both a clean six-hour clear dashi and a seven-hour creamy paitan whipped into a cloud-like espuma foam. It is part of the contemporary tori-paitan wave rather than Kyoto's classic se-abura shoyu lineage, and you taste that pure-chicken soul in every silky sip.

name: 中華そば おにぎり ◯△ (Chuka Soba Onigiri Maru-Sankaku)

noodles.notes: Whole-wheat-blend thin to medium-thin straight noodles with a crisp, clean (patsu-patsu) bite and a faintly chewy, mochi-like body; cut to drink in the ginger-forward broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Shoga (ginger) chuka soba — an aged-shoyu chicken clear soup spiked with fresh ginger and floated with melting back fat, the bowl built to be eaten alongside one of the shop's hand-formed onigiri.
ramen.chashu: Two cuts in one bowl: a large, lightly seared rare pork slice that stays tender and pink, plus a deeply braised, fall-apart, more strongly seasoned piece.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based aged-shoyu tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented. The onigiri are plain rice but the ramen itself is not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Soup is chicken-based with pork back-fat and pork/animal chashu, and the shellfish variant adds seafood dashi; no documented plant-only (kombu/shiitake) broth bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A Yamashina take on Kyoto's beloved se-abura shoyu lineage, where pools of melting back-fat ride a clear, aged-soy chicken soup — but here a bright shot of fresh ginger cuts the richness, lending the broth a gingery-savory snap that practically begs for a rice ball. You taste the city's fat-and-soy soul re-tuned for a lighter, more aromatic finish.

name: Oike Café × HACHINOBO（オイケカフェ ハチノボウ） (Oike Cafe x Hachinobo)

noodles.notes: Thick, high-hydration noodles with a springy, snappy bite that hold up against the heavy broth coating; described variously as curly/crinkled and supplier-made (Teigaku). A few accounts call them straight, so shape is reported with mild uncertainty.

ramen.signature_bowl: Cappuccino Ramen — a thick pork (tonkotsu) paitan broth whipped into a fine, airy cappuccino-like foam, finished with shoyu tare, thick high-hydration noodles, two kinds of chashu and diced onion, with a side of spicy takana pickles.
ramen.chashu: Two styles served together: a tender chicken chashu and a juicy braised pork loin (rosu) chashu. The lighter "tanrei" bowl instead features a richer three-layer braised pork belly.
notable_toppings: spicy takana (pickled mustard greens) on the side

gf.note: Standard ramen is wheat noodles with a shoyu (wheat) tare, so the core bowls are not celiac-safe. The menu does list a Laksa made with rice noodles, but its coconut-shrimp broth and seasonings are not verified gluten-free, and cross-contact in a shared kitchen is likely — ask staff before relying on it.

vegan.note: Bowls are built on a rich pork-bone broth with shoyu tare and pork/chicken chashu; no plant-based broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is Kyoto's standard-bearer for "awa-kei" (foam-style) ramen: a concentrated Hakata-leaning pork paitan blitzed into a silky cappuccino froth, so the dense pork umami arrives light and creamy on the tongue rather than heavy. A shoyu tare and a hit of raw onion and spicy takana keep the richness bright, making it a modern, design-forward take on tonkotsu.

name: 麺ショップ この味出来太蔵 (Men Shop Kono Aji Dekitazo)

noodles.notes: Noodles supplied by the renowned Kyoto noodle maker Teikaku (麺屋棣鄂). The standard chuka-soba uses straight medium noodles; the jiro-style variant swaps in thick, crinkled, wari-wari (sturdy, chewy) noodles.

ramen.signature_bowl: 中華そば (Chuka Soba) — the shop's foundational bowl, a chicken-and-pork-bone broth simmered around eight hours with a clean shoyu tare. Add the ajitama for the 味玉中華そば.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork belly, slow-cooked until meltingly tender (toro-toro) and deeply seasoned.
notable_toppings: wonton (on the koku-umami shoyu wonton soba)
bean sprouts and cabbage (on the jiro-style bowl)

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based shoyu tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free seasoning option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on a chicken-and-pork-bone broth with pork chashu and back-fat; no plant-based broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A friendly all-rounder in the Kyoto idiom: its soul is a chicken-and-pork-bone soup coaxed over roughly eight hours into something light yet quietly deep, finished with a crisp shoyu tare and a glint of back-fat in the local se-abura tradition. From that base the kitchen ranges confidently across miso, shio, a seafood chanpon-style bowl, dipping noodles and a gentle, garlic-laced Jiro-style bowl.

name: 更科 聖護院支店 (Sarashina Shogoin-shiten)

noodles.notes: Pale, relatively white straight Chinese noodles that suit the light, gentle broth rather than fight it.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chuka soba (中華そば) — order the (上) jo version for extra chashu.
ramen.chashu: Thin slices of pale, gently boiled (not braised or seared) pork — five to six slices in the jo bowl — soft and mild rather than caramelized.

gf.note: Chinese noodles are wheat-and-kansui and the shoyu tare is wheat-based; on top of that this is a soba/udon house where wheat is everywhere. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The broth leans on a Japanese-style stock (fish dashi heritage) and the bowl is dressed with pork chashu and fish-cake kamaboko. No documented plant-only option.

ramen.pantheon: A relic of old Kyoto machi-chuka: a Meiji-era soba house whose Chinese noodle bowl is built on the gentle, soba-stock-derived wafu soul of the shop, so the soup tastes more like a clear, kind Kyoto dashi-shoyu than any modern ramen. Pale broth, pale noodles, soothing rather than bold — the kind of everyday comfort bowl that has nearly vanished.

name: 大豊ラーメン 木屋町店 (Taiho Ramen Kiyamachi)

noodles.notes: Old-school low-hydration straight noodles (described thin to medium-thin) with a firm bite that stands up to the heavy, soy-soaked broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Chashu-men — the pork-bone soy ramen piled with the shop's black-pork chashu and a heap of shredded kujo negi.
ramen.chashu: Black-pork (kurobuta) chashu, soy-braised and boldly seasoned; served both as thin loin slices and as diced bits mixed through the negi.
notable_toppings: diced chashu folded into the negi

gf.note: Wheat-kansui noodles plus a heavy soy-based tare make this bowl unsuitable for celiac diners; no rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented.

vegan.note: The broth is pork-bone based with a pork chashu and soy tare; no plant-based broth or topping option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A late-night Kyoto institution tucked in a Kiyamachi back-alley, this is the city's beloved 'shime' bowl: an inky, pork-bone soy broth so dark and salt-forward it practically demands you've been drinking. You taste the Kyoto se-abura-shoyu lineage in the rendered pork fat and the aggressive shoyu tare, brightened by the signature crunch of shredded kujo negi.

name: 中華そば専門店 なか小路 (Chuka Soba Senmonten Nakakoji)

noodles.notes: Snappy, crisp-biting straight medium-thin noodles (chuusai) with a powdery, low-hydration 'patsu-patsu' texture, served firm by default. Sourced from Kondo Seimen. The brittle bite is a hallmark of the classic Kyoto chuka-soba style.

ramen.signature_bowl: Buta-soba — the back-fat soy-sauce pork bowl (se-abura chatcha). The chicken version, tori-soba, is the salt-forward sibling.
ramen.chashu: Two slices of pork belly chashu, briefly torch-seared (aburi) to order so the fat caramelizes at the edges; balanced lean-and-fat marbling.
notable_toppings: large chunks of pork back-fat (se-abura) floating on top

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a wheat-based soy tare make this bowl unsafe for celiacs, and no rice or konjac noodle alternative is offered.

vegan.note: The broth is built on pork (and chicken) bones with a soy tare, and bowls are dressed with back-fat and pork belly chashu — no plant-based option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A neighborhood torchbearer of old-school Kyoto chuka-soba: a clear, deeply simmered pork-and-chicken broth pulled tight with dark soy and crowned with big melting chunks of back-fat — the se-abura chatcha lineage running back through Shinpukusaikan and the Ichijoji ramen scene. You taste the city's working-class soul in the snappy thin noodles and that comforting, savory-sweet fat slick that never tips into heaviness.

name: らぁ麺や ふぢとら (Raamenya Fujitora)

noodles.notes: House-made fresh daily using Kyoto Izawa Seifun wheat for a strong toasted-wheat aroma. Slightly flattened (hira-uchi) straight noodles with a clean rectangular cut, smooth tsurutsuru throat-feel and a chewy yet crisp bite. The cold shoyu bowl uses a thicker, linguine-like flat noodle.

ramen.signature_bowl: Tokusei Shoyu Raamen (special soy-sauce chicken ramen) — the flagship; the Shio version showcases the same delicate chicken broth, and the soupless tantanmen is the third pillar to chase.
ramen.chashu: Two low-temperature (sous-vide) cuts served together: a thin rolled pork belly and thicker slices of shoulder loin, cut from Kagoshima kurobuta pork, kept rosy and tender rather than heavily braised.

gf.note: Wheat house-made noodles (kansui) and a soy-sauce tare built on a six-soy blend; the broth also carries dried-fish dashi. No rice/konjac noodle or GF-tare option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on jidori chicken bones, kurobuta pork bones and dried fish, with pork chashu and wonton throughout. No plant-based broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A refined chicken-forward shoyu in the modern delicate (tanrei) lineage carried out of a celebrated Kyoto kitchen: clear amber soup from black jidori chicken and kurobuta bones, lifted by dried fish and a layered six-soy tare, with a whisper of yuzu and chicken fat. The soul is restraint and clarity — you taste the bird and the soy, not grease — while a separately built soupless tantanmen shows the same hand with sesame and spice.

name: 麺処 かわ乃 (Mendokoro Kawano)

noodles.notes: Thin, faintly wavy straight noodles from a Kyoto noodle maker (Menya Teigaku), milled from whole-grain wheat; prized for a crisp, snappy, clean bite (patsu-patsu) that suits the delicate clear duck broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: Kamo soba (鴨そば) — the pure duck bowl: a clear duck-and-water broth finished with mellow shoyu tare and a slick of duck fat, topped with low-temperature duck loin. The special (tokusei) adds extra duck chashu and an ajitama. The duck-and-shellfish chuka soba (鴨と貝の中華そば, shoyu or shio) is the richer alternative.
ramen.chashu: Low-temperature-cooked duck loin (kamo rosu), sliced thin and rosy — tender and moist with a refined, gamey-sweet duck umami rather than pork. The special version piles on extra slices; a braised pork-shoulder chashu also accompanies the tokusei.
notable_toppings: two cuts of negi (sliced rings + fine white shreds)

gf.note: Wheat ramen noodles and a shoyu (soy/wheat) tare make these bowls unsuitable for celiacs; no rice- or konjac-noodle option is documented. The shio version still uses wheat noodles.

vegan.note: Every bowl is built on duck stock (with shellfish and beef bone in the chuka soba), finished with duck fat and topped with duck and pork; no plant-based broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto specialist that builds its soul on duck rather than the usual pork or chicken: the flagship kamo soba is drawn from duck and water alone, a crystal-clear chintan whose gentle, refined sweetness is restrained rather than cloying — you taste the bird, clean and direct. A second line deepens the duck with clam, scallop and beef bone for a fuller, shoyu-or-shio chuka soba, marking it as a refined-tradition (tanrei) duck house rather than any regional gotōchi style.

name: 熟成麺屋 神来 西院本店 (Jukusei Menya Jinrai Saiin Honten)

noodles.notes: Thin straight noodles custom-made by a renowned Kyoto noodle producer; fragrant with wheat aroma and a springy, firm bite (コシ) despite their slender gauge, built to be slurped fast and to sit lightly in the soy backfat broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: 特製炙りチャーシューメン (Tokusei Aburi Chashu-men) — the standard chicken-and-pork-bone soy backfat ramen loaded with extra torched chashu, Kujo negi and house menma; the plain ラーメン is the everyday entry point.
ramen.chashu: House-made pork chashu: blocks slow-simmered with aromatic vegetables and steeped in the shop's own soy marinade; the premium bowl serves it aburi (lightly torched/seared) so the edges caramelize and the fat softens over the hot broth.

gf.note: Wheat ramen noodles plus a soy-sauce (shoyu) tare make this unsafe for celiac diners; no rice- or konjac-noodle or GF-tare option is documented.

vegan.note: Broth is built on chicken and pork bones with pork backfat, a soy tare, and pork chashu; no plant-based (kombu/shiitake/veg) bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A textbook Kyoto se-abura shoyu bowl: a chicken-and-pork-bone soy broth crowned with a float of fragrant pork backfat, yet it drinks far lighter than it looks — clean and almost Kansai-udon-dashi in spirit rather than heavy. The backfat lends koku and gloss while the soy tare keeps the soul of the soup squarely in Kyoto's old-school back-fat lineage, with thin springy noodles and sweet Kujo negi sealing the local accent.

name: 麺家 しょうりん (Menya Shourin)

noodles.notes: Straight, medium-thin gauge from the venerable Kyoto noodle maker Teiigaku (棣鄂); wheaty and supple with good throat-feel, boiled slightly firm to stand up to the thick emulsified broth.

ramen.signature_bowl: 肉白湯そば (Niku Paitan Soba) — a thick, emulsified chicken-and-pork-bone white broth finished with a special chicken paste you stir in to deepen the richness, topped with a pepper accent.
ramen.chashu: Braised pork loin — thick-cut, tender and moist, drinking up the broth's essence; rich, chew-rewarding meat rather than fatty belly or aburi char.
notable_toppings: special whipped chicken paste (stirred into the broth for extra koku)

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles plus a shoyu (wheat) tare; no rice-noodle or gluten-free option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The soul of the bowl is an animal paitan from chicken and pork bones, dressed with pork chashu and a chicken paste; no plant-broth option is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Kyoto take on tori-paitan: instead of the city's old-guard se-abura shoyu, this bowl pours a glossy, lip-coating white broth simmered thick from chicken and pork bones, its richness dialed up tableside by stirring in a savory chicken paste. The lineage you taste is contemporary kotteri paitan, smoothed and refined rather than gamey.

name: 麺や 賀ら茂ん (Menya Garamon)

noodles.notes: Thin-to-medium straight noodles with a clean wheat aroma and a satisfying bite; their slim profile lets the soy broth cling well. The lighter chuka soba and the back-fat shoyu share the same noodle.

ramen.signature_bowl: Seabura Shoyu (背脂醤油) — the rich version of the house soy bowl, ladled with pork back-fat (and a touch of chili oil) over the same pork-chicken-kombu-seafood stock; order the aburi chashu as a 1-slice add-on. A lighter Chuka Soba (same broth, fat skimmed and finished with scallion oil) and a Seabura Miso round out the menu.
ramen.chashu: Large, tender sheets of pork with a creamy, sweet fat layer, finished by blow-torching the surface (aburi) for a fragrant, lightly charred top while staying soft and almost crumbly inside.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a wheat-based soy (shoyu) tare made with regional soy sauce; no rice/konjac noodle or GF-tare option is documented. Not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: Broth is built on pork bone, chicken carcass and seafood/kombu dashi, the tare is animal-laced shoyu, and the bowls carry pork back-fat and torched pork chashu. No plant-broth bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: A neighborhood bowl in the Kyoto se-abura shoyu lineage, where a clear pork-chicken-and-seafood broth is seasoned with a regional Kyotango soy sauce that gives a deep, faintly bitter savor lifted by a whisper of ginger. The rich version crowns that clear soup with melting beads of pork back-fat — soul of the city's old-school se-abura shoyu, where fat rides on clarity rather than clouding it.
